devil abuse of man or woman be call of old and succubus according to the difference of the sex that they converse with by two mean this great kind of abuse may possible be perform the one when the devil only as a spirit and steal out the sperm of a dead body abuse they that way they not graith see any shape or feel any thing but that which he so convey in that part as we read of a monastery of nun which be burn for their be that way abuse the other mean be when he borrow a dead body and so visible and as it seem unto they natural as a man converse with they but it be to be note that in whatsoever way he use it that sperm seem intolerable cold to the person abuse for if he steal out the nature of a quick person it can be so quick carry but it will both tine the strength and heat by the way which it can never have have for lack of agitation which in the time of procreation be the procurer and wakener up of these two natural quality and if he occupy the dead body as his lodging expel the same out thereof in the due time it must likewise be cold by the participation with the quality of the dead body whereout of it come and whereas you inquire if these spirit be divide in sex or not I think the rule of philosophy may easy resolve a man of the contrary for it be a sure principle of that art that nothing can be divide in sex except such live body as must have a natural seed to by but we know spirit have no seed proper to themselves nor yet can they gender one with a other phi. how be it then that they say sundry monster have be get by that way Epi these tale be nothing but for that they have no nature of their own I have show you already and that the cold nature of a dead body can work nothing in generation it be more nor plain as be already dead of itself as well as the rest of the body be want the natural heat and such other natural operation as be necessary for work that effect and in case such a thing be possible which be utter against all the rule of nature it will breed no monster but only such a natural offspring as will have come betwixt that man or woman and that other abuse person in case they both be alive have have a do with other for the devil part therein be but the naked carry or expel of that substance and so it can participate with no quality of the same indeed it be possible to the craft of the devil to make a woman belly to and special three omit the diverse vain sign that the papist attribute unto it such as the rage at holy water their flee aback from the cross their not abide the hear of God name and innumerable such his majesty instruction to his dear son henry the prince the argument sonnet GOD give not king the style of god in vain for on his throne his sceptre do they sway and as their subject ought they to obey so king shall fear and serve their god again if then you will enjoy a happy reign observe the statute of your heavenly king and from his law make all your law to spring since his lieutenant here you shall remain reward the just be steadfast true and plain repress the proud maintain aye the right walk always so as ever in his sight who guard the godly plague the profane and so you shall in princely virtue shine resemble right your mighty king divine to HENRY my dear son and natural SUCCESSOUR whom-to can so right appertain this book of instruction to a prince in all the point of his call aswell general as a christian towards God as particular as a king towards his people whom-to I say can it so just appertain as unto you my dear son since i the author thereof as your natural father must be careful for your godly and virtuous education as my elder son and the first fruit of God blessing towards i in my posterity and as a king must timous provide for your train up in all the point of a king office since you be my natural and lawful successor therein that be right inform hereby of the weight of your burden you may in time begin to consider that be bear to be a king you be rather bear to then not excel all your people so far in rank and honour as in daily care and hazardous painstaking for the dutiful administration of that great office that God have lay upon your shoulder lay so a just symmetry and proportion betwixt the height of your honourable place and the heavy weight of your great charge and consequent in case of fail which God forbid of the sadness of your fall according to the proportion of that height i have therefore for the great ease to your memory and that you may at the first cast up any part that you have to do with divide this treatise in three part the first teach you your duty towards God as a christian the next your duty in your office as a king and the three inform you how to behave yourself in indifferent thing which of themselves be neither right nor wrong but according as they be right or wrong use and yet will serve according to your behaviour therein to augment or impair your fame and authority at the hand of your people receive and welcome this book then as a faithful praeceptour and counsellor unto you which because my affair will not permit i ever to be present with you i ordain to be a resident faithful admonisher of you and because the hour of death be uncertain to i as unto all flesh i leave it as my testament and latter will unto you charge you in the presence of GOD and by the fatherly authority i have over you that you keep it ever with you as careful as Alexander do the iliad of Homer you will find it a just and impartial counsellor neither flatter you in any vice nor importune you at unmeet time it will not come un-called neither speak unspeered at and yet confer with it when you be at quiet you shall say with Scipio that you be to conclude then i charge you as ever you think to deserve my fatherly blessing to follow and put in practice as far as lie in you the precept hereafter follow and if you follow the contrary course I take the great GOD to record that this book shall one day be a witness betwixt i and you and shall procure to be ratify in heaven the curse that in that case here I give unto you for I protest before that great GOD I have rather not be a father and childless then be a father of wicked child but hope yea even promise unto myself that GOD who in his great blessing send you unto i shall in the same blessing as he have give i a son so make he a good and a godly son not repent he of his mercy show unto i I end with my earnest prayer to GOD to work effectual into you the fruit of that blessing which here from my heart I bestow upon you your love father i. r. to the reader charitable reader it be one of the golden sentence which Christ our saviour utter to his apostle that there be nothing so cover luk. 12. that shall not be reveal neither so hide that shall not be know and whatsoever they have speak in darkness shall be hear in the light and that which they have speak in the ear in secret place shall be public preach on the top of the house and since he have say it most true must it be since the author thereof be the fountain and very be of truth which shall move all godly and honest man to be very wary in all their secret action and whatsoever middess they use for attain to their most wish end lest otherwise how avowable soever the mark be whereat they aim the middess be discover to be shameful whereby they climb it may turn to the disgrace both of the good work itself and of the author thereof since the deep of our secret can be hide from that all-seeing eye and light pierce through the bowel of very darkness itself but as this be general true in the action of all man so be it more special true in the affair of king for king be public person by reason of their office and authority be as it be set as it be say of old upon a public stage in the sight of all the people where all the beholder eye be attentive bend to look and pry in the least circumstance of their secret drift which shall make king the more careful not to harbour the secret thought in their mind but such as in the own time they shall not be ashamed open to avouch assure themselves that time the mother of verity will in the due season bring she own daughter to perfection the true practice hereof I have as a king oft find in my own person though I thank God never to my shame have lay my count ever to walk as in the eye of the almighty examine ever so the secret of my drift before I give they course as how they may some day bide the touchstone of a public trial and among there of my secret action which have unlooked for of i come to public knowledge it have so fare with my (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) direct to my elder son which I write for exercise of my own engine and instruction of he who be appoint by God I hope to sit on my throne after i for the purpose and matter thereof be only fit for a king as teach he his office and the person whomfor it be ordain a king heir who secret counsellor and faithful admonisher it must be I think it no way convenient nor comely that either it shall to all be proclaim which to one only appertain and special be a messenger betwixt two so coniunct person or yet that the mould whereupon he shall frame his future behaviour when he come both unto the perfection of his year and possession of his inheritance shall before the hand be make common to the people the subject of his future happy government and therefore for the more secret and close keep of they I only permit seven of they to be print the printer be first swear for secrecy and these seven I disperse among some of my trusty servant to be keep close by they lest in case by the iniquity or wear of time any of they may have be lose yet some of they may have remain after i as witness to my son both of the honest integrity of my heart and of my fatherly affection and natural care towards he but since contrary to my intention and expectation as I have already say this book be now vent and set forth to the public view of the world and consequent subject to every man censure as the current of his affection lead he I be now force as well for resist to the malice of the child of envy who like wasp suck venom out of every wholesome herb as for the satisfaction of the godly honest sort in any thing that they may mistake therein both to publish and spread the true copy thereof for deface of the false copy that be already spread as I be inform as likewise by this preface to clear such part thereof as in respect of the concise shortness of my style may be misinterpret therein to come then particular to the matter of my book there be two special great point which as I be inform the malicious sort of man have detract therein and some of the honest sort have seem a little to mistake whereof the first and great be that some sentence therein shall seem to furnish ground to man to doubt of my sincerity in that religion which I have ever constant profess the other be that in some part thereof I shall seem to nourish in my mind a vindictive resolution against England or at the least some principal there for the queen my mother quarrel the first calumny most grievous indeed be ground upon the sharp and bitter word that therein be use in the description of the humour of puritan and rash-headie preacher that think it their honour to contend with king and perturb whole kingdom the other point be only ground upon the strait charge I give my son not to hear nor suffer any unreverent speech or book against any of his parent or progenitor wherein I do allege my own experience anent the queen my mother affirm that I never find any that be of perfect age the time of her reign here so steadfast true to i in all my trouble as these that constant keep their allegiance to she in her time but if the charitable reader will advise consider both the method and matter of my treatise he will easy judge what wrong I have sustain by the carp at both for my book suppose very small be divide in three several part the first part thereof only treat of a king duty towards God in religion wherein I have so clear make profession of my religion call it the religion wherein I be bring up and ever make profession of and wish he ever to continue in the same as the only true form of God worship that I will have think my sincere plainness in that first part upon that subject shall have dit the mouth of the most envious Momus that ever hell do hatch from bark at any other part of my book upon that ground except they will allege i to be contrary to myself which in so small a volume will smell of too great weakness and sliprinesse of memory and the second part of my book teach my son how to use his office in the administration of justice and politic government the three only contain a king outward behaviour in indifferent thing what agreeance and conformity he ought to keep betwixt his outward behaviour in these thing and the virtuous quality of his mind and how they shall serve for trunsh-man to interpret the inward disposition of the mind to the eye of they that can see far within he and therefore must only judge of he by the outward appearance so as if there be no more to be look into but the very method and order of the book it will sufficient clear i of that first and grievous imputation in the point of religion since in the first part where religion be only treat of I speak so plain and what in other part I speak of puritan it be only of their moral fault in that part where I speak of policy declare when they contemn the law and sovereign authority what punishment they deserve for the same and now as to the matter itself whereupon this scandal be take that I may sufficient satisfy all honest man and by a just apology raise up a brazen wall or bulwark against all the dart of the envious I will the more narrow rip up the word whereat they seem to be somewhat stomach first then as to the name of puritan I be not ignorant that the style thereof do proper belong only to that vile sect among the anabaptist call the family of love because they think themselves only pure and in a manner without sin the only true church and only worthy to be participant of the sacrament and all the rest of the world to be but abomination in the sight of God of this special sect I principal mean when I speak of puritan diverse of they as Browne penry and other have at sundry time come into Scotland to sow their popple among we and from my heart I wish that they have leave no scholar behind they who by their fruit will in the own time be manifest and partly indeed I give this style to such brainsick and heady preacher their disciple and follower as refuse to be call of that sect yet participate too much with their humour in maintain the above mention error not only agree with the general rule of all anabaptist in the contempt of the civil magistrate and in lean to their own dream and revelation but particular with this sect in account all man profane that swear not to all their fantasy in make for every particular question of the policy of the church as great commotion as if the article of the trinity be call in controversy in make the scripture to be rule by their conscience and not their conscience by the scripture and he that deny the least jot of their ground not worthy to enjoy the benefit of breathe much less to participate with they of the sacrament and before that any of their ground be impugn let king people law and all be tread under foot such holy war be to be prefer to a ungodly peace no in such case christian prince be not only to be resist unto but not to be pray for for prayer must come of faith and it be reveal to their conscience that GOD will hear no prayer for such a prince judge then christian reader if I wrong this sort of people in give they the style of that sect who error they imitate and since they be content to wear their livery let they not be ashamed to borrow also their name it be only of this kind of man that in this book I write so sharp and who I wish my son to punish in-case they refuse to obey the law and will not cease to stir up a rebellion who against I have write the more bitter in respect of diverse famous libel and injurious speech spread by some of they not only dishonourable invective against all christian prince but even reproachful to our profession and religion in respect they be come out under colour thereof and yet be never answer but by papist who general meddle aswell against they as the religion itself whereby the skandale be rather double then take away but on the other part I protest upon my honour I mean it not general of all preacher or other that like better of the single form of policy in our church then of the many ceremony in the church of England that be persuade that their bishop smell of a papal supremacy that the surplise the corner cap and such like be the outward badge of popish error no I be so far from be contentious in these thing which for my own part I ever esteem as indifferent as I do equal love and honour the learned and grave man of either of these opinion it can no way become i to pronounce so light a sentence in so old a controversy we all God be praise do agree in the ground and the bitterness of man upon such question do but trouble the peace of the church and give advantage and entry to the papist by our division but towards they I only use this provision that where the law be otherways they may content themselves sober and quiet with their own opinion not resist to the authority nor break the law of the country neither above all stir any rebellion or schism but possess their soul in peace let they press by patience and well ground reason either to persuade all the rest to like of their judgement or where they see better ground on the other part not to be ashamed peaceable to incline thereunto lay aside all praeoccupied opinion and that this be the only meaning of my book and not any coldness or crack in religion that place do plain witness where after I have speak of the fault in our ecclesiastical estate I exhort my son to be beneficial unto the goodman of the ministry praise God there that there be present a sufficient number of good man of they in this kingdom and yet be they all know to be against the form of the english church yea so far I be in that place from admit corruption in religion as I wish he in promoove they to use such caution as may preserve their estate from creep to corruption ever use that form through the whole book where ever I speak of bad preacher term they some of the minister and not minister or ministry in general and to conclude this point of religion what indifferency of religion can Momus call that in i where speak of my son marriage in case it please God before that time to cut the thread of my life I plain forewarn he of the inconuenient that be like to ensue incase he shall marry any that be of a different profession in religion from he notwithstanding that the number of prince profess our religion be so small as it be hard to foresee how he can be that way meet match according to his rank and as for the other point that by some part in this book it shall appear that I do nourish in my mind a vindictive resolution against England or some principal there it be sure more than wonderful unto i upon what ground they can have gather such conclusion for as upon the one part ineither by name nor description point out England in that part of my discourse so upon the other I plain bewray my meaning to be of scottishman where I conclude that purpose in these term that the love I bear to my son have move i to be so plain in this argument for so that I discharge my conscience to he in utter the verity I care not what any traitor or treason-allow do think of it and englishman can not thereby be mean since they can be no traitor where they ought no allegiance I be not ignorant of a wise and princely apophthegm which the same queen of England utter about the time of her own coronation but the drift of that discourse do full clear my intention be only ground upon that precept to my son that he shall not permit any unreverent detract of his praedecessour bring in that purpose of my mother only for a example of my experience anent scottishman without use any persuasion to he of revenge for a king give of any fault the due style infer no reduction of the falter pardon no I be by a degree near of kin unto my mother than he be neither think I myself either that unworthy or that near my end that I need to make such a davidicall testament since I have ever think it the duty of a worthy prince rather with a pike than a pen to write his just revenge but in this matter I have no delight to be large wish all man to judge of my future project according to my bypass action thus have as much insist in the clear of these two point as will I hope give sufficient satisfaction to all honest man and leave the envious to the food of their own venom I will hearty pray thou love reader charitable to conceive of my honest intention in this book I know the great part of the people of this whole isle have be very curious for a sight thereof some for the love they bear i either be particular acquaint with i or by a good report that perhaps they have hear of i and therefore long to see any thing that proceed from that author who they so love and honour since book be vive idee of the author mind some only for mere curiosity that think it their honour to know all new thing be curious to glut their eye therewith only that they may vaunt they to have see it and some fraught with causeless envy at the author do greedy search out the book think their stomach jit enough for turn never so wholesome food into noisome and infective bumour so as this their great concurrence in curiofitie though proceed from far different complexion have enforce the untimous divulgate of this book far contrary to my intention as I have already say to which Hydra of diversly-enclined spectator I have no targe to oppone but plainness patience and sincerity plainness for resolve and satisfy of the first sort patience for to bear with the shallowness of the next and sincerity to defy the malice of the three withal though I can please all man therein I be content so that jone please the virtuous sort and though they also find not every thing therein so full to answer their expectation as the argument will seem to require although I will wish they modest to remember that God have not bestow all his gift upon one but part they by a justice distributive and that many eye see more than one and that the variety of man mind be such that yea and that even the very face that God have by nature bring forth in the world do every one in some of their particular lineament differ from any other yet in truth it be not my intention in handle of this purpose as it be easy to perceive full to set down here all such ground as may out of the best writer have be allege and out of my own invention and experience add for the perfect institution of a king but only to give some such precept to my own son for the government of this kingdom as be meet for he to be instruct in and best become i to be the informer of if I in this book have be too particular plain impute it to the necessity of the subject not so much be ordain for the institution of a prince in general as I have say as contain particular precept to my son in special whereof he can have make but a general use if they have not contain the particular disease of this kingdom with the best remedy for the same which it become i best as a king have learn both the theoric and practic thereof more plain to express than any simple school man that only know matter of kingdom by contemplation but if in some place it seem too obscure impute it to the shortness thereof be both for the respect of myself and of my son constrain there-unto my own respect for fault of leisure be so continual occupy in the affair of my office as my great burden and restless fashery be more than know to all that know or bear of i for my son respect because I know by myself that a prince so long as he be young will be so carry away with some sort of delight or other that he can patient abide the read of any large volume and when he come to a full maturity of age he must be so busy in the active part of his charge as he will not be permit to bestow many hour upon the contemplative part thereof so as it be neither fit for he nor possible for i to have make this treatise any more ample than it be indeed I be little beholden to the curiosity of some who think it too large already as appear for lack of leisure to copy it draw some note out of it for speed sake put in the one half of the purpose and leave out the other not unlike the man that allege that part of the psalm but leave out the precede word and of these note make a little pamphlet lack both my method and half of my matter entitle it forsooth the king testament as if I have eik a three testament of my own to the two that be in the holy scripture it be true that in a place thereof for affirmation of the purpose I be speak of to my son I bring myself in there as speak upon my testament for in that sense every record in write of a man opinion in anything in respect that paper outlive their author be as it be a testament of that man will in that case and in that sense it be that in that place I call this treatise a testament but from any particular sentence in a book to give the book itself a title be as ridiculous as to style the book of the psalm the book of because with these word one of they do begin well leave these new baptizer and blocker of other man book to their own folly ireturne to my purpose anent the shortness of this book suspect that all my excuse for the shortness thereof shall not satisfy some especial in our neighbour country who think that as I have so narrow in this treatise touch all the principal sickness in our kingdom with overture for the remedy thereof as I say before so look they to have find something therein that shall have touch the sickness of their state in the like sort but they will easy excuse i thereof if they will consider the form I have use in this treatise wherein I only teach my son out of my own experience what form of government be fit for this kingdom and in one part thereof speak of the border I plain there do excuse myself that I will speak nothing of the state of England as a matter wherein I never have experience I know indeed no kingdom lack she own disease and likewise what interest I have in the prosperity of that state for although I will be silent my blood and descent do sufficient proclaim it but notwithstanding since there be a lawful queen there present reign who have so long with so great wisdom and felicity govern her kingdom as I must in true sincerity confess the like have not be read nor hear of either in our time or since the day of the roman emperor Augustus it can no way become i far inferior to she in knowledge and experience to be a busybody in other prince matter and to fish in other folk water as the proverb be no I hope by the contrary with God grace ever to keep that christian rule to do as I will be do to and I doubt nothing yea even in her name I dare promise by the bypast experience of her happy government as I have already say that no good subject shall be more careful to inform she of any corruption steal in in her state than she shall be zealous for the discharge of her conscience and honour to see the same purge and restore to the ancient integrity and further during her time become i least of any to meddle in and thus have resolve all the doubt so far as I can imagine may be move against this treatise it only rest to pray thou charitable reader to interpret favourable this birth of i according to the integrity of the author and not look for perfection in the work itself as for my part I only glory thereof in this point that I trust no sort of virtue be condemn nor any degree of vice allow in it and that though it be not perhaps so gorgeous deck and rich attire as it ought to be it be at the least right proportion in all the member without any monstrous deformity in any of they and special that since it be first write in secret and be now publish not of ambition but of a kind of necessity it must be take of all man for the true image of my very mind and form of the rule which I have prescribe to myself and i which as in all my action I have bitherto press to express so far as the nature of my charge and the condition of time will permit i so bear it a discovery of that which may be look for at my hand and whereto even in my secret thought I have engage myself for the time to come and thus in a firm trust that it shall please God who with my be and crown give i this mind to maintain and augment the same in i and my posterity to the discharge of our conscience the maintenance of our honour and weal of our people I bid thou hearty farewell of a king christian dvetie to ward god. the first book as he can be think worthy to rule and command other that can rule and dantone his own proper affection and unreasonable appetite so can he not be think worthy to govern a christian people the true ground of good government know and fear God that in his own person and heart fear not and love not the divine majesty neither can any thing in his government succeed well with he devise and labour as he listen as come from a filthy spring if his person be unsanctified for as that royal prophet say except the lord build the house psal 127 1. they labour in vain that build it except the lord keep the city the keeper watch it in vain in respect the blessing of God have only power to give the success thereunto and as Paul say he plant 1. cor. 3.6 Apollo water but it be God only that give the increase therefore my son first of all thing learn to know and love that God whom-to you have a double obligation double bond of a prince to God first for that he make you a man and next for that he make you a little GOD to sit on his throne and rule over other man remember that as in dignity he have erect you above other so ought you in thankfulness towards he go as far beyond all other a moat in another eye be a beam into you a blemish in another be a leprous boil into you and a venial sin as the papist call it in another be a great crime into you think not therefore that the highness of your dignity the greatness of the fault of a prince dimmish your fault much less give you a licence to sin but by the contrary your fault shall be aggravate according to the height of your dignity any sin that you commit not be a single sin procure but the fall of one but be a sin and therefore draw with it the whole multitude to be guilty of the same remember then that this glister worldly glory of king the true glory of king be give they by God to teach they to press so to glister and shine before their people in all work of sanctification and righteousness that their person as bright lamp of godliness and virtue may go in and out before their people give light to all their step remember also that by the right knowledge and fear of God which be the begin of wisdom Prou 9.10 as Solomon say you shall know all the thing necessary for the discharge of your duty both as a christian and as a king see in he as in a mirror the course of all earthly thing whereof he be the spring and only mover now the only way to bring you to this knowledge the mean to know God be diligent to read his word and earnest to pray for the right understanding thereof search the scripture say Christ for they bear testimony of i and John 5.39 the whole scripture say Paul be give by inspiration of God and be profitable to teach 2. tim. 3.16.17 to convince to correct and to instruct in righteousness that the man of God may be absolute be make perfect unto all good work and most proper of any other belong the read thereof unto king deut. 17. since in that part of scripture where the godly king be first make mention of that be ordain to rule over the people of God there be a express and most notable exhortation and commandment give they to read and meditate in the law of God I join to this the careful hear of the doctrine with attendance and reverence for faith come by hear rom. 10.17 say the same apostle but above all beware you wrest not the word to your own appetite as over many do make it like a bell to sound as you please to interpret but by the contrary frame all your affection to follow precise the rule there set down the whole scripture chief contain two thing a command wherein chief the whole scripture consist and a prohibition to do such thing and to abstain from the contrary obey in both neither think it enough to abstain from evil and do no good nor think not that if you do many good thing it may serve you for a cloak to mix evil turn therewith and as in these two point the whole scripture principal consist two degree of the service of God so in two degree stand the whole service of God by man interior or upward exterior or downward the first by prayer in faith towards God the next by work flow before the world which be nothing else but the exercise of religion towards God and of equity towards your neighbour as for the particular point of religion I need not to dilate they I be no hypocrite follow my footstep a regardable pattern and your own present education therein I thank God I be never ashamed to give account of my profession howsoever the malicious lie tongue of some have traduce i and if my conscience have not resolve i that all my religion present profess by i and my kingdom be ground upon the plain word of the scripture without the which all point of religion be superfluous as any thing contrary to the same be abomination I have never outward avow it for pleasure or awe of any flesh and as for the point of equity towards your neighbour because that will fall in proper upon the second part concern a king office I leave it to the own room for the first part then of man service to his god religion which be religion that be the worship of God according to his reveal will it be whole ground upon the scripture as I have already say quicken by faith and conserve by conscience for the scripture I have now speak of it in general but that you may the more ready make choice of any part thereof for your instruction or comfort remember short this method the whole scripture be dyte by God spirit the method of scripture thereby as by his lively word to instruct and rule the whole church militant to the end of the word it be compose of two part the old and new testament the ground of the former be the law which show our sin and contain justice the ground of the other be Christ who pardon sin contain grace the sum of the law be the ten commandment more large delate in the book of Moses of the Law interpret and apply by the prophet and by the history be the example show of obedience or disobedience thereto and what or be according give by God but because no man be able to keep the law nor any part thereof it please God of his infinite wisdom and goodness to incarnate his only son in our nature for satisfaction of his justice in his suffering for we that since we can not be save by do we may at least be save by believe the ground therefore of the word of grace of grace be contain in the four history of the birth life death resurrection and ascension of Christ the large interpretation and use thereof be contain in the epistle of the apostle and the practice in the faithful or unfaithful with the history of the infancy and first progress of the church be contain in their act will you then know your sin by the law use of the Law read the book of Moses contain it will you have a commentary thereupon read the prophet and likewise the book of the proverbe and Ecclesiastes write by that great pattern of wisdom Solomon which will not only serve you for instruction how to walk in the obedience of the law of God but be also so full of golden sentence and moral precept in all thing that can concern your conversation in the world as among all the profane philosopher and poet you shall not find so rich a storehouse of precept of natural wisdom agree with the will and divine wisdom of God will you see how good man be reward and wicked punish look the historical part of these same book of Moses together with the history of joshua the judge ezra Nehemiah Esther and job but especial the book of the king and chronicle wherewith you ought to be familiar acquaint for there shall you see yourself as in a mirror in the catalogue either of the good or the evil king will you know the doctrine life and death of our saviour Christ use of the gospel read the evangelist will you be more particular train up in his school meditate upon the epistle of the apostle and will you be acquaint with the practice of that doctrine in the person of the primitive church cast up the apostle act and as to the apocryphe book I omit they because I be no Papist as I say before and indeed some of they be no way like the dytement of the spirit of God but when you read the scripture how to read the scripture read it with a sanctify and chaste heart admire reverent such obscure place as you understand not blame only your own capacity read with delight the plain place and study careful to understand those that be somewhat difficile press to be a good textuarie for the scripture be ever the best interpreter of itself but press not curious to seek out far than be contain therein for that be over unmannerly a presumption to strive to be further upon God secret than he have will you be for what he think needful for we to know that have he reveal there and delight most in read such part of the scripture as may best serve for your instruction in your call reject foolish curiosity upon genealogy and contention tit. 3.9 which be but vain and profit not as Paul say now as to faith which be the nourisher and quickener of religion i'faith the nourisher of religion as I have already say it be a sure persuasion and apprehension of the promise of God apply they to your soul and therefore may it just be call the golden chain that link the faithful soul to Christ and because it grow not in our garden but be the free gift of God philip. 1.29 as the same apostle say it must be nourish by prayer which be nothing else but a friendly talk with God as for teach you the form of your prayer prayer and whence to learn the best form thereof the psalm of David be the meet schoolmaster that you can be acquaint with next the prayer of our saviour which be the only rule of prayer whereout of as of most rich and pure fountain you may learn all form of prayer necessary for your comfort at all occasion and so much the fit be they for you then for the common sort in respect the composer thereof be a king and therefore best behove to know a king want and what thing be meet to be require by a king at God hand for remedy thereof use often to pray when you be quiet several exercise of prayer especial forget it not in your bed how oft soever you do it at other time for public prayer serve as much for example as for any particular comfort to the supplicant in your prayer be neither over strange with God what rule or regard to be use in prayer like the ignorant common sort that pray nothing but out of book nor yet over homely with he like some of the vain pharisaical that think they rule he upon their finger the former way will breed a uncouth coldness in you towards he the other will breed in you a contempt of he but in your prayer to God speak with all reverence for if a subject will not speak but reverent to a king much less shall any flesh presume to talk with God as with his companion crave in your prayer not only thing spiritual but also thing temporal what to crave of God sometime of great and sometime of less consequence that you may lay up in store his grant of these thing for confirmation of your faith and to be a arles-peny unto you of his love pray as you find your heart move you but see that you suit no unlawful thing as revenge rom. 14.23 lust or such like for that prayer can not come of faith and whatsoever be do without faith be sin as the apostle say when you obtain your prayer how to interpret the issue of prayer thank he joyful therefore if otherways bear patient press to win he with importunity as the widow do the unrighteous judge and if notwithstanding thereof you be not hear assure yourself God forsee that which you ask be not for your weal and learn in time so to interpret all the adversity that God shall send unto you so shall you in the midst of they not only be arm with patience but joyful lift up your eye from the present trouble to the happy end that God will turn it to and when you find it once so fall out by proof arm yourself with the experience thereof against the next trouble assure yourself though you can in time of the shower see through the cloud yet in the end shall you find God send it for your weal as you find in the former and as for conscience conscience the conserver of religion which I call the conserver of religion it be nothing else but the light of knowledge that God have plant in man which ever watch over all his action as it bear he a joyful testimony when he do right so chop it he with a feel that he have do wrong when ever he commit any sin and sure although this conscience be a great torture to the wicked yet be it as great a comfort to the godly if we will consider it right for have we not a great advantage that have within ourselves while we live here a count-book and inventary of all the crime that we shall be accuse of the inventary of our life either at the hour of our death or at the great day of judgement which when we please yea though we forget will chop and remember we to look upon it that while we have leisure and be here we may remember to amend and so at the day of our trial compear with new and white garment wash in the blood of the lamb 7.14 as s. John say above all then my son labour to keep sound this conscience which many prattle of but over few feel especial be careful to keep it free from two disease wherewith it use oft to be infect the disease of conscience to wit leprosy and superstition the former be the mother of atheism the other of heresy by a leaprouse conscience I mean a cauterize conscience 1. tim. 4.2 as Paul call it be become senseless of sin through sleep in a careless security as king David be after his murder and adultery ever till he be waken by the prophet nathan similitude and by superstition I mean when one restrain himself to any other rule in the service of God then be warrant by the word the only true square of God serucie as for a preservative against this leprosy preservative against leprosy of conscience remember ever once in the four and twenty hour either in the night or when you be at great quiet to call yourself to account of all your last day action either wherein you have commit thing you shall not or omit the thing you shall do either in your christian or kingly call and in that account let not yourself be smooth over with that flatter (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) which be overkind a sickness to all mankind but censure yourself as sharp as if you be your own enemy for if you judge yourself you shall not be judge 1. cor. 11.31 as the apostle say and then according to your censure reform your action as far as you may eschew ever wilful and witting to contrary your conscience for a small sin wilful commit with a deliberate resolution to break the bridle of conscience therein be far more grievous before God than a great sin commit in a sudden passion when conscience be asleep last account remember therefore in all your action of the great account that you be one day to make in all the day of your life ever learn to die and live every day as it be your last horat. lib. 1. epist and therefore I will not have you to pray with the papist to be preserve from sudden death but that God will give you grace so to live as you may every hour of your life be ready for death so shall you attain to the virtue of true fortitude never be afraid for the horror of death true fortitude come when he listen and especial beware to offend your conscience with use of swear or lie suppose but in jest for other be but a use foolish use of oath and a sin clothe with no delight nor gain and therefore the more inexcusable even in the sight of man and lie come also much of a vile use which banish shame therefore beware even to deny the truth which be a sort of lie that may best be eschew by a person of your rank for if any thing be ask at you that you think not meet to reveal if you say that question be not pertinent for they to ask who dare examine you further and use sometime this answer both in true and false thing that shall be ask at you such unmannerly people will never be the wise thereof and for keep your conscience sound from that sickness of superstition against superstition you must neither lay the safety of your conscience upon the credit of your own conceit nor yet of other man humour how great doctor of divinity that ever they be but you must only ground it upon the express scripture for conscience not ground upon sure knowledge be either a ignorant fantasy or a arrogant vanity beware therefore in this case with two extremity the one to believe with the papist the church authority better than your own knowledge the other to lean with the anabaptist to your own conceit and dream revelation but learn wise to discern betwixt point of salvation and indifferent thing difference of internal and external thing betwixt substance and ceremony and betwixt the express commandment and will of God in his word and the invention or ordinance of man since all that be necessary for salvation be contain in the scripture for in any thing that be express command or prohibit in the book of God you can be over precise even in the least thing count every sin not according to the light estimation and common use of it in the world but as the book of God count of it but as for all other thing not contain in the scripture spare not to use or alter they as the necessity of the time shall require account of thing external and when any of the spiritual office-bearer in the church speak unto you any thing that be well warrant by the word reverence and obey they as the herald of the most high God but if pass that bound they urge you to embrace any of their fantasy in the place of God word or will colour their particular with a pretend zeal acknowledge they for no other than vain man exceed the bound of their call and according to your office grave and with authority redact they in order again to conclude then conclusion both this purpose of conscience and the first part of this book keep God more spare in your mouth but abundant in your heart be precise in effect but social in show kythe more by your deed then by your word the love of virtue and hatred of vice and delight more to be godly and virtuous indeed then to be think and call so expect more for your praise and reward in heaven than here and apply to all your outward action Christ command to pray and give your alm secret so shall you on the one part be inward garnish with true christian humility not outward with the proud Pharisie glory in your godliness but say as Christ command we all when we have do all that we can Luke 10.17 and on the other part you shall eschew outward before the world the suspicion of filthy proud hypocrisy and deceitful dissimulation of a king dvetie in his office the second book but as you be clothe with two calling so must you be alike careful for the discharge of they both that as you be a good christian so you may be a good king discharge your office as I show before in the point of justice and equity the office of a King which in two sundry way you must do the one in establish and execute Plato in Polit. which be the life of the law good law among your people Isocr in sym. the other by your behaviour in your own person and with your servant to teach your people by your example for people be natural incline to counterfeit like ape their prince manner plate in polis according to the notable say of Plato express by the poet Claudian in 4. cons. hon. for the part of make and execute of law consider first the true difference betwixt a lawful good king and a usurp tyrant and you shall the more easy understand your duty herein difference of a king and a tyrant for the one acknowledge himself ordain for his people have receive from God a burden of government Plato in Polit. whereof he must be countable the other think his people ordain for he arist 5. Polit. a prey to his passion and inordinate appetite as the fruit of his magnanimity and therefore as their end be direct contrary so be their whole action as mean whereby they press to attain to their end a good king think his high honour to consist in the due discharge of his call emploi all his study and pain to procure and maintain Xen. 8. cyr. by the make and execution of good law the welfare and peace of his people and as their natural father and kindly master cic. lib. 5. rep. think his great contentment stand in their prosperity and his great surety in have their heart subject his own private affection and appetite to the weal and stand of his subject ever think the common interest his chief particular where by the contrary a usurp tyrant think his great honour and felicity to consist in attain arist 5. Trist and many respect may lawful let a admission that will not be sufficient cause of deprivation all your servant and court must be compose partly of minor of the choice of servant such as young lord to be bring up in your company or page and such like and partly of man of perfect age for serve you in such room as aught to be fill with man of wisdom and discretion for the first sort you can do no more but choose they within age arist 1 and 5. polit that be come of a good and virtuous kind as baptism be use for though traduce but be immediate create by God cic. and infuse from above yet it be most certain that virtue or vice will oftentimes with the heritage be transfer from the parent to the posterity witness the experience of the late house of Gowree Plat. 6. Leg. arist 2. and 1. pol. and run on a blood as the proverb be the sickness of the mind become as kind to some race as these sickness of the body that infect in the seed especial choose such minor as be come of a true and honest race and have not have the house whereof they be descend infect with falsehood and as for the other sort of your company and servant that aught to be of perfect age Plat. 6. the leg Isocr in pan arist 5. pol. first see that they be of a good fame and without blemish otherwise what can the people think but that you have choose a company unto you according to your own humour and so have prefer these man for the love of their vice and crime that you know they to beguiltie of dem. 2. ph. for the people that see you not within can judge of you but according to the outward appearance of your action and company which only be subject to their sight Plat. 7. rep. 3. 12. reward they prefer their posterity before other as kindly so shall you not only be best serve for if the hater of your parent can love you as I show before it follow of necessity their lover must love you but further you shall kith your thankful memory of your father and procure the blessing of these old servant in not miss their old master in you which otherwise will be turn in a prayer for i and a curse for you use they therefore when God shall call i as the testimony of your affection towards i trust and advance those far who I find faithful which you must not discern by their reward at my hand for reward as they be call so be they subject unto fortune but according to the trust I give they have ofttimes have better heart than hap to the reward of sundry and on the other part as I wish you to kith your constant love towards they that I love so desire I you to kith in the same measure your constant hatred to they that I hate I mean bring not home nor restore not such as you find stand banish or fore-faulted by i the contrary will kith in you over great a contempt of i and lightness in your own nature for how can they be true to the son that be false to the father but to return to the purpose anent the choice of your servant you shall by this wise form of do eschew the inconuenient that in my minority I fall in anent the choice of my servant for by they that have the command where I be bring up be my servant put unto i not choose they that be meet to serve i but who they think meet to serve their turn about i a domestic and near example as kithe well in many of they at the first rebellion raise against i which compel i to make a great alteration among my servant and yet the example of that corruption make i to be long trouble thereafter with solicitor recommend servant unto i more for serve in effect their friend that put they in than their master that admit they let my example then teach you to follow the rule here set down arist 2. pol. choose your servant for your own use and not for the use of other and since you must be to all your people so choose your servant indifferent out of all quarter not respect other man appetite but their own Isoc in sym. tacit. 3. hist. curt. 8. for since in the first part of this treatise I forewarn you to be at war with your own inward flatterer (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) how much more shall you be at war with outward flatterer who be nothing so sib to you as yourself be by the sell of such counterfeit ware only press to ground their greatness upon your ruin and therefore be careful to prefer none as you will be answerable to God but only for their worthiness of public receiver but special choose honest diligent mean but responsal man to be your receiver in money matter mean I say that you may when you please take a sharp account of their intromission without peril of their breed any trouble to your estate for this oversight have be the great cause of my mis-thriving in money matter especial put never a foreigner a special principle in policy arist 5. pol. cic. q. in any principal office of estate for that will never fail to stir up sedition and envy in the countrymen heart both against you and he but as I say before if God provide you with more country then this choose the borne-man of every country to be your chief counsellor therein and for conclusion of my advice anent the choice of your servant Plat. in 1. al. in pol. and 5. the legib. Arist. 2. delight to be serve with man of the noble blood that may be have for beside that their service shall breed you great goodwill and least envy contrary to that of start-ups you shall oft find virtue follow noble race as I have say before speak of the nobility now as to the other point government of the Court Isocr in Arcop anent your govern of your servant when you have choose they make your court and company to be a pattern of godliness and all honest virtue to all the rest of the people in Panath be a daily watchman over your servant that they obey your law precise for how can your law be keep in the country if they be break at your ear punish the breach thereof in a courtier more severe then in the person of any other of your subject and above all suffer none of they by abuse their credit with you to oppress or wrong any of your subject be homely or strange with they arist 2. pol. as you think their behaviour deserve tacit. 1. hist. and their nature may bear with think a quarrelous man a pest in your company val. lib. 2. curt. 4. be careful ever to prefer the gentile nature and trusty to the inward office about you especial in your chalmer suffer none about you to meddle in any man particular Demost. 8. phil. sal. in cat. lin. 22. but like the turk janisares let they know no father but you nor particular but you and if any will meddle in their kin or friend quarrel give they their leave for since you must be of no surname nor kin but equal to all honest man it become you not to be follow with partial or factious servant tacit. eod. and 1 an. teach obedience to your servant and not to think themselves overwise and as when any of they deserve it you must not spare to put they away so without a see cause change none of they the groundstone of good government at. 5. polit. tacit. in ag. Dion li. 52. Xent in age Isoc in sym. ph. id. cic. q. pay they as all other your subject with or as they deserve which be the very groundstone of good government employ every man as you think he qualify but use not one in all thing lest he wax proud and be envy of his fellow love they best that be plain with you and disguise not the truth for all their kin suffer none to be evil tongue nor backbiter of they they hate command a hearty and brotherly love among all they that serve you and short maintain peace in your court banish envy cherish modesty banish debauch insolence foster humility and repress pride set down such a comely and honourable order in all the point of your service that when stranger shall visit your court 1. King 10. they may with the queen of sheba admire your wisdom in the glory of your house and comely order among your servant but the principal blessing that you can get of good company of marriage will stand in your marry of a godly and virtuous wife for she must be near unto you gen. 2.23 than any other company be flesh of your flesh and bone of your bone as Adam say of Hevah and because I know not but God may call i before you be ready for marriage I will short set down to you here my advice therein first of all consider that marriage be the great earthly felicity or misery that can come to a man according as it please God to bless or curse the same since then without the blessing of GOD you can look for a happy success in marriage you must be careful both in your preparation for it preparation to marriage and in the choice and usage of your wife to procure the same by your preparation I mean that you must keep your body clean and unpolluted till you give it to your wife whom-to only it belong for how can you just crave to be join with a pure virgin if your body be pollute why shall the one half be clean and the other defile and although I know fornication be think but a light and a venial sin by the most part of the world yet remember well what I say to you in my first book anent conscience and count every sin and breach of God law not according as the vain world esteem of it but as God the judge and maker of the law account of the same hear God command by the mouth of Paul to abstain from fornication 1. cor. 6.10 declare that the fornicator shall not inherit the kingdom of heaven and by the mouth of John reckon out fornication among other grievous sin that debar the committer among dog and swine Reuel. 22.25 from entry in that spiritual and heavenly jerusalem and consider if a man shall once take upon he to count that light which God call heavy and venial that which God call grievous the dangerous effect of lust begin first to measure any one sin by the rule of his lust and appetite and not of his conscience what shall let he to do so with the next that his affection shall stir he to the like reason serve for all and so to go forward till he place his whole corrupt affection in God room and then what shall come of he but as a man give over to his own filthy affection shall perish into they and because we be all of that nature that sibbe example touch we near consider the difference of success that God grant in the marriage of the king my grandfather and i your own father a domestic example the reward of his incontinency proceed from his evil education be the sudden death at one time of two pleasant young prince and a daughter only bear to succeed to he who he have never the hap so much as once to see or bless before his death leave a double curse behind he to the land both a woman of sex and a new bear babe of age to reign over they and as for the blessing God have bestow on i in grant i both a great continency and the fruit follow there-upon yourself and sib folk to you be praise be to God sufficient witness which I hope the same god of his infinite mercy shall continue and increase without repentance to i and my posterity be not ashamed then to keep clean your body which be the temple of the holy spirit 1. cor. 6.19 notwithstanding all vain allurement to the contrary discern true and wise of every virtue and vice according to the true quality thereof and not according to the vain conceit of man as for your choice in marriage respect chief the three cause wherefore marriage be first ordain by God and then accessary cause of marriage ro. 2. the three accessory which as I have say ought also to be respect without derogate to the principal cause be beauty riches and friendship by alliance which be all blessing of God for beauty increase your love to your wife content you the better with she without care for other and riches and great alliance do both make she the able to be a helper unto you but if over great respect be have to these accessory the principal cause be overseen which be over oft practise in the world as of themselves they be a blessing be well use so the abuse of they will turn they in a curse for what can all these worldly respect avail when a man shall find himself couple with a devil to be one flesh with he and the half marrow in his bed then though too late shall he find that beauty without bounty wealth without wisdom and great friendship without grace and honesty be but fair show and the deceitful mask of infinite misery but have you respect my son to these three special cause in your marriage matth. 13. a special caution in marriage which flow from the first institution thereof and and therefore I will rath have you to Marie one that be full of your own religion her rank and other quality be agreeable to your estate for although that to my great regret the number of any prince of power and account profess our religion be but very small and that therefore this advice seem to be the more straight and difficile yet you have deep to weigh and consider upon these doubt how you and your wife can be of one flesh and keep unity betwixt you be member of two opposite church disagreement in religion bring ever with it disagreement in manner and the dissension betwixt your preacher and she will breed and foster a dissension among your subject take their example from your family beside the peril of the evil education of your child neither pride you that you will be able to frame and make she as you please that deceive Solomon the wise king that ever be the grace of perseverance not be a flower that grow in our garden remember also that marriage be one of the great action that a man do in all his time especial in take of his first wife and if he marry first base beneath his rank he will ever be the less account of thereafter for keep the blood pure pla. 5. rep. cic. 2. din. arist gen. an. lucr. 4. and last remember to choose your wife as I good kind how much more careful shall he be for the breed of his own loin so shall you in your marriage have respect to your conscience honour and natural weal in your successor when you be marry keep inviolable your promise make to God in your marriage which stand all in do of one thing and abstain from another to treat she in all thing as your wife and the half of yourself and to make your body I trust I need not to insist here to dissuade you from the filthy vice of adultery remember only what solemn promise you make to God at your marriage and since it be only by the force of that promise that your child succeed to you which otherways they can not do equity and reason will you shall keep your part thereof God be ever a severe avenger of all perjury cic. 2. the leg and it be no oath make in jest that give power to child to succeed to great kingdom have the king my grandfather example before your eye who by his adultery breed the wrack of his lawful daughter and heir in beget that bastard who unnatural rebel and procure the ruin of his own Soverane and sister and what good her posterity have get of some of that unlawful generation bothuell his treacherous attempt can bear witness keep praecise then your promise make at marriage as you will wish to be partaker of the blessing therein and for your behaviour to your wife the scripture can best give you counsel therein treat she as your own flesh command she as her lord cherish she as your helper rule she as your pupil and please she in all thing reasonable arist. 8. and epist 7. cic. q. and or which be your proper office therefore beside your education it be necessary you delight in read and seek the knowledge of all lawful thing but with these two restriction first that you choose idle hour for it not interrupt therewith the discharge of your office and next that you study not for knowledge naked but that your principal end be id. 1. to make you able thereby to use your office practise according to your knowledge in all the point of your call id. 1. offic. not like these vain astrologian that study night and day on the course of the star only that they may for satisfy their curiosity know their course but since all art and science be link every one with other their great principle agree in one which move the poet to feign the nine muse to be all sister study they that out of their harmony you may suck the knowledge of all faculty and consequent be on the counsel of all craft that you may be able to contain they all in order as I have already say for knowledge and learning be a light burden the weight whereof will never press your shoulder first of all then study to be well see in the scripture the scripture deut. 17. as I remember you in the first book as well for the knowledge of your own salvation as that you may be able to contain your church in their call as for the rule they well be no small point of your office take special heed that they vague not from their text in the pulpit and if ever you will have peace in your land suffer they not to meddle in that place with the estate or policy but punish severe the turn with time to a natural habitude in you and as by their hear of your law so by their sight of your person both their eye and their ear may lead and allure they to the love of virtue and hatred of vice of a king behaviour in indifferent thing the three book it be a true old say c ph. 8.3 leg ovid. liu. quin. 4. decl that a king be as one set on a stage who small action and gesture all the people gazing do behold and therefore although a king be never so praecise in the discharge of his office the people who see but the outward part will ever judge of the substance by the circumstance and according to the outward appearance if his behaviour be light or dissolute will conceive praeoccupied conceit of the king inward intention which although with time the trier of all truth it will evanish by the evidence of the contrary effect yet arist 5. pol. in different action and their dependency Plato in phil. and 9 the leg and praeiudge conceit will in the mean time breed contempt the mother of rebellion and disorder and beside that it be certain that all the indifferent action and behaviour of a man have a certain hold and dependence either upon virtue or vice according as they be use or rule for there be not a mid betwixt they no more then betwixt their reward heaven and hell be careful then my son so to frame all your indifferent action and outward behaviour as they may serve for the furtherance and forthsetting of your inward virtuous disposition the whole indifferent action of a man two sort of they I divide in two sort in his behaviour in thing necessary as food sleep raiment speak write and gesture and in thing not necessary though convenient and lawful as pastime or exercise and use of company for recreation as to the indifferent thing necessary first sort and how they be indifferent although that of themselves they can be want and so in that case be not indifferent as likewise incase they be not use with moderation decline so to the extremity which be vice yet the quality and form of use they may smell of virtue or vice and be great furtherer to any of they to begin then at the thing necessary one of the publick indifferent action of a king and that many especial stranger will narrow take heed to be his manner of refection at his table and his behaviour thereat therefore as king use oft to eat public form at the table Xen. in cyr. it be meet and honourable that you also do so as well to eschew the opinion that you love not to haunt company which be one of the mark of a tyrant as likewise that your delight to eat private be not think to be for private satisfy of your gluttony which you will be ashamed shall be public see let your table be honourable serve but serve your appetite with few dish as young Cyrus do which both be holesom Xen. 1. cyr. and free from the vice of delicacy which be a degree of gluttony plut. in apoth and use most to eat of reasonablie-groffe and common-meate aswell for make your body strong and durable for travel at all occasion either in peace or in war as that you may be the heartly receive by your mean subject in their house when their cheer may suffice you which otherways will be impute to you for pride and daintiness and breed coldness and disdain in they let all your food be simple sen. ep. 96. without composition or sauce which be more like medecine then meat the use of they be count among the ancient roman a filthy vice of delicacy because they serve only for please of the taste and not for satisfy of the necessity of nature sen. consol. alb. Iwen. sat. 2. abhor Apicius their own citizen for his vice of delicacy and monstrous gluttony like as both the grecian and roman have in detestation the very name of Philoxenus arist 4 eth. for his filthy wish of a crane-craig and therefore be that sentence use among they against these artificial false appetite Xen. dict. and fact Socr. Laert. in Socr. cic. 5. Plat. 6. Leg. plin. l. 14. but beware with use excess of meat and drink and chief beware of drunkenness which be a beastly vice namely in a king but special beware with it because it be one of those vice that increase with age in the form of your meate-eating be neither uncivil like a gross cynic nor affectatlie mignarde like a dainty dame but eat in a manly round and honest fashion cic. 1. off. it be no way comely to dispatch affair or to be pensive at meat but keep then a open and cheerful countenance cause to read pleasant history unto you that profit may be mix with pleasure and when you be not dispose entertain pleasant quick but honest discourse and because meat provoke sleep be also moderate in your sleep of sleep pla. 7. the leg for it go much by use and remember that if your whole life be divide in four part three of they will be find to be consume on meat drink sleep and unnecessary occupation but albeit ordinary time will common be keep in meat and sleep yet use yourself sometime so best form of diet pla. 6. the leg that any time in the four and twenty hour may be alike to you for any of they that thereby your diet may be accommodate to your affair and not your affair to your diet not therefore use yourself to over great-softnesse and delicacy in your sleep more than in your meat and special in-case you have ado with the war let not your chalmer be throng and common in the time of your rest form in the chalmer aswell for comeliness as for eschew of carry report out of the same let they that have the credit to serve in your chalmer val. 2. cur. 4. be trusty and secret for a king will have need to use secrecy in many thing but yet behave yourself so in your great secret as you need not be ashamed suppose they be all proclaim at the market cross pla. 6. the leg but special see that those of your chalmer be of a sound fame and without blemish take no heed to any of your dream dream not to be take heed to for all prophecy vision and prophetic dream be accomplish and cease in Christ and therefore take no heed to freet either in dream or any other thing for that error proceed of ignorance and be unworthy of a christian who shall be assure rom. 14. titus 1. as Paul say all day and meat be alike to christian next follow to speak of raiment of apparel the on-putting whereof be the ordinary action that follow next to sleep Isocr reg. be also moderate in your raiment neither over superfluous like a debauch waster nor yet over base like a miserable wretch not artificial trim and deck like a courtizane nor yet over sluggish clothe like a country clown not over light like a Candie soldier or a vain young courtier nor yet over grave cic. 1. offic. like a minister but in your garment be proper cleanly comely and honest wear your clothes in a careless yet comely form keep in they a mid form betwixt the gravity of the one and lightness of the other thereby to signify that by your call you be mix of both the profession Plat. as a judge make and pronounce the law by the power of the sword as your office be likewise mix betwixt the ecclesiastical and civil estate for a king be not as both the papist and anabaptist will have he to the which error also the puritan incline over far but to return to the purpose of garment they ought to be use according to their first institution by God which be for three cause first to hide our nakedness and shame next and consequent to make we more comely and three to preserve we from the injury of heat and cold if to hide our nakedness and shameful part than these natural part ordain to be hide shall not be represent by any undecent form in the clothes and if they shall help our comeliness they shall not then by their paint preen fashion serve for bait to filthy lechery as false hair and fairding do among unchaste woman and if they shall preserve we from the injury of heat and cold man shall not like senseless stone contemn God in lightly the season glory to conquer honour on heat and cold and although it be praiseworthy and necessary in a prince to be when he shall have ado with war upon the field yet I think it meet that you go both clothe and arm then naked to the battle except you will make you light for away-running and yet for coward and short in your clothes keep a proportion aswell with the season of the year as of your age in the fashion of they be careless use they according to the common form of the time sometime rich cic. 1. off. sometime meanly clothe as occasion serve without keep any precise rule therein for if your mind be find occupy upon they ar. alex. it will be think idle otherways and you shall be account in the number of one of these which will make your spirit and judgement to be less thought of but special eschew to be effeminate in your clothes in perfume preen or such like and fail never in time of war to be galliarde and brave both in clothes and countenance and make not a fool of yourself in disguise or wear long hair or nail which be but excrement of nature and bewray such misuser of they to be either of a vindictive or a vain light natural especial make no vow in such vain and outward thing as concern either meat or clothes let yourself and all your court wear no ordinary armour with your clothes but such as be knightly and honourable what ordinary armour to be wear at Court I mean rapiersworde and dagger for tuilyesome weapon in the court betoken confusion in the country and therefore banish not only from your court all traitorous offensive weapon forbid by the law as gun and such like whereof I speak already but also all traitorous defensive arm as secret plate-sleeve and such like unseen armour for beside that the wearer thereof may be presuppose to have a secret evil intention they want both the use that defensive armour be ordain for which be to be able to hold out violence and by their outward glaunse in their enemy eye to strike a terror in their heart where by the contrary they can serve for neither be not only unable to resist but dangerous for shot and give no outward show against the enemy be only ordain for betray under trust whereof honest man shall be ashamed to bear the outward badge not resemble the thing they be not and for answer against these argument I know none but the old scot fashion which if it be wrong be no more to be allow for ancientness than the old mass be which also our forefather use the next thing that you have to take heed to of language and gesture arist 3. theod. cic. in q. and add bren. be your speak and language whereunto I join your gesture since action be one of the chief quality that be require in a orator for as the tongue speak to the ear so do the gesture speak to the eye of the auditor in both your speak and your gesture use a natural and plain form cic. 1. offic. not faird with artifice for as the frenchman say contrefaict fin but eschew all affectate form in both in your language be plain honest natural comely clean short id. cod and sententious eschew both the extremity aswell in not use any rustical corrupt lay as booke-language and pen and inkhorn term and lest of all mignard and effeminate term but let the great part of your eloquence consist in a natural clear and sensible form of the delivery of your mind cic. q. and add brut. build ever upon certain and good ground temper it with gravity quickness or merriness according to the subject and occasion of the time not taunt in theology nor allege and profane the scripture in drink purpose as over many do use also the like form in your gesture 1. off. neither look silly like a stupid pedant nor unsetle with a uncouth morgue like a new-comeov cavalier but let your behaviour be natural grave and according to the fashion of the country phil. alex. cic. 2. off. be not oversparing in your courtesy for that will be impute to incivility and arrogancy nor yet over prodigal in iowk or nod at every step for that form of be popular become better aspire Absalon arist 4. eth. cic. at. then lawful king frame ever your gesture according to your present action look grave and with a majesty when you sit in judgement or give audience to ambassador homely when you be in private with your own servant merry when you be at any pastime or merry discourse and let your countenance smell of courage and magnanimity when you be at the war and remember I say over again to be plain and sensible in your language Isoc reg. and in for beside that it be the tongue office to be the messenger of the mind it may be think a point of imbecility of spirit in a king to speak obscure much more untrue cic. 3. off. as if he stand in awe of any in utter his thought remember also id. 1. off. form in reason to put a difference betwixt your form of language in reason and your pronounce of sentence or declaratour of your will in judgement or any other way in the point of your office for in the former case you must reason pleasant and patient not like a king but like a private man and a scholar otherways your impatience of contradiction will be interpret to be for lack of reason on your part where in the point of your office you shall ripe advise indeed before you give forth your sentence but from it be give forth the suffering of any contradiction diminish the majesty of your authority in judgement Isoc nic. cic. q. and make the processes endless the like form will also be observe by all your inferior judge and magistrate now as to your writing of writing and what style fit a prince which be nothing else but a form of en-registrate speech use a plain short but stately style both in your proclamation and missive especial to foreign prince and if your engine spur you to write any work either in verse or in prose I can but allow you to
