practice it but take no longsome work in hand for distract you from your call flatter not yourself in your labour cic. 1. off. but before they be set forth let they first be privy censure by some of the best skill man in that craft that in these work you meddle with and because your write will remain as true picture of your mind to all posterity let they be free of all uncomeliness and unhonestie and according to Horace his counsel I mean both your verse and your prose let first that fury and heat wherewith they be write cool at leisure and then as a uncouth judge and censor revise they over again before they be publish if you will write worthy choose subject worthy of you that be not full of vanity but of virtue eschew obscurity and delight ever to be plain and sensible and if you write in verse remember that it be not the principal part of a poem to rhyme right and flow well with many pretty word but the chief commendation of a poem be that when the verse shall be shake sundry in prose ar. art. poet. it shall be find so rich in quick invention and poetic flower and in fair and pertinent comparison as it shall retain the lustre of a poem although in prose and I will also advise you to write in your own language for there be nothing leave to be say in greek and latin already and ynew of poor scholar will match you in these language and beside that it best become a king to purify and make famous his own tongue wherein he may go before all his subject as it set he well to do in all honest and lawful thing and among all unnecessary thing that be lawful and expedient of the exercise of the body Xen. 1. cyr. I think exercise of the body most commendable to be use by a young prince in such honest game or pastime as may further ability and maintain health for albeit I grant it to be most requisite for a king to exercise his engine which sure with idleness will rust and become blunt yet certain bodily exercise and game be very commendable Plat. 6. the leg ar. 7. and 8. pol. cic. 1. off. as well for bannish of idleness the mother of all vice as for make his body able and durable for travel which be very necessary for a King but from this count I debar all rough and violent exercise as the football meet for lame then make able the user thereof as likewise such tumble trick as only serve for comedian and balladines to win their bread with pl. eod. but the exercise that I will have you to use although but moderate not make a craft of they be run leap wrestle fence dance and play at the caitch or tennis archery pall maille and such like other fair and pleasant field-game Xen. in cyr. be deiug and the honourable and most commendable game that you can use be on horseback for it become a prince best of any man to be a fair and good horseman use therefore to ride and danton great and courageous horse that I may say of you as Philip say of great Alexander his son (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) plut. in alex. and special use such game on horseback as may teach you to handle your arm thereon such as the tilt the ring and low-riding for handle of your sword I can omit here the hunt namely with run hound of hunt which be the most honourable and noble sort thereof for it be a thievish form of hunt to shoot with gun and bow and grey hound hunt be not so martial a game but because I will not be think a partial praiser of this sport in cyn. 1. cyr. and rep. lac. cic. 1. offic. I remit you to Xenophon a old and famous writer who have no mind of flatter you or i in this purpose and who also set down a fair pattern cyropoedia for the education of a young king under the suppose name of Cyrus as for hawk I condemn it not but I must praise it more spare of hawk because it neither resemble the war so near as hunt do in make a man hardy and skilful ride in all ground and be more uncertain and subject to mischance and which be worst of all be therethrough a extreme stirrer up of passion but in use either of these game arist 10. eth. observe that moderation that you slip not therewith the hour appoint for your affair which you ought ever precise to keep remember that these game be but ordain for you in enable you for your office for the which you be ordain and as for sit house-pastime of house-game wherewith man by drive time spur a free and fast enough run horse as the proverb be although they be not profitable for the exercise either of mind or body arist 8. pol. yet can I not utter condemn they since they may at time supply the room which be empty will be patent to pernicious idleness dan. de lus al. I will not therefore agree with the curiosity of some learned man in our age in forbid card die and other such like game of hazard although otherways sure I reverence they as notable and godly man for they be deceive therein in sound their argument upon a mistake ground which be that the play at such game be a kind of cast of lot and therefore unlawful wherein they deceive themselves for the cast of lot be use for trial of the truth in any obscure thing that otherways can not be get clear and therefore be a sort of prophecy whereby the contrary no man go to any of these play to clear any obscure truth but only to gage so much of his own money as he please upon the hazard of the run of the card or die aswell as he will do upon the speed of a horse or a dog or any such like gaigeour and so if they be unlawful all gaigeour upon uncertainty must likeways be condemn not that thereby I take the defence of vain carder and dicer that waste their and their time whereof few consider the preciousness upon prodigal and continual play cic. 1. offic. no I will rather allow it to be discharge where such corruption can be eschew but only I can condemn you at some time when you have no other thing ado as a good king will be seldom and be weary of read or evil dispose in your person and when it be foul and stormy weather then I say may you lawful play at the card or table for as to dice I think it become best debauch soldier to play at on the head of their drum be only rule by hazard and subject to knavish cog and as for the chess I think it over fond because it be overwise and philosophic a folly for where all such light play be ordain to free man head for a time from the fashion thought on their affair it by the contrary fill and trouble man head with as many fashious toy of the play as before it be fill with thought on his affair but in your play I will have you to keep three rule first rule in play or you play consider you do it only for your recreation and resolve to hazard the loss of all that you play and next for that cause play no more than you care to cast among page and last play always fair play precise that you come not in use of trick and lie in jest otherwise if you can keep these rule my counsel be that you allutter abstain from these play for neither a mad passion for loss nor falsehood use for desire of gain can be call a play now it be not only lawful but necessary what choice of company that you have company meet for every thing you take on hand aswell in your game and exercise as in your grave and earnest affair Isoc reg. cic. 1. off. but learn to distinguish time according to the occasion choose your company according confer not with hunter at your counsel nor in your counsel affair nor dispatch not affair at hunt or other game and have the like respect to the season of your age use your sort of recreation and company therefore agree thereunto for it become best as kindly every age to smell of their own quality ar. 2. theod. insolence and unlawful thing be always eschew and not that a colt shall draw the plough and a old horse run away with the harrow but take heed special that your company for recreation be choose of honest person not defame or vicious mix filthy talk with merriness more be and chief abstain from haunt before your marriage men. the idle company of dame which be nothing else but be war likewaies to abuse yourself in make your sporter your counsellor and delight not to keep ordinary in your company comedian or balladine for the tyrant delight most in they pl. 3. the rep. ar. 7. and 8. pol. sen. 1. ep. Dyon glory to be both author and actor of comedy and tragedy themselves whereupon the answer that the poet Philoxenus disdainful give to the tyrant of Syracuse there-anent be now come in a proverb suidas and all the ruse that Nero make of himself when he die be Suet. in ner. mean of his skill in menstral and play of tragoedy as indeed his whole life and death be all but one tragedy delight not also to be in your own person a player upon instrument especial on such as common man win their live with nor yet to be fine of any mechanic craft 1. sep. say Du Bartas who work as they be all most worthy to be read by any prince or other good christian so will I especial wish you to be well verse in they but spare not sometime by merry company to be free from importunity for you shall be ever move with reason which be the only quality whereby man differ from beast and not with importunity curt. 8. for the which cause as also for augment your majesty you shall not be so facile of accesse-giving at all time as I have be liu. 35. Xen. in age cit. and yet not altogether retire or lock up like the king of Persia appoint also certain hour for public audience and since my trust be that God have ordain you for more kingdom than this as I have oft already say press by the outward behaviour as well of your own person a special good rule in government as of your court in all indifferent thing to allure piece and piece the rest of your kingdom to follow the fashion of that kingdom of you that you find most civil easy to be rule and most obedient to the law for these outward and indifferent thing will serve great for allurement to the people to embrace and follow virtue but beware of thraw or constrain they thereto let it be bring on with time and at leisure special by so mix through alliance and daily conversation the inhabitant of every kingdom with other as may with time make they to grow and wield all in one which may easy be do betwixt these two nation be both but one isle of Britain and already join in unity of religion and language the fruitful effect of the union so that even as in the time of our ancestor the long war and many bloody battle betwixt these two country breed a natural and hereditary hatred in every of they against the other the unite and weld of they hereafter in one by all sort of friendship commerce and alliance will by the contrary produce and maintain a natural and inseparable unity of love among they already kthe in the happy amity as we have already praise be to God a great experience of the good beginning hereof and of the quench of the old hate in the heart of both the people procure by the mean of this long and happy amity between the queen my dear sister and i which during the whole time of both our reign have ever be inviolable observe and for conclusion of this my whole treatise conclusion in form of abridge of the whole treatise remember my son by your true and constant depend upon God to look for a blessing to all your action in your office by the outward use thereof to testify the inward uprightness of your heart and by your behaviour in all indifferent thing to set forth the vive image of your virtuous disposition and in respect of the greatness and weight of your burden to be patient in hear keep your heart free from praeoccupation ripe in conclude Thuc. 6. Dion 52. and constant in your resolution for better it be to bide at your resolution although there be some defect in it then by daily change to effectuate nothing take the pattern thereof from the microcosm of your own body wherein you have two eye signify great foresight and providence with a narrow look in all thing and also two ear signify patient hear and that of both the party but you have but one tongue for pronounce a plain sensible and uniform sentence and but one head and one heart for keep a constant and uniform resolution according to your apprehension have two hand and two foot with many finger and toe for quick execution in employ all instrument meet for effectuate your deliberation but forget not to digest ever your passion before you determine upon any thing since hir. lib. 1. epist. utter only your anger according to the apostle rule take pleasure not only to reward ephes 4. but to advance the good which be a chief point of a king glory but make none overgreat arist 5. pol. Dion. 52. but according as the power of the country may bear and punish the evil but every man according to his own offence not punish nor blame the father for the son Plat. 9 the leg nor the brother for the brother much less general to hate a whole race for the fault of one for and above all let the measure of your love to every one be according to the measure of his virtue let your favour to be no long tie to any than the continuance of his virtuous disposition shall deserve not admit the excuse upon a just revenge to procure oversight to a injury for the first injury be commit against the party but the party revenge thereof at his own hand be a wrong commit against you in usurp your office who to only the sword belong for revenge of all the injury commit against any of your people thus hope in the goodness of God that your natural inclination shall have a happy sympathy with these pr@cept make the wiseman schoolmaster which be the example of other to be your teacher according to that old verse eschew so the over-late repentance by your own experience which be the schoolmaster of fool I will for end of all require you my son as ever you think to deserve my fatherly blessing to keep continual before the eye of your mind the greatness of your charge Plat. in pol. cic. 5. d^ re^ make the faithful and due discharge thereof the principal butt you shoot at in all your action count it ever the principal and all your other action but as accessory to be employ as middess for the further of that principal and be content to let other excel in other thing let it be your chief earthly glory to excel in your own craft according to the worthy counsel and charge of Anchises to his posterity in that sublime and heroical poet wherein also my dicton be include Virg 6. aeu the true law of free monarchy or the reciproque and MUTVALL dvetie betwixt a free king and his natural subject a advertisement to the reader accept i pray you my dear countryman as thankful this pamphlet that i offer unto you as love it be write for your weal i will be loath both to be faschious and fectlesse and therefore if it be not sententious at least it be short it may be you miss many thing that you look for in it but for excuse thereof consider right that I only lie down herein the true ground to teach you the right-way without waste time upon refute the adversary and yet I trust if you will take narrow tent you shall find most of their great gun pay home again either with contrary conclusion or tacit objection suppose in a dairned form and indirect for my intention be to instruct and not if i may eschew it the profit I will wish you to make of it be as well so to frame all your action according to these ground as may confirm you in the course of honest and obedient subject to your king in all time come as also when you shall fall in purpose with any that shall praise or excuse the bypass rebellion that break forth either in this country or in any other you shall herewith be arm against their Siren song lay their particular example to the square of these ground whereby you shall sound keep the course of righteous judgement decern wise of every action only according to the quality thereof and not according to your prejudge conceit of the committer so shall you by reap profit to yourselves turn my pain into pleasure but least the whole pamphlet run out at the gape mouth of this preface if it be any more enlarge I end with commit you to God and i to your charitable censure c. (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) the true law of free monarchy or the reciproque and mutual duty betwixt a free king and his natural subject as there be not a thing so necessary to be know by the people of any land next the knowledge of their god as the right knowledge of their allegiance according to the form of government establish among they especial in a monarchy which form of government as resemble the divinity approach near to perfection as all the learned and wise man from the beginning have agree upon unity be the perfection of all thing so have the ignorance and which be worse the seduce opinion of the multitude blind by they who think themselves able to teach and instruct the ignorant procure the wrack and overthrow of sundry flourish commonwealth and heap heavy calamity threaten utter destruction upon other and the smile success that unlawful rebellion have oftentimes have against prince in aage past such have be the misery and iniquity of the time have by way of practice strengthen many in their error albeit there can be a more deceivable argument then to judge ay the justness of the cause by the event thereof as hereafter shall be prove more at length and among other no commonwealth that ever have be since the beginning have have great need of the true knowledge of this ground than this our so long disorder and distract commonwealth have the misknowledge hereof be the only spring from whence have flow so many endless calamity misery and confusion as be better feel by many than the cause thereof well know and deep consider the natural zeal therefore that I bear to this my native country with the great pity I have to see the so-long disturbance thereof for lack of the true knowledge of this ground as I have say before have compel i at last to break silence to discharge my conscience to you my dear country man herein that know the ground from whence these your many endless trouble have proceed as well as you have already too-long taste the bitter fruit thereof you may by knowledge and eschew of the cause escape and divert the lamentable effect that ever necessary follow thereupon I have choose then only to set down in this short treatise the true ground of the mutual duty and allegiance betwixt a free and absolute monarch and his people not to trouble your patience with answer the contrary proposition which some have not be ashamed to set down in writ to the poison of infinite number of simple soul and their own perpetual and well deserve infamy for by answer they I can not have eschew while to pick and bite well salt their person which will rather have breed contentiousness among the reader as they have like or mislike then sound instruction of the truth which I protest to he that be the searcher of all heart be the only mark that I shoot at herein first then I will set down the true ground whereupon I be to build out of the scripture since monarchy be the true pattern of divinity as I have already say next from the fundamental law of our own kingdom which near must concern we three from the law of nature by diverse similitude draw out of the same and will conclude sign by answer the most weighty and appear incommodity that can be object the prince duty to his subject be so clear set down in many place of the scripture and so open confess by all the good prince according to their oath in their coronation as not need to be long therein I shall as short as I can run through it king be call god by the prophetical king David i sal. 82.6 because they sit upon GOD his throne in the earth and have the count of their administration to give unto he psal 101. psal 101. 2. King 18. 2. chron. 29. 2. King 22. and 23.2 34. and 35. psal 72. 1. king 3. their office be to minister justice and judgement to the people as the same David say to advance the good and punish the evil as he likewise say to establish good law to his people and procure obedience to the same as diverse good king of judah do to procure the peace of the people as the same David say to decide all controversy that can arise among they as Solomon do to be the minister of God for the weal of they that do well and as the minister of God rom. 13. to take vengeance upon they that do evil as s. Paul say and final 1. sam. 8. as a good pastor to go out and in before his people as be say in the first of Samuel jerem. 29. that through the prince prosperity the people peace may be procure as jeremy say and therefore in the coronation of our own king as well as of every christian monarch they give their oath first to maintain the religion present profess within their country according to their law whereby it be establish and to punish all those that shall press to alter or disturb the profession thereof and next to maintain all the lowable and good law make by their predecessor to see they put in execution and the breaker and violater thereof to be punish according to the tenor of the same and last to maintain the whole country and every state therein in all their ancient privilege and liberty as well against all foreign enemy as among themselves and short to procure the weal and flourish of his people not only in maintain and put to execution the old lowable law of the country and by establish of new as necessity and evil manner will require but by all other mean possible to foresee and prevent all danger that be likely to fall upon they and to maintain concord wealth and civility among they as a love father and careful watchman care for they more than for himself know himself to be ordain for they and they not for he and therefore countable to that great God who place he as his lieutenant over they upon the peril of his soul to procure the weal of both soul and body as far as in he lie of all they that be commit to his charge and this oath in the coronation be the clear civil and fundamental law whereby the king office be proper define by the law of nature the king become a natural father to all his liege at his coronation and as the father of his fatherly duty be bind to care for the nourish education and virtuous government of his child even so be the king bind to care for all his subject as all the toil and pain that the father can take for his child will be think light and well bestow by he so that the effect thereof redound to their profit and weal so ought the prince to do towards his people as the kindly father ought to foresee all inconuenient and danger that may arise towards his child and though with the hazard of his own person press to prevent the same so ought the king towards his people as the father wrath and correction upon any of his child that offend aught to be by a fatherly chastisement season with pity as long as there be any hope of amendment in they so ought the king towards any of his liege that offend in that measure and short as the father chief joy ought to be in procure his child welfare rejoice at their weal sorrow and pity at their evil to hazard for their safety travel for their rest wake for their sleep and in a word to think that his earthly felicity and life stand and live more in they nor in himself so ought a good prince think of his people as to the other branch of this mutual and reciproque band be the duty and allegiance that the liege owe to their king the ground whereof I take out of the word of Samuel dite by God spirit when God have give he commandment to hear the people voice in choose and anoint they a King and because that place of scripture be well understand be so pertinent for our purpose I have insert herein the very word of the Text. 9 now therefore hearken to their voice howbeit yet testify unto they and show they the manner of the king that shall reign over they 10 so Samuel tell all the word of the lord unto the people that ask a king of he 11 and he say this shall be the manner of the king that shall reign over you he will take your son and appoint they to his charet and to be his horseman and some shall run before his chariot 12 also he will make they his captain over thousand and captain over fifty and to ear his ground and to reap his harvest and to make instrument of war and the thing that serve for his charet 13 he will also take your daughter and make they apothicary and cook and baker 14 and he will take your field and your vineyard and your best olive tree and give they to his servant 15 and he will take the ten of your seed and of your vineyard and give it to his eunuch and to his servant 16 and he will take your man servant and your maid servant and the chief of your young man and your ass and put they to his work 17 he will take the ten of your sheep and you shall be his servant 18 and you shall cry out at that day because of your king who you have choose you and the lord God will not bear you at that day 19 but the people will not hear the voice of Samuel but do say nay but there shall be a king over we 20 and we also will be like all other nation and our king shall judge we and go out before we and fight our battle that these word and discourse of Samuel be dite by God spirit it need no further probation but that it be a place of scripture since the whole scripture be dite by that inspiration as Paul say which ground no good christian will or dare deny whereupon it must necessary follow that these speech proceed not from any ambition in Samuel as one loath to quit the reins that he so long have rule and therefore desirous by make odious the government of a king to dissuade the people from their far importunate crave of one for as the text prove it plain he then convene they to give they a resolute grant of their demand as God by his own mouth command he say harken to the voice of the people and to press to dissuade they from that which he then come to grant unto they be a thing very impertinent in a wise man much more in the prophet of the most high God and likewise it well appear in all the course of his life after that his so long refuse of their suit before come not of any ambition in he which he well prove in pray and as it be importune God for the weal of Saul yea after God have declare his reprobation unto he yet he desist not while God himself be wrath at his pray and discharge his father suit in that errand and that these word of Samuel be not utter as a prophecy of Saul their first king defection it well appear as well because we hear no mention make in the scripture of any his tyranny and oppression which if it have be will not have be leave unpainted out therein as well as his other fault be as in a true mirror of all the king behaviour who it describe as likewise in respect that Saul be choose by God for his virtue and meet quality to govern his people whereas his defection spring after-hand from the corruption of his own nature and not through any default in God who they that think so will make as a stepfather to his people in make wilful a choice of the unmeet for govern they since the election of that king lie absolute and immediate in God hand but by the contrary it be plain and evident that this speech of Samuel to the people be to prepare their heart before the hand to the due obedience of that king which God be to give unto they and therefore open up unto they what may be the intolerable quality that may fall in some of their king thereby prepare they to patience not to resist to God ordinance but as he will have say since God have grant your importunate suit in give you a king as you have else commit a error in shake off god yoke and overhasty seek of a king so beware you fall not into the next in cast off also rash that yoke which God at your earnest suit have lay upon you how hard that ever it seem to be for as you can not have obtain one without the permission and ordinance of God so may you no more fro he be once set over you shake he off without the same warrant and therefore in time arm yourselves with patience and humility since he that have the only power to make he have the only power to unmake he and you only to obey bear with these strait that I now foreshow you as with the finger of God which lie not in you to take off and will you consider the very word of the text in order as they be set down it shall plain declare the obedience that the people owe to their king in all respect first God command Samuel to do two thing the one to grant the people their suit in give they a king the other to forewarn they what some king will do unto they that they may not thereafter in their grudge and murmur say when they shall feel the snare here fore-spoken we will never have have a king of God in case when we crave he he have let we know how we will have be use by he as now we find but over-late and this be mean by these word now therefore hearken unto their voice howbeit yet testify unto they and show they the manner of the king that shall rule over they and next Samuel in execution of this commandment of God he likewise do two thing first he declare unto they what point of justice and equity their king will break in his behaviour unto they and next he put they out of hope that weary as they will they shall not have leave to shake off that yoke which God through their importunity have lay upon they the point of equity that the king shall break unto they be express in these word 11 he will take your son and appoint they to his charet and to be his horseman and some shall run before his chariot 12 also he will make they his captain over thousand and captain over fifty and to ear his ground and to reap his harvest and to make instrument of war and the thing that serve for his charet 13 he will also take your daughter and make they apothecary and cook and baker the point of justice that he shall break unto they be express in these word 14 he will take your field and your vineyard and your best olive tree and give they to his servant 15 and he will take the ten of your seed and of your vineyard and give it to his eunuch and to his servant and also the ten of your sheep as if he will say the best and noble of your blood shall be compel in slavish and servile office to serve he and not content of his own patrimony will make up a rent to his own use out of your best land vineyard orchard and store of cattle so as invert the law of nature and office of a king your person and the person of your posterity together with your land and all that you possess shall serve his private use and inordinate appetite and as unto the next point which be his forewarn they that weary as they will they shall not have leave to shake off the yoke which God through their importunity have lay upon they it be express in these word 18 and you shall cry out at that day because of your king who you have choose you and the lord will not hear you at that day as he will say when you shall find these thing in proof that now I forewarn you of although you shall grudge and murmur yet it shall not be lawful to you to cast it off in respect it be not only the ordinance of God but also yourselves have choose he unto you thereby renounce for ever all privilege by your willing consent out of your hand whereby in any time hereafter you will claim and call back unto yourselves again that power which God shall not permit you to do and for further take away of all excuse and retraction of this their contract after their consent to under-lie this yoke with all the burden that he have declare unto they he crave their answer and consent to his proposition which appear by their answer as it be express in these word 19 nay but there shall be a king over we 20 and we also will be like all other nation and our king shall judge we and go out before we and fight our battle as if they will have say all your speech and hard condition shall not scar we but we will take the good and evil of it upon we and we will be content to bear whatsoever burden it shall please our king to lay upon we aswell as other nation do and for the good we will get of he in fight our battle we will more patient bear any burden that shall please he to lay on we now then since the erection of this kingdom and monarchy among the jew and the law thereof may and aught to be a pattern to all christian and well found monarchy as be found by God himself who by his oracle and out of his own mouth give the law thereof what liberty can broil spirit and rebellious mind claim just to against any christian monarchy since they can claim to no great liberty on their part nor the people of God may have do and no great tyranny be ever execute by any prince or tyrant who they can object nor be here forewarn to the people of God and yet all rebellion countermand unto they if tyrannize over man person son daughter and servant redact noble house and man and woman of noble blood to slavish and servile office and extortion and spoil of their land and good to the prince own private use and commodity and of his courtier and servant may be call a tyranny and that this proposition ground upon the scripture may the more clear appear to be true by the practice often prove in the same book we never read that ever the prophet persuade the people to rebel against the prince how wicked soever he be when Samuel by god command pronounce to the same king Saul 1. sam. 15. that his kingdom be rend from he and give to another which in effect be a degrade of he yet his next action follow that be peaceable to turn home and with flood of tear to pray to God to have some compassion upon he and David notwithstanding he be inaugurate in that same degrade king room not only when he be cruel persecute for no offence but good service do unto he will not presume have he in his power scant but with great reverence to touch the garment of the anoint of the lord and in his word bless he but likewise 1. sam. 2 4. 2. sam. 1. when one come to he vaunt himself untrue to have slay Saul he without form of process or trial of his guilt cause only for guiltiness of his tongue put he to sudden death and although there be never a more monstrous persecutor and tyrant nor Achab be yet all the rebellion that Elias ever raise against he be to fly to the wilderness where for fault of sustentation he be feed with the corbies and I think no man will doubt but Samuel David and Elias have as great power to persuade the people if they have like to have employ their credit to uproar and rebellion against these wicked king as any of our seditious preacher in these day of whatsoever religion either in this country or in France have that busy themselves most to stir up rebellion under cloak of religion this far the only love of verity I protest without hatred at their person have move i to be somewhat satyric and if any will lean to the extraordinary example of degrade or kill of king in the scripture thereby to cloak the people rebellion as by the deed of jehu and such like extraordinary I answer beside that they want the like warrant that they have if extraordinary example of the scripture shall be draw in daily practice murder under trai as in the person of Ahud and jael theft as in the person of the israelite come out of Egypt lie to their parent to the hurt of their brother as in the person of jacob shall all be count as lawful and allowable virtue as rebellion against prince and to conclude the practice through the whole scripture prove the people obedience give to that sentence in the law of God thou shall not rail upon the judge neither speak evil of the ruler of thy people to end then the ground of my proposition take out of the scripture let two special and notable example one under the law another under the evangel jer. 27. conclude this part of my allegiance under the law jeremy threaten the people of God with utter destruction for rebellion to Nabuchadnezar the king of Babel who although he be a idolatrous persecuter a foreign king a tyrant and usurper of their liberty yet in respect they have once receive and acknowledge he for their king he not only command they to obey he jere. 29. but even to pray for his prosperity adjoin the reason to it because in his prosperity stand their peace and under the evangel that king who Paul bid the roman obey and serve for conscience sake jere. 13. be Nero that bloody tyrant a infamy to his age and a monster to the world be also a idolatrous persecuter as the king of Babel be if then idolatry and defection from God tyranny over their people and persecution of the saint for their profession sake hinder not the spirit of God to command his people under all high pain to give they all due and hearty obedience for conscience sake give to Caesar that which be Caesar and to God that which be god as Christ say and that this practice throughout the book of God agree with this law which he make in the erection of that monarchy as be at length before deduce what shameless presumption be it to any christian people now adays to claim to that unlawful liberty which God refuse to his own peculiar and choose people short then to take up in two or three sentence ground upon all these argument out of the law of God the duty and allegiance of the people to their lawful king their obedience I say aught to be to he as to God lieutenant in earth obey his command in all thing except direct against God as the command of God minister acknowledge he a judge set by GOD over they have power to judge they but to be judge only by GOD who to only he must give count of his judgement fear he as their judge love he as their father pray for he as their protector for his continuance if he be good for his amendment if he be wicked follow and obey his lawful command eschew and fly his fury in his unlawful without resistance but by sob and tear to God according to that sentence use in the primitive church in the time of the persecution now as for the describe the allegiance that the liege owe to their native king out of the fundamental and civil law especial of this country as I promise the ground must first be set down of the first manner of establish the law and form of government among we that the ground be first right lay we may thereafter build right thereupon although it be true according to the affirmation of those that pride themselves to be the scourge of tyrant that in the first beginning of king rise among gentile in the time of the first age diverse commonwealth and society of man choose out one among themselves who for his virtue and valour be more eminent than the rest be choose out by they and set up in that room to maintain the weak in their right to throw down oppressor and to foster and continue the society among man which can not otherwise but by virtue of that unity be well do yet these example be nothing pertinent to we because our kingdom and diverse other monarchy be not in that case but have their beginning in a far contrary fashion for as our chronicle bear witness this i will and especial our part of it be scant inhabit but by very few and they as barbarous and scant of civility as number there come our first king Fergus with a great number with he out of Ireland which be long inhabit before we and make himself master of the country by his own friendship and force as well of the ireland-man that come with he as of the countryman that willing fall to he he make himself king and lord as well of the whole land as of the whole inhabitant within the same thereafter he and his successor a long while after their be king make and establish their law from time to time and as the occasion require so the truth be direct contrary in our state to the false affirmation of such seditious writer as will persuade we that the law and state of our country be establish before the admit of a king where by the contrary you see it plain prove that a wise king come in among first establish the estate and form of government and thereafter make law by himself and his successor according thereto the king therefore in Scotland be before any estate or rank of man within the same before any parliament be hold or law make and by they be the land distribute which at the first be whole they state erect and decern and form of government devise and establish and so it follow of necessity that the king be the author and maker of the law and not the law of the king and to prove this my assertion more clear it be evident by the roll of our chancellery which contain our elder and fundamental law that the king be and the whole subject be but his vassal and from he hold all their land as their over-lord who according to good service do unto he change their hold from tack to few from ward to blanch erect new barony and unite old without advice or authority of either parliament or any other subalterin judicial seat so as if wrong may be admit in play albeit I grant wrong shall be wrong in all person the king may have a better colour for his pleasure without further reason to take the land from his liege as over-lord of the whole and do with it as please he since all that they hold be of he then as foolish writer say the people may unmake the king and put a other in his room but either of they as unlawful and against the ordinance of God aught to be alike odious to be think much less put in practice and according to these fundamental law already allege we daily see that in the parliament which be nothing else but the head court of the king and his vassal the law be but crave by his subject and only make by he at their rogation and with their advice for albeit the king make daily statute and ordinance enjoin such pain thereto as he think meet without any advice of parliament or estate yet it lie in the power of no parliament to make any kind of law or statute without his sceptre be to it for give it the force of a law and although diverse change have be in other country of the blood royal and kingly house the kingdom be reave by conquest from one to another as in our neighbour country in England which be never in we yet the same ground of the king right over all the land and subject thereof remain alike in all other free monarchy as well as in this for when the bastard of Normandy come into England and make himself king be it not by force and with a mighty army where he give the law and take none change the law invert the order of government set down the stranger his follower in many of the old possessor room as at this day well appear a great part of the gentleman in England be come of the norman blood and their old law which to this day they be rule by be write in his language and not in they and yet his successor have with great happiness enjoy the crown to this day whereof the like be also do by all they that conquest they before and for conclusion of this point that the king be over-lord over the whole land it be likewise daily prove by the law of our hoard of want of heir and of Bastardies for if a hoard be find under the earth because it be no more in the keep or use of any person it of the law pertain to the king if a person inheritor of any land or good die without any sort of heir all his land and good return to the king and if a bastard die unrehable without heir of his body which rehable only lie in the king hand all that he have likewise return to the king and as you see it manifest that the king be over-lord of the whole land so be he master over every person that inhabit the same have power over the life and death of every one of they for although a just prince will not take the life of any of his subject without a clear law yet the same law whereby he take they be make by himself or his predecessor and so the power flow always from himself as by daily experience we see good and just prince will from time to time make new law and statute adjoin the penalty to the breaker thereof which before the law be make have be no crime to the subject to have commit not that I deny the old definition of a king and of a law which make the king to be a speak law and the law a dumb king for certain a king that govern not by his law can neither be countable to God for his administration nor have a happy and establish reign for albeit it be true that I have at length prove that the king be above the law as both the author and giver of strength thereto yet a good king will not only delight to rule his subject by the law but even will conform himself in his own action thereuneto always keep that ground that the health of the commonwealth be his chief law and where he see the law doubtsome or rigorous he may interpret or mitigate the same lest otherwise be and therefore general law make public in parliament may upon know respect to the king by his authority be mitigate and suspend upon cause only know to he as likewise although I have say a good king will frame all his action to be according to the law yet be he not bind thereto but of his good will and for good example-giving to his subject for as in the law of abstain from eat of flesh in lenton the king will for example sake make his own house to observe the law yet no man will think he need to take a licence to eat flesh and although by our law the bear and wear of hagbut and pistolet be forbid yet no man can find any fault in the king for cause his train use they in any ray upon the borderer or other malefactor or rebellious subject so as I have already say a good king although he be above the law will subject and frame his action thereto for example sake to his subject and of his own freewill but not as subject or bind thereto since I have so clear prove then out of the fundamental law and practice of this country what right and power a king have over his land and subject it be easy to be understand what allegiance and obedience his liege owe unto he I mean always of such free monarchy as our king be and not of elective king and much less of such sort of governor as the duke of Venice be who aristocratick and limit government be nothing like to free monarchy although the malice of some writer have not be ashamed to mis-know any difference to be betwixt they and if it be not lawful to any particular lord tenant or vassal upon whatsoever pretext to control and displace their master and over-lord as be clear nor the sun by all law of the world how much less may the subject and vassal of the great over-lord the king control or displace he and since in all inferior judgement in the land the people may not upon any respect displace their magistrate although but subaltern for the people of a borough can displace their provost before the time of their election nor in ecclesiastical policy the flock can upon any pretence displace the pastor nor judge of he yea even the poor schoolmaster can be displace by his scholar if these I say whereof some be but inferior subaltern and temporal magistrate and none of they equal in any sort to the dignity of a king can be displace for any occasion or pretext by they that be rule by they how much less be it lawful upon any pretext to control or displace the great provost and great schoolmaster of the whole land except by invert the order of all law and reason the command may be make to command their commander the judge to judge their judge and they that be govern to govern their time about their lord and governor and the agreement of the law of nature in this our ground with the law and constitution of God and man already allege will by two similitude easy appear the king towards his people be right compare to a father of child and to a head of a body compose of diverse member for as father the good prince and magistrate of the people of God acknowledge themselves to their subject and for all other well rule commonwealth the style of be ever and be common use to king and the proper office of a king towards his subject agree very well with the office of the head towards the body and all member thereof for from the head be the seat of judgement proceed the care and foresight of guide and prevent all evil that may come to the body or any part thereof the head care for the body so do the king for his people as the discourse and direction flow from the head and the execution according thereunto belong to the rest of the member every one according to their office so be it betwixt a wise prince and his people as the judgement come from the head may not only employ the member every one in their own office as long as they be able for it but likewise in case any of they be affect with any infirmity must care and provide for their remedy in-case it be curable and if otherwise gar cut they off for fear of infect of the rest even so be it betwixt the prince and his people and as there be ever hope of cure any disease member by the direction of the head as long as it be whole but by the contrary if it be trouble all the member be partaker of that pain so be it betwixt the prince and his people and now first for the father part who natural love to his child I describe in the first part of this my discourse speak of the duty that king owe to their subject consider I pray you what duty his child owe to he and whether upon any pretext whatsoever it will not be think monstrous and unnatural to his son to rise up against he to control he at their appetite and when they think good to slay he or to cut he off and adopt to themselves any other they please in his room or can any pretence of wickedness or rigour on his part be a just excuse for his child to put hand into he and although we see by the course of nature that love use to descend more then to ascend in case it be true that the father hate and wrong the child never so much will any man endue with the least sponke of reason think it lawful for they to meet he with the line yea suppose the father be furious follow his son with a draw sword be it lawful for they to turn and strike again or make any resistance but by flight I think sure if there be no more but the example of bruit beast and unreasonable creature it may serve well enough to qualify and prove this my argnment we read often the piety that the stork have to their old and decay parent and general we know that there be many sort of beast and fowl that with violence and many bloody stroke will beat and banish their young one from they how soon they perceive they to be able to fend themselves but we never read or hear of any resistance on their part except among the viper which prove such person as aught to be reasonable creature and yet unnatural follow this example to be endue with their viperous nature and for the similitude of the head and the body it may very well fall out that the head will be force to garre cut off some rot member as I have already say to keep the rest of the body in integrity but what state the body can be in if the head for any infirmity that can fall to it be cut off I leave it to the reader judgement so as to conclude this part if the child may upon any pretext that can be imagine lawful rise up against their father cut he off and choose any other who they please in his room and if the body for the weal of it may for any infirmity that can be in the head strike it off than I can deny that the people may rebel control and displace or cut off their king at their own pleasure and upon respect move they and whether these similitude represent better the office of a king or the office of master or deacon of craft or doctor in physic which jolly comparison be use by such writer as maintain the contrary proposition I leave it also to the reader discretion and in case any doubt may arise in any part of this treatise I will according to my promise with the solution of four principal and most weighty doubt that the adversary may object conclude this discourse and first it be cast up by diverse that employ their pen upon apology for rebellion and treason that every man be bear to carry such a natural zeal and duty to his commonwealth as to his mother that see it so rend and deadly wound as while it will be by wicked and tyrannous king good citizen will be force for the natural zeal and duty they owe to their own native country to put their hand to work for free their commonwealth from such a pest whereunto I give two answer first it be a sure axiom in theology that evil shall not be do that good may come of it the wickedness therefore of the king can never make they that be ordain to be judge by he to become his judge and if it be not lawful to a private man to revenge his private injury upon his private adversary since God have only give the sword to the magistrate how much less be it lawful to the people or any part of they who all be but private man the authority be always with the magistrate as I have already prove to take upon they the use of the sword who to it belong not against the public magistrate who to only it belong next in place of relieve the commonwealth out of distress which be their only excuse and colour they shall heap double distress and desolation upon it and so their rebellion shall procure the contrary effect that they pretend it for for a king can be imagine to be so unruly and tyrannous but the commonwealth will be keep in better order notwithstanding thereof by he than it can be by his way-taking for first all sudden mutation be perilous in commonwealth hope be thereby give to all bare man to set up themselves and fly with other man feather the reins be loose to all the insolence that disorder people can commit by hope of impunity because of the looseness of all thing and next it be certain that a king can never be so monstrous vicious but he will general favour justice and maintain some order except in the particular wherein his inordinate lust and passion carry he away where by the contrary no king be nothing be unlawful to none and so the old opinion of the philosopher prove true that better it be to live in a commonwealth where nothing be lawful then where all thing be lawful to all man the commonwealth at that time resemble a undaunted young horse that have cast his rider for as the divine poet dv BARTAS say better it be to suffer some disorder in the estate and some spot in the commonwealth then in pretend to reform utter to overthrow the republic the second objection they ground upon the curse that hang over the commonwealth where a wicked king reign and say they there can be a more acceptable deed in the sight of God nor more dutiful to their commonweal then to free the country of such a curse and vindicate to they their liberty which be natural to all creature to crave whereunto for answer I grant indeed that a wicked king be send by God for a curse to his people and a plague for their sin but that it be lawful to they to shake off that curse at their own hand which God have lay on they that I deny and may so do just will any deny that the king of Babel be a curse to the people of God as be plain fore-spoken and threaten unto they in the prophecy of their captivity and what be Nero to the christian church in his time and yet jeremy and Paul as you have else hear command they not only to obey they but hearty to pray for their welfare it be certain then as I have already by the law of God sufficient prove that patience earnest prayer to God and amendment of their life be the only lawful mean to move God to relieve they of that heavy curse as for vindicate to themselves their own liberty what lawful power have they to revoke to themselves again those privilege which by their own consent before be so full put out of their hand for if a prince can just bring back again to himself the privilege once bestow by he or his predecessor upon any state or rank of his subject how much less may the subject reave out of the prince hand that superiority which he and his predecessor have so long brook over they but the unhappy iniquity of the time which have oft time give over good success to their treasonable attempt furnish they the ground of their three objection for say they the fortunate success that God have so oft give to such enterprise prove plain by the practice that God favour the justness of their quarrel to the which I answer that it be true indeed that all the success of battle as well as other worldly thing lie only in God hand and therefore it be that in the scripture he take to himself the style of God of host but upon that general to conclude that he ever give victory to the just quarrel will prove the Philistine and diverse other neighbour enemy of the people of God to have oft time have the just quarrel against the people of God in respect of the many victory they obtain against they and by that same argument they have also just quarrel against the ark of God for they win it in the field and keep it long prisoner in their country as likewise by all good writer as well theologue as other the duel and singular combat be disallow which be only make upon pretence that GOD will kiss thereby the justice of the quarrel for we must consider that the innocent party be not innocent before God and therefore God will make oft time they that have the wrong side revenge just his quarrel and when he have do cast his scourge in the fire as he oft time do to his own people stir up and strengthen their enemy while they be humble in his sight and then deliver they in their hand so God as the great judge may just punish his deputy and for his rebellion against he stir up his rebel to meet he with the like and when it be do the part of the instrument be no better than the devil part be in tempt and torture such as God commit to he as his hangman to do therefore as I say in the beginning it be oft time a very deceivable argument to judge of the cause by the event and the last objection be ground upon the mutual paction and adstipulation as they call it betwixt the king and his people at the time of his coronation for there say they there be a mutual paction and contract bind up and swear betwixt the king and the people whereupon it follow that if the one part of the contract or the indent be break upon the king side the people be no long bind to keep their part of it but be thereby free of their oath for say they a contract betwixt two party of all law free the one party if the other break unto he as to this contract allege make at the coronation of a king although I deny any such contract to be make then especial contain such a clause as they allege yet I confess that a king at his coronation or at the entry to his kingdom willing promise to his people to discharge honourable and true the office give he by God over they but presume that thereafter he break his promise unto they never so inexcusable the question be who shall be judge of the break give unto they this contractwere make unto they never so sick according to their allegiance I think no man that have but the small entrance into the civil law will doubt that of all law either civil or municipal of any nation a contract can be think break by the one party and so the other likewise to be free therefrom except that first a lawful trial and cognition be have by the ordinary judge of the breaker thereof or else every man may be both party and judge in his own cause which be absurd once to be think now in this contract I say betwixt the king and his people God be doubtless the only judge both because to he only the king must make count of his administration as be oft say before as likewise by the oath in the coronation God be make judge and revenger of the breaker for in his presence as only judge of oath all oath ought to be make then since God be the only judge betwixt the two party contractor the cognition and revenge must only appertain to he it follow therefore of necessity that God must first give sentence upon the king that break before the people can think themselves free of their oath what justice then be it that the party shall be both judge and party usurp upon himself the office of God may by this argument easy appear and shall it lie in the hand of headless multitude when they please to weary off subjection to cast off the yoke of government that God have lay upon they to judge and punish he whomby they shall be judge and punish and in that case wherein by their violence they kythe themselves to be most passionate party to use the office of a ungracious judge or arbiter nay to speak true of that case as it stand betwixt the king and his people none of they ought to judge of the other break for consider right the two party at the time of their mutual promise the king be the one party and the whole people in one body be the other party and therefore since it be certain that a king in case so it shall fall out that his people in one body have rebel against he he shall not in that case as think himself free of his promise and oath become a utter enemy and practice the wreak of his whole people and native country although he ought just to punish the principal author and bellows of that universal rebellion how much less than ought the people that be always subject unto he and naked of all authority on their part press to judge and overthrow he otherwise the people as the one party contracter shall no soon challenge the king as breaker but he assoon shall judge they as breaker so as the victor make the tyner the traitor as our proverb be the party shall aye become both judge and party in his own particular as I have already say and it be here likewise to be note that the duty and allegiance which the people swear to their prince be not only bind to themselves but likewise to their lawful heir and posterity the lineal succession of crown be begin among the people of God and happy continue in diverse Christian commonwealth so as no objection either of heresy or whatsoever private statute or law may free the people from their oath-giving to their king and his succession establish by the old fundamental law of the kingdom for as he be their heritable over-lord and so by birth not by any right in the coronation come to his crown it be a like unlawful the crown ever stand full to displace he that succeed thereto as to eject the former for at the very moment of the expire of the king reign the near and lawful heir enter in his place and so to refuse he or intrude another be not to hold out uncom in but to expel and put out their righteous King and I trust at this time whole France acknowledge the superstitious rebellion of the liguer who upon pretence of heresy by force of arm hold so long out to the great desolation of their whole country their native and righteous king from possess of his own crown and natural kingdom not that by all this former discourse of i and apology for king I mean that whatsoever error and intolerable abomination a sovereign prince commit he ought to escape all punishment as if thereby the world be only ordain for king and they without controlment to turn it upside down at their pleasure but by the contrary by remit they to God who be their only ordinary judge I remit they to the soreit and sharp schoolmaster that can be devise for they for the further a king be prefer by God above all other rank and degree of man and the high that his seat be above they the great be his obligation to his maker and therefore in case he forget himself his unthankfulness be in the same measure of height the sad and sharp will his correction be and according to the greatness of the height he be in the weight of his fall will recompense the same for the further that any person be oblige to God his offence become and grow so much the great than it will be in any other Jove thunderclap light often and sore upon the high and stately oak then on the low and supple willow tree and the high bench be sliddriest to sit upon neither be it ever hear that any king forget himself towards God or in his vocation but God with the greatness of the plague revenge the greatness of his ingratitude neither think I by the force and argument of this my discourse so to persuade the people that none will hereafter be raise up and rebel against wicked prince but remit to the justice and providence of God to stir up such scourge as please he for punishment of wicked king who make the very vermin and filthy dust of the earth to bridle the insolency of proud Pharaoh my only purpose and intention in this treatise be to persuade as far as lie in i by these sure and infallible ground all such good christian reader as bear not only the naked name of a christian but kith the fruit thereof in their daily form of life to keep their heart and hand free from such monstrous and unnatural rebellion whensoever the wickedness of a prince shall procure the same at God hand that when it shall please God to cast such scourge of prince and instrument of his fury in the fire you may stand up with clean hand and unspotted conscience have prove yourselves in all your action true christian towards God and dutiful subject towards your king have remit the judgement and punishment of all his wrong to he who to only of right it appertain but crave at God and hope that God shall continue his blessing with we in not send such fearful desolation I hearty wish our king behaviour so to be and continue among we as our god in earth and love father endue with such property as I describe a king in the first part of this treatise and that you my dear countryman and charitable reader may press by all mean to procure the prosperity and welfare of your king that as he must on the one part think all his earthly felicity and happiness ground upon your weal care more for himself for your sake then for his own think himself only ordain for your weal such holy and happy emulation may arise betwixt he and you as his care for your quietness and your care for his honour and preservation may in all your action daily strive together that the land may think themselves bless with such a king and the king may think himself most happy in rule over so love and obedient subject a counterblaste to tobacco to the reader as every human body dear country man how wholesome soever be notwithstanding subject or at least natural incline to some sort of disease or infirmity so be there no commonwealth or body-politicke how well govern or peaceable soever it be that lack the own popular error and natural incline corruption and therefore be it no wonder although this our country and commonwealth though peaceable though wealthy though long flourish in both be among the rest subject to the own natural infirmity we be of all nation the people most love and most reverent obedient to our prince yet be we as time have often bear witness too easy to be seduce to make rebellion upon very slight ground our fortunate and oft prove valour in war abroad our hearty and reverend obedience to our prince at home have breed we a long and a thrice happy peace our peace have bread wealth and peace and wealth have bring forth a general sluggishness which make we wallow in all sort of idle delight and soft delicacy the first seed of the subversion of all great monarchy our clergy be become negligent and lazy our nobility and gentry prodigal and sell to their private delight our lawyer covetous our common people prodigal and curious and general all sort of people more careful for their private end then for their mother the commonwealth for remedy whereof it be the king part as the proper physician of his politicke-bodie to purge it of all those disease by medicine meet for the same as by a certain mild and yet just form of government to maintain the public quietness and prevent all occasion of commotion by the example of his own person and court to make we all ashamed of our sluggish delicacy and to stir we up to the practice again of all honest exercise and martial shadow of war as likewise by he and his court moderateness in apparel to make we ashamed of our prodigality by his quick admonition and careful oversee of the clergy to waken they up again to be more diligent in their office by the sharp trial and severe punishment of the partial covetous and bribe lawyer to reform their corruption and general by the example of his own person and by the due execution of good law to reform and abolish piece and piece these old and evil ground abuse for this will not be but as every one of these disease must from the king receive the own cure proper for it so be there some sort of abuse in commonwealth that though they be of so base and contemptible a condition as they be too low for the law to look on and to mean for a king to interpone his authority or bend his eye upon yet be they corruption aswell as the great of they so be a ant a animal aswell as a elephant so be a wren aswell as a swan and so be a small dint of the toothache a disease aswell as the fearful plague be but for these base sort of corruption in commonwealth not only the king or any inferior magistrate but may serve to be a physician by discover and impugn the error and by persuade reformation thereof and sure in my opinion there can be a more base and yet hurtful corruption in a country then be the vile use or rather abuse of take tobacco in this kingdom which have move i short to discover the abuse thereof in this follow little pamphlet if any think it a light argument so be it but a toy that be bestow upon it and since the subject be but of smoke i think the fume of a idle brain may serve for a sufficient battery against so fumou and feeble a enemy if my ground be find true it be all i look for but if they carry the force of persuasion with they it be all i can wish and more than I can expect my only care be that you my dear countryman may right conceive even by this small trifle of the sincerity of my meaning in great matter never to spare any pain that may tend to the procure of your weal and prosperity a counterblaste to tobacco that the manifold abuse of this vile custom of tobacco take may the better be espy it be fit that first you enter into consideration both of the first original thereof and likewise of the reason of the first entry thereof into this country for certain as such custom that have their first institution either from a godly necessary or honourable ground and be first bring in by the mean of some worthy virtuous and great personage be ever and most just hold in great and reverend estimation and account by all wise virtuous and temperate spirit so shall it by the contrary just bring a great disgrace into that sort of custom which have their original from base corruption and barbarity do in like sort make their first entry into a country by a inconsiderate and childish affectation of novelty as be the true case of the first invention of tobacco take and of the first entry thereof among we for tobacco be a common herb which though under diverse name grow almost every where be first find out by some of the barbarous indian to be a preservative or antidote against the pock a filthy disease whereunto these barbarous people be as all man know very much subject what through the uncleanely and adust constitution of their body and what through the intemperate heat of their climate so that as from they be first bring into Christendom that most detestable disease so from they likewise be bring this use of tobacco as a stink and unsavoury antidote for so corrupt and execrable a malady the stink suffumigation whereof they yet use against that disease make so one canker or venom to eat out another and now good countryman let we I pray you consider what honour or policy can move we to imitate the barbarous and beastly manner of the wild godless and slavish indian especial in so vile and stink a custom shall we that disdain to imitate the manner of our neighbour France have the style of the first christian kingdom and that can endure the spirit of the spaniard their king be now comparable in largeness of dominion to the great emperor of Turkey shall we I say that have be so long civil and wealthy in peace famous and invincible in war fortunate in both we that have be ever able to aid any of our neighbour but never deaf any of their ear with any of our supplication for assistance shall we I say without blush abase ourselves so far as to imitate these beastly indian slave to the spaniard refuse to the world and as yet alien from the holy covenant of God why do we not as well imitate they in walk naked as they do in prefer glass feather and such toy to gold and precious stone as they do yea why do we not deny God and adore the devil as they do now to the corrupt baseness of the first use of this tobacco do very well agree the foolish and groundless first entry thereof into this kingdom it be not so long since the first entry of this abuse among we here as this present age can yet very well remember both the first author and the form of the first introduction of it among we it be neither bring in by king great conqueror nor learned doctor of physic with the report of a great discovery for a conquest some two or three savage man be bring in together with this savage custom but the pity be the poor wild barbarous man die but that vile barbarous custom be yet alive yea in fresh vigour so as it seem a miracle to i how a custom spring from so vile a ground and bring in by a father so general hate shall be welcome upon so slender a warrant for if they that first put it in practice here have remember for what respect it be use by they from whence it come I be sure they will have be loath to have take so far the imputation of that disease upon they as they do by use the cure thereof for and counterpoison be never use but where poison be think to precede but since it be true that diverse custom slight ground and with no better warrant enter in a commonwealth may yet in the use of they thereafter prove both necessary and profitable it be therefore next to be examine if there be not a full sympathy and true proportion between the base ground and foolish entry and the loathsome and hurtful use of this stink antidote I be now therefore hearty to pray you to consider first upon what false and erroneous ground you have first build the general good like thereof and next what sin towards God and foolish vanity before the world you commit in the detestable use of it as for these deceitful ground that have special move you to take a good and great conceit thereof I shall content myself to examine here only four of the principal of they two found upon the theoric of a deceivable appearance of reason and two of they upon the mistake practic of general experience first it be think by you a sure aphorism in the physics that the brain of all man be natural cold and wet all dry and hot thing shall be good for they of which nature this stink suffumigation be and therefore of good use to they of this argument both the proposition and assumption be false and so the conclusion can but be void of itself for as to the proposition that because the brain be cold and moist therefore thing that be hot and dry be best for they it be a inept consequence for man be compound of the four complexion who father be the four element although there be a mixture of they all in all the part of his body yet must the diverse part of our microcosm or little world within ourselves be diverse more incline some to one some to another complexion according to the diversity of their use that of these discord a perfect harmony may be make up for the maintenance of the whole body the application then of a thing of a contrary nature to any of these part be to interrupt they of their due function and by consequence hurtful to the health of the whole body as if a man because the liver be hot as the fountain of blood and as it be a oven to the stomach will therefore apply and wear close upon his liver and stomach a cake of lead he may within a very short time I hope be sustain very good cheap at a ordinary beside the clear of his conscience from that deadly sin of gluttony and as if because the heart be full of vital spirit and in perpetual motion a man will therefore lay a heavy pound stone on his breast for stay and hold down that wanton palpitation I doubt not but his breast will be more bruise with the weight thereof than the heart will be comfort with such a disagreeable and contrarious cure and even so be it with the brain for if a man because the brain be cold and humid will therefore use inward by smell or outward by application thing of hot and dry quality all the gain that he can make thereof will only be to put himself in a great forwardness for run mad by overwatch himself the coldness and moistness of our brain be the only ordinary mean that procure our sleep and rest indeed I do not deny but when it fall out that any of these or any part of our body grow to be distemper and to tend to a extremity beyond the compass of nature temperate mixture that in that case cure of contrary quality to the intemperate inclination of that part be wise prepare and discreet minister may be both necessary and helpful for strengthen and assist nature in the expulsion of her enemy for this be the true definition of all profitable physic but first these cure ought not to be use but where there be need of they the contrary whereof be daily practise in this general use of tobacco by all sort and complexion of people and next I deny the minor of this argument as I have already say in regard that this tobacco be not simple of a dry and hot quality but rather have a certain venomous faculty join with the heat thereof which make it have a antipathy against nature as by the hateful smell thereof do well appear for the nose be the proper organ and convoy of the sense of smell to the brain which be the only fountain of that sense do ever serve we for a infallible witness whether that odour which we smell be healthful or hurtful to the brain except when it fall out that the senseit self be corrupt and abuse through some infirmity and distemper in the brain and that the suffumigation thereof can have a dry quality it need no further probation then that it be a smoke all smoke and vapour be of itself humid as draw near to the nature of the air and easy to be resolve again into water whereof there need no other proof but the meteor which be breed of nothing else but of the vapour and exhalation suck up by the sun out of the earth the sea and water yet be the same smoky vapour turn and transform into reins snow dew hoar frost and such like watery meteor as by the contrary the rainy cloud be often transform and evaporate in bluster wind the second argument ground on a show of reason be that this filthy smoke aswell through the heat and strength thereof as by a natural force and quality be able and fit to purge both the head and stomach of rheum and distillation as experience teach by the spit and avoid phlegm immediate after the take of it but the fallacy of this argument may easy appear by my late prepare description of the meteor for even as the smoky vapour suck up by the sun and stay in the low and cold region of the air be there contract into cloud and turn into rain and such other watery meteor so this stink smoke be suck up by the nose and imprison in the cold and moist brain be by their cold and wet faculty turn and cast forth again in watery distillation and so be you make free and purge of nothing but that wherewith you wilful burden yourselves and therefore be you no wise in take tobacco for purge you of distillation then if for prevent the colic you will take all kind of windy meat and drink and for prevent of the stone you will take all kind of meat and drink that will breed gravel in the kidney and then when you be force to avoid much wind out of your stomach and much gravel in your urine that you shall attribute the thank thereof to such nourishment as breed those within you that behoove either to be expel by the force of nature or you to have burst at the broad side as the proverb be as for the other two reason found upon experience the first of which be that the whole people will not have take so general a good like thereof if they have not by experience find it very sovereign and good for they for answer thereunto how easy the mind of any people wherewith God have
