replenish this world may be draw to the foolish affectation of any novelty I leave it to the discreet judgement of any man that be reasonable do we not daily see that a man can no soon bring over from beyond the sea any new form of apparel but that he can be think a man of spirit that will not present imitate the same and so from hand to hand it spread till it be practise by all not for any commodity that be in it but only because it be come to be the fashion for such be the force of that natural self-love in every one of we and such be the corruption of envy breed in the breast of every one as we can be content unless we imitate every thing that our fellow do and so prove ourselves capable of every thing whereof they be capable like ape counterfeit the manner of other to our own destruction for let one or two of the great master of mathematics in any of the two famous university but constant affirm any clear day that they seesome strange apparition in the sky they will I warrant you be second by the great part of the student in that profession so loath will they be to be think inferior to their fellow either in depth of knowledge or sharpness of sight and therefore the general good like and embrace of this foolish custom do but only proceed from that affectation of novelty and popular error whereof I have already speak the other argument draw from a mistake experience be but the more particular probation of this general because it be allege to be find true by proof that by the take of tobacco diverse and very many do find themselves cure of diverse disease as on the other part no man ever receive harm thereby in this argument there be first a great mistake and next a monstrous absurdity for be it not a very great mistake to take as they say in the logic because peradventure when a sick man have have his disease at the height he have at that instant take tobacco and afterward his disease take the natural course of decline and consequent the patient of recover his health o then the tobacco forsooth be the worker of that miracle beside that it be a thing well know to all physician that the apprehension and conceit of the patient have by waken and unite the vital spirit and so strengthen nature a great power and virtue to cure diverse disease for a evident proof of mistake in the like case I pray you what foolish boy what silly wench what old dote wife or ignorant country clown be not a physician for the toothache for the colic and diverse such common disease yea will not every man you meet withal teach you a sundry curefor the same and swear by that mean either himself or some of his near kinsman and friend be cure and yet I hope no man be so foolish as to believe they and all these toy do only proceed fro the mistake as I have already say and so if a man chance to recover one of any disease after he have take tobacco that must have the thanks of all but by the contrary if a man smoke himself to death with it and many have do o then some other disease must bear the blame for that fault so do old harlot thank their harlotry for their many year that custom be healthful say they but never have mind how many die of the pock in the flower of their youth and so do old drunkard think they prolong their day by their swinelike diet but never remember how many die drown in drink before they be half old and what great absurdity can therebe then to say that one cure shall serve for diverse nay contrarious sort of disease it be a undoubted ground among all physician that there be almost no sort either of nourishment or medicine that have not some thing in it disagreeable to some part of man body because as I have already say the nature of the temperature of every part be so different from another that according to the old proverb that which be good for the head be evil for the neck and the shoulder for even as a strong enemy that invade a town or fortress although in his siege thereof he do belay and compass it round about yet he make his breach and entry at some one or few special part thereof which he have try and find to be weak and least able to resist so sickness do make her particular assault upon such part or part of our body as be weak and easy to be overcome by that sort of disease which then do assail we although all the rest of the body by sympathy feel itself to be as it be belay and besiege by the affliction of that special part the grief and smart thereof be by the sense of feel disperse through all the rest of our member and therefore the skilful physician press by such cure to purge and strengthen that part which be afflict as be only fit for that sort of disease and do best agree with the nature of that infirm part which be abuse to a disease of another nature will prove as hurtful for the one as helpful for the other yea not only will a skilful and wary physician becarefull to use no cure but that which be fit for that sort of disease but he will also consider all other circumstance and make the remedy suitable thereunto as the temperature of the clime where the patient be the constitution of the planet the time of the moon the season of the year the age and complexion of the patient and the present state of his body in strength or weakness for one cure must not ever be use for the selfsame disease but according to the vary of any of the foresay circumstance that sort of remedy must be use which be fit for the same where by the contrary in this case such be the miraculous omnipotency of our strong taste tobacco as it cure all sort of disease which never any drug can do before in all person and at all time it cure all manner of distillation either in the head or stomach if you believe their axiom although in very deed it do both corrupt the brain and by cause over quick digestion fill the stomach full of crudity it cure the gout in the foot and which be miraculous in that very instant when the smoke thereof as light fly up into the head the virtue thereof as heavy run down to the little toe it help all sort of ague it make a man sober that be drink it refresh a weary man and yet make a man hungry be take when they go to bed it make one sleep sound and yet be take when a man be sleepy and drowsy it will as they say awake his brain and quicken his understanding as for cure of the pock it serve for that use but among the pocky indian slave here in England it be refine and will not deign to cure here any other then clean and gentlemanly disease o omnipotent power of tobacco and if it can by the smoke thereof chase out devil as the smoke of Tobias fish do which I be sure can smell no strongly it will serve for a precious relic both for the superstitious priest and the insolent puritan to cast out devil withal admit then and not confess that the use thereof be healthful for some sort of disease shall it be use for all sickness shall it be use by all man shall it be use at all time yea shall it be use by able young strong healthful man medicine have that virtue that it never leave a man in that state wherein it find he it make a sick man whole but a whole man sick and as medicine help nature be take at time of necessity so be ever and continual use it do but weaken weary and wear nature what speak I of medicine nay let a man every hour of the day or as oft as many in this country use to take tobacco let a man I say but take as oft the best sort of nourishment in meat and drink that can be devise he shall with the continual use thereof weaken both his head and his stomach all his member shall becomefeeble his spirit dull and in the end as a drowsy lazy belly-god he shall evanish in a lethargy and from this weakness it proceed that many in this kingdom have have such a continual use of take this unsavoury smoke as now they be not able to forbear the same no more than a old drunkard can abide to be long sober without fall into a incurable weakness and evil constitution for their continual custom have make to they so to those that from their birth have be continual nourish upon poison and thing venomous wholesome meat be only poisonable thus have as I trust sufficient answer the most principal argument that be use in defence of this vile custom it rest only to inform you what sin and vanity you commit in the filthy abuse thereof first be you not guilty of sinful and shameful lust for lust may be as well in any of the sense as in feeling that although you be trouble with no disease but in perfect health yet can you neither be merry at a ordinary nor lascivious in the stew if you lack tobacco to provoke your appetite to any of those sort of recreation lust after it as the child of Israel do in the wilderness after quail second it be as you use or rather abuse it a branch of the sin of drunkenness which be the root of all sin for as the only delight that drunkard take in wine be in the strength of the taste and the force of the fume thereof that mount up to the brain for no drunkard love any weak or sweet drink so be not those I mean the strong heat and the fume the only quality that make tobacco so delectable to all the lover of it and as no man like strong heady drink the first day because fit but by custom be piece and piece allure while in the end a drunkard will have as great a thirst to be drink as a sober man to quench his thirst with a draught when he have need of it so be not this the very case of all the great taker of tobacco which therefore they themselves do attribute to a bewitch quality in it three be it not the great sin of all that you the people of all sort of this kingdom who be create and ordain by God to bestow both your person and good for the maintenance both of the honour and safety of your king and common wealth shall disable yourselves in both in your person have by this continual vile custom bring yourselves to this shameful imbecility that you be not able to ride or walk the journey of a jew sabbath but you must have a reekie coal bring you from the next poor house to kindle your tobacco with whereas he can be think able for any service in the war that can endure oftentimes the want of meat drink and sleep much more than must he endure the want of tobacco in the time of the many glorious and victorious battle fight by this nation there be no word of tobacco but now if it be time of war and that you be to make some sudden cavalcado upon your enemy if any of you shall seek leisure to stay behind his fellow for take of tobacco for my part I shall never be sorry for any evil chance that may befall he to take a custom in any thing that can be leave again be most harmful to the people of any land mollicy and delicacy be the wrack and overthrow first of the persian and next of the roman empire and this very custom of take tobacco whereof our present purpose be be even at this day account so effeminate among the indian themselves as in the market they will offer no price for a slave to be sell who they find to be a great tobacco taker now how you be by this custom disable in your good let the gentry of this land bear witness some of they bestow three some four hundred pound a year upon this precious stink which I be sure may be bestow upon many far better use I read indeed of a knavish courtier who for abuse the favour of the emperor Alexander Severus his master by take bribe to intercede for sundry person in his master ear for who he never once open his mouth be just choke with smoke with this doom but of so many smoke-buyer as be at this present in this kingdom I never read nor hear and for the vanity commit in this filthy custom be it not both great vanity and uncleanness that at the table a place of respect of cleanliness of modesty man shall not be ashamed to sit toss of tobacco pipe and puff of the smoke of tobacco one to another make the filthy smoke and stink thereof to exhale athwart the dish and infect the air when very often man that abhor it be at their repast sure smoke become a kitchen far better than a dine chamber and yet it make a kitchen also oftentimes in the inward part of man soil and infect they with a unctuous and oily kind of soot as have be find in some great tobacco taker that after their death be open and not only meat time but no other time nor action be exempt from the public use of this uncivil trick so as if the wife of deep list to contest with this nation for good manner their worst manner will in all reason be find at least not so dishonest as we be in this point the public use whereof at all time and in all place have now so far prevail as diverse man very sound both in judgement and complexion have be at last force to take it also without desire partly because they be ashamed to seem singular like the two philosopher that be force to duck themselves in that rain water and so become fool as well as the rest of the people and partly to be as one that be content to eat garlic which he do not love that he may not be trouble with the smell of it in the breath of his fellow and be it not a great vanity that a man can hearty welcome his friend now but straight they must be in hand with tobacco no it be become in place of a a point of good fellowship and he that will refuse to take a pipe of tobacco among his fellow though by his own election he will rather feel the savour of a sink be account peevish and no good company even as they do with tipple in the cold eastern country yea the mistress can in a more mannerly kind entertain her servant then by give he out of her fair hand a pipe of tobacco but herein be not only a great vanity but a great contempt of God good gift that the sweetness of man breath be a good gift of God shall be wilful corrupt by this stink smoke wherein I must confess it have too strong a virtue and so that which be a ornament of nature and can neither by any artifice be at the first acquire nor once lose be recover again shall be filthy corrupt with a incurable stink which vile quality be as direct contrary to that wrong opinion which be hold of the wholesomeness thereof as the venom of putrefaction be contrary to the virtue preservative moreover which be a great iniquity and against all humanity the husband shall not be ashamed to reduce thereby his delicate wholesome and clean complexioned wife to that extremity that either she must also corrupt her sweet breath therewith or else resolve to live in a perpetual stink torment have you not reason then to be ashamed and to forbear this filthy novelty so base ground so foolish receive and so gross mistake in the right use thereof in your abuse thereof sin against God harm yourselves both in person and good and rake also thereby the mark and note of vanity upon you by the custom thereof make yourselves to be wonder at by all foreign civil nation and by all stranger that come among you to be scorn and contemn a custom loathsome to the eye hateful to the nose harmful to the brain dangerous to the lung and in the black stink fume thereof near resemble the horrible stygian smoke of the pit that be bottomless a discourse of the manner of the discovery of the powder-treason join with the examination of some of the prisoner there be a time when no man ought to keep silence for it have ever be hold as a general rule and undoubted maxim in all well govern commonwealth whether Christian and so guide by the divine light of God word or ethnic and so lead by the glimmer twilight of nature yet howsoever their profession be upon this ground have they all agree that when either their religion their king or their country be in any extreme hazard no good countryman ought then to withhold either his tongue or his hand according to his call and faculty from aid to repel the injury repress the violence and avenge the guilt upon the author thereof but if ever any people have such a occasion minister unto they it be sure this people now nay this whole isle and all the rest belong to this great and glorious monarchy for if in any heathenish republic no private man can think his life more happy and glorious bestow then in the defence of any one of these three that be either or and that the endanger of any one of these will at once stir the whole body of the commonwealth not any more as divide member but as a solid and individual lump how much more ought we the true christian people that inhabit this unite and true happy isle under the wing of our gracious and religious monarch nay how infinite great cause have we to feel and ressent ourselves of the smart of that wound not only intend and execrate not consecrate for the utter extinguish of our true christian profession nor joint therewith only for the cut off of our head and father politic and that nothing may be want for make this sacrilegious parricide a pattern of mischief and a crime nay a mother or storehouse of all crime without example they shall have join the destruction of the body to the head so as shall all at one thunderclap have be send to heaven together the king our head the queen our fertile mother and those young and hopeful olive plant not they but we our reverend clergy our honourable nobility the faithful councillor the grave judge the great part of the worthy knight and gentry aswell as of the wise burgess the whole clerk of the crown counsel signet seal or of any other principal judgement seat all the learned lawyer together with a infinite number of the common people nay their furious rage shall not only have light upon reasonable and sensible creature without distinction either of degree sex or age but even the insensible stock and stone shall not have be free of their fury the hall of justice the house of parliament the church use for the coronation of our king the monument of our former prince the crown and other mark of royalty all the record aswell of parliament as of every particular man right with a great number of charter and such like shall all have be comprehend under that fearful chaos and so the earth as it be open shall have send forth of the bottom of the stygian lake such sulphure smoke furious flame and fearful thunder as shall have by their diabolical doomsday destroy and deface in the twinkle of a eye not only our present live prince and people but even our insensible monument reserve for future aage so as not only ourselves that be mortal but the immortal monument of our ancient prince and nobility that have be so precious preserve from age to age as the remain trophy of their eternal glory and have so long triumph over envious time shall now have be all consume together and so not only we but the memory of we and we shall have be thus extinguish in a instant the true horror therefore of this detestable device have stir i up to bethink myself wherein I may best discharge my conscience in a cause so general and common if it be to bring but one stone to the building or rather with the widow one mite to the common box but since to so hateful and unheard-of invention there can be no great enemy than the self the simple truth thereof be once public know and that there need no strong argument to bring such a plot in universal detestation than the certainty that so monstrous a thing can once be devise nay conclude upon wrought in in full readiness and within twelve hour of the execution my threefold zeal to those blessing whereof they will have so violent make we all widow have make i resolve to set down here the true narration of that monstrous and unnatural intend tragedy have better occasion by the mean of my service and continual attendance in court to know the truth thereof than other that peradventure have it only by relation at the three or four hand so that whereas those worse than Catiline think to have extirp we and our memory their infamous memory shall by these mean remain to the end of the world upon the one part and upon the other God great and merciful deliverance of his anoint and we all shall remain in never-dying record and God grant that it may be in marble table of thankfulness engrave in our heart while this land and whole monarchy flourish in a most happy and plentiful peace as well at home as abroad sustain and conduct by these two main pillar of all good government piety and justice no foreign grudge nor inward whisper of discontentment any way appear the king be upon his return from his hunt exercise at Royston upon occasion of the draw near of the parliament time which have be twice prorogue already partly in regard of the season of the year and partly of the term as the wind be ever still immediate before a storm and as the sun blenk often hot to foretell a follow shower so at that time of great calm do this secretly-hatched thunder begin to cast forth the first flash and flame lightning of the approach tempest for the Saturday of the week immediate precede the king return which be upon a Thursday be but ten day before the parliament the lord Mountegle son and heir to the lord morley a letter deliver to the lord Mountegle be in his own lodging ready to go to supper at sven of the clock at night one of his footman who he have send of a errand over the street be meet by a unknown man of a reasonable tall personage who deliver he a letter charge he to put it in my lord his master hand which my lord no soon receive but that have break it up and perceive the same to be of a unknown and somewhat unlegible hand and without either date or subscription do call one of his man unto he for help he to read it but no soon do he conceive the strange content thereof although he be somewhat perplex what construction to make of it as whether of a matter of consequence as indeed it be or whether some foolish devise Pasquil by some of his enemy to scar he from his attendance at the parliament yet do he as a most dutiful and loyal subject conclude not to conceal it what ever may come of it whereupon notwithstaud the lateness and darkness of the night in that season of the year he present repair to his majesty palace at Whitehall and there deliver the same to the earl of Salisbury his majesty principal secretary reveal to the earl of Salisbury whereupon the say earl of Salisbury have read the letter and hear the manner of the come of it to his hand do great encourage and commend my lord for his discretion tell he plain that whatsoever the purpose of the letter may prove hereafter yet do this accident put he in mind of diverse advertisement he have receive from beyond the sea wherewith he have acquaint aswell the king himself as diverse of his privy counsellor concern some business the papist be in both at home and abroad make preparation for some combination among they against this parliament time for enable they to deliver at that time to the king some petition for toleration of religion purpose of the papist for deliver a petition to his majesty to crave toleration of religion which shall be deliver in some such order and so well back as the king shall be loath to refuse their request like the sturdy beggar crave alm with one open hand but carry a stone in the other in case of refusal and therefore do the earl of Salisbury conclude with the lord Mountegle that he will in regard of the king absence impart the same letter to some more of his majesty council whereof my l. Mountegle like well only add this request by way of protestation that whatsoever the event hereof may prove it shall not be impute to he as proceed from too light and too sudden a apprehension that he deliver this letter be only move thereunto for demonstration of his ready devotion and care for preservation of his majesty and the State and thus do the earl of Salisbury present acquaint the lord chamberlain with the say letter the lord Chamberlain make privy to the letter by the earl of Salubury whereupon they two in presence of the lord Mountegle call to mind the former intelligence already mention which seem to have some relation with this letter the tender care which they ever carry to the preservation of his majesty person make they apprehend that some perilous attempt do thereby appear to be intend against the same which do the more near concern the say l. Chamberlain to have a care of in regard that it do belong to the charge of his office to oversee as well all place of assembly where his majesty be to repair as his highness own private house and therefore do the say two counsellor conclude that they shall join unto themselves three more of the council to wit the lord admiral the earl of Worcester and Northampton to be also particular acquaint with this accident who have all of they concur together to the re-examination of the content of the say letter they do conclude that how slight a matter it may at the first appear to be yet be it not absolute to be contemn in respect of the care which it behoove they to have of the preservation of his majesty person but yet resolve for two reason think meet by the councillor to acquaint the king with the letter first to acquaint the king himself with the same before they proceed to any further inquisition in the matter aswell for the expectation and experience they have of his majesty fortunate judgement in clear and solve of obscure riddle and doubtful mystery as also because the more time will in the mean while be give for the practice to ripen if any be whereby the discovery may be the more clear and evident and the ground of proceed thereupon more safe just and easy and so according to their determination do the say earl of Salisbury repair to the king in his gallery upon Friday be Athallow day in the afternoon upon alhallow day the earl of Sasuburie show the letter to the King which be the day after his majesty arrival and none but himself be present with his highness at that time where without any other speech or judgement give of the letter but only relate simple the form of the delivery thereof he present it to his majesty the content whereof follow my lord out of the love I bear to some of your friend I have a care of your preservation therefore I will advise you as you tender your life to devise some excuse to shift off your attendance at this parliament for God and man have concur to punish the wickedness of this Time and think not slight of this advertisement but retire yourself into your country where you may expect the event in safety for though there be no appearance of any stir yet I say they shall receive a terrible blow this parliament and yet they shall not see who burt they this counsel be not to be contemn because it may do you good and can do you no harm for the danger be pass so soon as you have burn the letter and I hope God will give you the grace to make good use of it to who holy protection I commend you the king no soon read the letter but after a little pause his majesty judgement of the letter and then read it over again he deliver his judgement of it in such sort as he think it be not to be contemn for that the style of it seem to be more quick and pithy then be usual to be in any Pasquil or libel the superfluity of idle brain but the earl of Salisbury perceive the king to apprehend it deepely than he look for know his nature tell he that he think by one sentence in it that it be like to be write by some fool or madman read to he this sentence in it for the danger be past as soon as you have burn the letter which he say be likely to be the say of a fool for if the danger be pass so soon as the letter be burn than the warning behoove to be of little avail when the burn of the letter may make the danger to be eschew but the king by the contrary consider the former sentence in the letter that they shall receive a terrible blow at this parliament and yet shall not see who hurt they join it to the sentence immediate follow already allege do thereupon conjecture that the danger mention shall be some sudden danger by blow up of powder for no other insurrection rebellion or whatsoever other private and desperate attempt can be commit or attempt in time of parliament and the author thereof unseen except only it be by a blow up of powder which may be perform by one base knave in a dark corner whereupon he be move to interpret and construe the latter sentence in the letter allege by the earl of Salisbury against all ordinary sense and construction in grammar as if by these word for the danger be past as soon as you have burn the letter shall be close understand the suddenty and quickness of the danger which shall be as quick perform and at a end as that paper shall be of blease up in the fire turn that word of as soon to the sense of as quick and therefore wish that before his go to the parliament his majesty opinion for search of the under room of the parliament house the under room of the parliament house may be well and narrow search but the earl of Salisbury wonder at this his majesty commentary which he know to be so far contrary to his ordinary and natural disposition who do rather ever sin upon the other side in not apprehend nor trust due advertisement of practice and peril when he be true inform of they whereby he have many time draw himself into many desperate danger and interpret right this extraordinary caution at this time to proceed from the vigilant care he have of the whole state more than of his own person which can not but have all perish together if this designment have succeed he think good to dissemble still unto the king that there have be any just cause of such apprehension and end the purpose with some merry jest upon this subject as his custom be take his leave for that time but though he seem so to neglect it to his majesty yet his customable and watchful care of the king and the state still boil within he and have with the bless virgin Marie lay up in his heart the king so strange judgement and construction of it he can not be at rest till he acquaint the foresay lord what have pass between the king and he in private whereupon they be all so earnest to renew again the memory of the same purpose to his majesty as it be agree that he shall the next day be Saturday repair to his highness which he do in the same privy gallery and renew the memory thereof the l. Chamberlain then be present with the King the determination to search the parliament house and the room under it at what time it be determine that the say lord Chamberlain shall according to his custom and office view all the parliament house both above and below and consider what likelihood or appearance of any such danger may possible be gather by the sight of they but yet as well for stay of idle rumour as for be the more able to discern any mystery the near that thing be in readiness his journey thither be ordain to be defer till the afternoon before the sit down of the parliament which be upon the Monday follow at what time he according to this conclusion go to the parliament house accompany with my lord Mountegle be in zeal to the king service earnest and curious to see the event of that accident whereof he have the fortune to be the first discoverer where have view all the low room wood and coale find by the lord chamberlain in the vault he find in the vault under the upper house great store and provision of billet faggot and coal and inquire of Whyneard keeper of the wardrobe to what use he have put those low room and cellar he tell he that Thomas Percy have hire both the house and part of the cellar or vault under the same and that the wood and coale therein be the say gentlemansowne provision whereupon the lord Chamberlain cast his eye aside perceive a fellow stand in a corner there call himself the say percy man and keeper of that house for he but indeed be Guido fawkes Guido fawkes bear the name of percy man the owner of that hand which shall have act that monstrous tragedy the lord Chamberlain look upon all thing with a heedful indeed yet in outward appearance with but a careless and racklesse eye as become so wise and diligent a minister he present address himself to the king in the say privy gallery wherein the presence of the lord treasurer the lord admiral the earl of Worcester Northampton and Salisbury the lord chamberlain report and judgement of what he have observe in the search he make his report what he have see and observe there note that Mountegle have tell he that he no soon hear Thomas Percy name to be the possessor of that house but consider both his backwardness in religion and the old dearness in friendship between himself and the say Percy he do great suspect the matter and that the letter shall come from he the say lord Chamberlain also tell that he do not wonder a little at the extraordinary great provision of wood and coal in that house where Thomas Percy have so seldom occasion to remain as likewise it give he in his mind that his man look like a very tall and desperate fellow this can not but increase the king former apprehension and jealousy whereupon he insist as before that the house be narrow to be search and that those billet and coal will be search to the bottom it be most suspicious that they be lay there only for cover of the powder of this same mind also be all the counsellor then present disputation about the manner of the further search but upon the fashion of make of the search be it long debate for upon the one side they be all so jealous of the king safety that they all agree that there can not be too much caution use for prevent his danger and yet upon the other part they be all extreme loath and dainty that in case this letter shall prove to be nothing but the evaporation of a idle brain then a curious search be make and nothing find shall not only turn to the general scandal of the king and the state as be so suspicious of every light and frivolous toy but likewise lay a ill favour imputation upon the earl of Northumberland one of his majesty great subject and counsellor this tho. Percy be his kinsman and most confident familiar and the rather be they curious upon this point know how far the king detest to be think suspicious or jealous of any of his good subject though of the mean degree and therefore though they all agree upon the main ground which be to provide for the security of the king person yet do they much differ in the circumstance by which this action may be best carry with least din and occasion of slander but the king himself still persist that there be diverse shrewd appearance and that a narrow search of those place can prejudge no man that be innocent he at last plain resolve they that either must all the part of those room be narrow search and no possibility of danger leave unexamined or else he and they all must resolve not to meddle in it at all but plain to go the next day to the parliament and leave the success to fortune which he believe they will be loath to take upon their conscience for in such a case as this agree that the search shall be under colour of seek for wardrobe stuff miss by Whynniard a half do be worse than no do at all whereupon it be at last conclude that nothing shall be leave unsearched in those house and yet for the better colour and stay of rumour in case nothing be find it be think meet that upon a pretence of whyneard miss some of the king stuff or hang which he have in keep all those room shall be narrow rip for they and to this purpose be sir Thomas knevet a gentleman of his majesty privy chamber employ be a justice of peace in Westminster and one of who ancient fidelity both the late queen and our now sovereign have have large proof who according to the trust commit unto he go about the midnight next after to the parliamenthouse accompany with such a small number as be fit for that errand but before his entry in the house fawkes find at midnight without the house find Thomas percy allege man stand without the door his clothes and boot on at so dead a time of the night he resolve to apprehend he as he do and thereafter go forward to the search of the house where after he have cause to be overturn some of the billet and coal he first find one of the small barrel of powder and after all the rest to the number of thirty six barrel great and small and thereafter search the fellow who he have take find three match and all other instrument fit for blow up the powder ready upon he which make he instant confess his own guiltiness declare also unto he that if he have happen to be within the house when he take he as he be immediate before at the end of his work he will not have fail to have blow he up house and all thus after sir Thomas have cause the wretch to be sure bind and well guard by the company he have bring with he he himself return back to the king palace and give warning of his success to the lord Chamberlain and earl of Salisbury who immediate warn the rest of the council that lie in the house as soon as they can get themselves ready come with their fellow counsellor to the king bedchamber be at that time near four of the clock in the morning and at the first entry of the king chamber door upon sir Thomas knevets return the council warn the lord Chamberlain be not any long able to conceal his joy for the prevent of so great a danger tell the king in a confuse haste that all be find and discover and the traitor in hand and fast bind then order be first take for send for the rest of the council that lie in the town the prisoner himself be bring into the house where in respect of the strangeness of the accident no man be stay from the sight or speak with he and within a while after the council do examine he who seem to put on a roman resolution do both to the council and to every other person that speak with he that day appear so constant and settle upon his ground as we all think we have find some new Mutius Scaevola bear in England for notwithstanding the horror of the fact the guilt of his conscience his sudden surprise the terror which shall have be strike in he by come into the presence of so grave a council and the restless and confuse question that every man all that day do vex he with yet be his countenance so far from be deject as he often smile in scornful manner not only avow the fact but repent only with the say Scaevola his fail in the execution thereof whereof he say the devil and not God be the discoverer answer quick to every man objection scoff at any idle question which be propound unto he and jest with such as he think have no authority to examine he all that day can the council get nothing out of he touch his complice refuse to answer to any such question which he think may discover the plot and lay all the blame upon himself whereunto he say he be move only for religion and conscience sake deny the king to be his lawful sovereign or the anoint of God in respect he be a heretic and give himself no other name than John johnson servant to Thomas Percy but the next morning be carry to the tower he do not there remain above two or three day be twice or thrice in that space reexamine and the rack only offer and show unto he when the mask of his roman fortitude do visible begin to wear and slide off his face and then do he begin to confess part of the truth and thereafter to open the whole matter as do appear by his deposition immediate follow the true copy of the declaration of GVIDO fawkes take in the presence of the counsellor who name be under write I confess that a practice in general be first break unto i against his majesty for relief of the catholic cause and not invent or propound by myself and this be first propound unto i about Easter last be twelve month beyond the sea in the low-countries of the archduke obeisance by Thomas winter who come thereupon with i into England and there we impart our purpose to three other gentleman more namely Robert Catesby Thomas Percy and John wright who all five consult together of the mean how to execute the same and take a vow among ourselves for secrecy Catesby propound to have it perform by gunpowder and by make a i under the upper house of parliament which place we make choice of the rather because religion have be unjust suppress there it be fit that justice and punishment shall be execute there this be resolve among we Thomas Percy hire a house at Westminster for that purpose near adjoin to the parliament house and there we begin to make our i about the 11. of December 1604. the five that first enter into the work be Thomas Percy robert catesby Thomas winter John Wright and myself and soon after we take another unto we Christopher Wright have swear he also and take the sacrament for secrecy when we come to the very foundation of the wall of the house which be about three yard thick and find it a matter of great difficulty we take unto we another gentleman Robert winter in like manner with oath and sacrament as aforesaid it be about Christmas when we bring our i unto the Wal and about Candlemas we have wrought the wall half through and while they be in work I stand as sentinel to descry any man that come near whereof I give they warning and so they cease until I give notice again to proceed all we seven lie in the house and have shoot and powder be resolve to die in that place before we shall yield or be take as they be work upon the wall they hear a rush in a cellar of remove of coal whereupon we fear we have be discover and they send i to go to the cellar who find that the coal be a sell and that the cellar be to be let view the commodity thereof for our purpose Percy go and hire the same for yearly rent we have before this provide and bring into the house twenty barrel of powder which we remove into the cellar and cover the same with billet and faggot which be provide for that purpose about easter the parliament be prorogue till October next we disperse ourselves and I retire into the low country by advice and direction of the rest aswell to acquaint Owen with the particular of the plot as also lest by my long stay I may have grow suspicious and so have come in question in the mean time Percy have the key of the cellar lay in more powder and wood into it I return about the begin of September next and then receive the key again of Percy we bring in more powder and billet to cover the same again and so I go for a time into the country till the 30. of October it be further resolve among we that the same day that this act shall have be perform some other of our confederate shall have surprise the person of the lady elizabeth the king elder daughter who be keep in Warwickshire at the lord haringtons house and present have proclaim her queen have a project of a proclamation ready for that purpose wherein we make no mention of alter of religion nor will have avow the deed to be we until we shall have have power enough to make our party good and then we will have avow both concern duke CHARLES the king second son we have sundry consultation how to seize on his person but because we find no mean how to compass it the duke be keep near London where we have not force enough we resolve to serve our turn with the lady elizabeth the name of other principal person that be make privy afterward to this horrible conspiracy Euerard Digby knight Ambrose Rookwood Francis tresham John Grant Robert keyes Commiss Nottingham Worcester Suffolk Devonshire Northampton Salisbury marre Dunbar popham edw. Cook William Waad and in regard that before this discourse can be ready to go to the press Thomas winter be apprehend and bring to the tower make a confession in substance agree with this former of fawkes only large in some circumstance I have think good to insert the same likewise in this place for the further clear of the matter and great benefit of the reader THOMAS winter confession take the 23 of November 1605. in the presence of the counsellor who name be under-written my most honourable lord not out of hope to obtain pardon for speak of my temporal part I may say the fault be great than can be forgive nor affect hereby the title of a good subject for I must redeem my country from as great a danger as I have hazard the bring of she into before I can purchase any such opinion only at your honour command I will brief set down my own accusation and how far I have proceed in this business which I shall the faithfullier do since I see such course be not please to almighty God and that all or the most material part have be already confess I remain with my brother in the country from Alhallontyde until the beginning of Lent in the year of our lord 1603. the first year of the king reign about which time master Catesby send thither entreat i to come to London where he and other my friend will be glad to see i I desire he to excuse i for I find myself not very well dispose and which have happen never to i before return the messenger without my company short I receive another letter in any wise to come at the second summons I present come up and find he with master John Wright at Lambeth where he break with i how necessary it be not to forsake our country for he know I have then a resolution to go over but to deliver she from the servitude in which she remain or at least to assist she with our uttermost endeavour I answer that I have often hazard my life upon far light term and now will not refuse any good occasion wherein I may do service to the catholic cause but for myself I know no mean probable to succeed he say that he have bethink he of a way at one instant to deliver we from all our bond and without any foreign help to replant again the catholic religion and with all tell i in a word it be to blow up the parliament house with gunpowder for say he in that place have they do we all the mischief and perchance God have desseign that place for their punishment I wonder at the strangeness of the conceit and tell he that true it be this strike at the root and will breed a confusion fit to beget new alteration but if it shall not take effect as most of this nature miscarry the scandal will be so great which catholic religion may hereby sustain as not only our enemy but our friend also will with good reason condemn we he tell i the nature of the disease require so sharp a remedy and ask i if I will give my consent I tell he yes in this or what else soever if he resolve upon it I will venture my life but I propose many difficulty as want of a house and of one to carry the i noise in the work and such like his answer be let we give a attempt and where it fail pass no further but first quoth he because we will leave no peaceable and quiet way untried you shall go over and inform the constable of the state of the catholic here in England entreat he to solicit his majesty at his come hither that the penal law may be recall and we admit into the rank of his other subject withal you may bring over some confident gentleman such as you shall understand best able for this business and name unto i master fawkes short after I pass the sea and find the constable at Bergen near Dunkirk where by help of master Owen I deliver my message who answer be that he have strict command from his master to do all good office for the catholic and for his own part he think himself bind in conscience so to do and that no good occasion shall be omit but speak to he nothing of this matter return to dunkirck with master Owen we have speech whether he think the constable will faithful help we or no. he say he believe nothing less and that they seek only their own end hold small account of catholic I tell he that there be many gentleman in England who will not forsake their country until they have try the uttermost and rather venture their life then forsake she in this misery and to add one more to our number as a fit man both for counsel and execution of whatsoever we shall resolve wish for master fawkes who I have hear good commendation of he tell i the gentleman deserve no less but be at Brussels and that if he come not as happy he may before my departure he will send he short after into England I go soon after to Ostend where sir William Stanley as then be not but come two day after I remain with he three or four day in which time I ask he if the catholic in England shall do any thing to help themselves whether he think the archduke will second they he answer no for all those part be so desirous of peace with England as they will endure no speech of other enterprise neither be it fit say he to set any project afoot now the peace be upon conclude I tell he there be no such resolution and so fall to discourse of other matter until I come to speak of master fawkes who company I wish over into England I ask of his sufficiency in the war and tell he we shall need such as he if occasion require he give very good commendation of he and as we be thus discourse and I ready to depart for Newport and take my leave of sir William master fawkes come into our company new return and salute we this be the gentleman say sir William that you wish for and so we embrace again I tell he some good friend of his wish his company in England and that if he please to come to dunkircke we will have further conference whither I be then go so take my leave of they both I depart about two day after come master fawkes to Dunkirck where I tell he that we be upon a resolution to do somewhat in England if the peace with Spain help we not but have as yet resolve upon nothing such or the like talk we pass at gravel where I lie for a wind and when it serve come both in one passage to Greenwich near which place we take a pair of oar and so come up to London and come to master Catesby who we find in his lodging he welcome we into England and ask i what news from the constable I tell he good word but I fear the deed will not answer this be the beginning of Easter term and about the midst of the same term whether send for by master Catesby or upon some business of his own up come master Thomas Percy the first word he speak after he come into our company be shall we always gentleman talk and never do any thing master Catesby take he aside and have speech about somewhat to be do so as first we may all take a oath of secrecy which we resolve within two or three day to do so as there we meet behind s. Clement master Catesby master Percy master Wright master Guy fawkes and myself and have upon a primer give each other the oath of secrecy in a chamber where no other body be we go after into the next room and hear mass and receive the bless sacrament upon the same then do master Catesby disclose to master Percy and I together with jacke Wright tell to master fawkes the business for which we take this oath which they both approve and then be m. Percy send to take the house which m. Catesby in my absence have learn do belong to one Ferris which with some difficulty in the end he obtain and become as Ferris before be tenant to Whynniard m. fawkes undergo the name of m. percy man call himself johnson because his face be the most unknown and receive the key of the house until we hear that the parliament be adjourn to the seven of February at which time we all depart several way into the country to meet again at the begin of Michaelmas term before this time also it be think convenient to have a house that may answer to m. percy where we may make provision of powder and wood for the mine which be there make ready shall in a night be convey by boat to the house by the parliament because we be loath to foil that with often go in and out there be none that we can devise so fit as Lambeth where master Catesby often lay and to be keeper thereof by m. catesbies choice we receive into the number key as a trusty honest man this be about a month before Michaelmas some fortnight after towards the beginning of the term m. fawkes and I come to m. Catesby at Morecrofts where we agree that now be time to begin and set thing in order for the mine so as master fawkes go to London and the next day send for i to come over to he when I come the cause be for that the scottish lord be appoint to sit in conference of the union in master percy house this hinder our beginning until a fortnight before Christmas by which time both master Percy and master Wright be come to London and we against their come have provide a good part of the powder so as we all five enter with tool fit to begin our work have provide ourselves of baked-meate the less to need send abroad we enter late in the night and be never see save only master percy man until Christmas Eve in which time we wrought under a little entry to the wall of the parliament house and underprop it as we go with wood while we be together we begin to fashion our business and discourse what we shall do after this deed be do the first question be how we may surprise the next heir the prince haply will be at the parliament with the king his father how shall we then be able to seize on the duke this burden master Percy undertake that by his acquaintance he with another gentleman will enter the chamber without suspicion and have some dozen other at several door to expect his come and two or three on horseback at the court gate to receive he he will undertake the blow be give until which he will attend in the duke chamber to carry he safe away for he suppose most of the court will be absent and such as be there not suspect or unprovided for any such matter for the lady ELIZABETH it be easy to surprise she in the country by draw friend together at a hunt near the lord harington and Ashbie m. catesbies house be not far off be a fit place for preparation the next be for money and horse which if we can provide in any reasonable measure have the heir apparent and the first knowledge by four or five day be odds sufficient then what lord we shall save from the parliament which be first agree in general as many as we can that be catholic or so dispose but after we descend to speak of particular next what foreign prince we shall acquaint with this before or join with after for this point we agree that first we can not enjoin prince to that secrecy nor oblige they by oath so to be secure of their promise beside we know not whether they will approve the project or dislike it and if they do allow thereof to prepare before may beget suspicion and not to provide until the business be act the same letter that carry news of the thing do may as well entreat their help and furtherance Spain be too slow in his preparation to hope any good from in the first extremity and France too near and too dangerous who with the ship of Holland we fear of all the world may make away with we but while we be in the middle of these discourse we hear that the parliament shall be anew adjourn until after Michaelmas upon which tiding we break off both discourse and work until after Christmas about Candlemas we bring over in a boat the powder which we have provide at Lambeth and lay it in m. percy house because we be willing to have all our danger in one place we wrought also another fortnight in the mine against the stone wall which be very hard to beat through at which time we call in Kit Wright and near to easter as we wrought the three time opportunity be give to hire the cellar in which we resolve to lay the powder and leave the mine now by reason that the charge of maintain we all so long together beside the number of several house which for several use have be hire and buy of powder etc. have lie heavy on m. Catesby alone to support it be necessary for he to call in some other to ease his charge and to that end desire leave that he with m. Percy and a three who they shall call may acquaint who they think fit and willing to the business for many say he may be content that I shall know who will not therefore that all the company shall be acquaint with their name to this we all agree after this master fawkes lay into the cellar which he have new take a thousand of billet and five hundred of faggot and with that cover the powder because we may have the house free to suffer any one to enter that will master Catesby wish we to consider whether it be not now necessary to send m. fawkes over both to absent himself for a time as also to acquaint sir William Stanley and m. Owen with this matter we agree that he shall provide that he give it they with the same oath that we have take it before to keep it secret from all the world the reason why we desire sir William Stanley shall be acquaint herewith be to have he with we so soon as he can and for m. Owen he may hold good correspondency after with foreign prince so m. fawkes depart about easter for Flanders and return the latter end of August he tell i that when he arrive at Brussels sir William Stanley be not return from Spain so as he utter the matter only to Owen who seem well please with the business but tell he that sure sir William will not be acquaint with any plot as have business now afoot in the court of England but he himself will be always ready to tell it he and send he away so soon as it be do about this time do m. Percy and m. Catesby meet at the bath where they agree that the company be yet but few m. Catesby shall have the other authority to call in who he think best by which authority he call in after sir Euerard Digby though at what time I know not and last of all m. Francis tresham the first promise as I hear m. Catesby say fifteen hundred pound the second two thousand pound m. Percy himself promise all that he can get of the earl of northumberland rent which be about four thousand pound and to provide many gallop horse to the number of ten mean while m. fawkes and myself alone buy some new powder as suspect the first to be dank and convey it into the cellar and set it in order as we resolve it shall stand then be the parliament anew prorogue until the five of November so as we all go down until some ten day before when m. Catesby come up with m. fawkes to a house by Enfield chase call white-webbe whither I come to they and m. Catesby will i to inquire whether the young prince come to the parliament I tell he that I hear that his grace think not to be there then must we have our horse say m. Catesby beyond the water and provision of more company to surprise the prince and leave the duke alone two day after be Sunday at night in come one to my chamber and tell i that a letter have be give to my l. Mountegle to this effect that he wish his lordship absence from the parliament because a blow will there be give which letter he present carry to my l. of Salisbury on the morrow I go to white-webbe and tell it m. Catesby assure he withal that the matter be disclose and wish he in any case to forsake his country he tell i he will see further as yet and resolve to send m. fawkes to try the uttermost protest if the part belong to himself he will try the same adventure on Wednesday master fawkes go and return at night of which we be very glad Thursday I come to London and Friday master Catesby master tresham and I meet at Barnet where we question how this letter shall be send to my l. Mountegle but can not conceive for master tresham forswear it who we only suspect on Saturday night I meet m. tresham again in Lincoln inn walk wherein he tell such speech that my lord of Salisbury shall use to the king as I give it lose the second time and repeat the same to m. Catesby who hereupon be resolve to be go but stay to have m. Percy come up who consent herein we want on Sunday m. Percy be deal with to that end will needs abide the uttermost trial this suspicion of all hand put we into such confusion as m. Catesby resolve to go down into the country the Monday that m. Percy go to Zion and m. Percy resolve to follow the same night or early the next morning about five of the clock be Tuesday come the young wright to my chamber and tell i that a nobleman call the l. Mountegle say arise and come along to Essex house for I be go to call up my l. of Northumberland say withal the matter be discover go back m. Wright quoth I and learn what you can about Essex gate short he return and say sure all be lose for lepton be get on horseback at Essex door and as he part he ask if their lordship will have any more with he and be answer no be ride fast up Fleetstreet as he can ride go you then quoth I to m. Percy for sure it be for he they seek and bid he be go I will stay and see the uttermost then I go to the court gate and find they strait guard so as no body can enter from thence I go down towards the parliament house and in the middle of Kings-street find the guard stand that will not let i pass and as I return I hear one say there be a treason discover in which the king and the lord shall have be blow up so then I be full satisfy that all be know and go to the stable where my gelding stand and ride into the country master Catesby have appoint our meeting at dunchurch but I can not overtake they until I come to my brother which be Wednesday night on Thursday we take the armour at my lord windsore and go that night to one Stephen Littleton house where the next day be Friday as I be early abroad to discover my man come to i and say that a heavy mischance have sever all the company for that m. Catesby m. Rookwood and m. grant be burn with gunpowder upon which sight the rest disperse master Littleton wish i to fly and so will he I tell he I will first see the body of my friend and bury he whatsoever befall i when I come I find m. Catesby reasonable well master Percy both the wright m. Rookwood and master Grant I ask they what they resolve to do they answer we mean here to die I say again I will take such part as they do about eleven of the clock come the company to beset the house and as I walk into the court I be shoot into the shoulder which lose i the use of my arm the next shot be the elder wright strike dead after he the young m. wright and four Ambrose Rookwood shot then say m. Catesby to i stand before the door they be to enter stand by i Tom and we will die together sir quoth I I have lose the use of my right arm and I fear that will cause i to be take so as we stand close together m. Catesby m. Percy and myself they two be shoot as far as I can guess with one bullet and then the company enter upon i hurt i in the belly with a pike and give i other wound until one come behind and catch hold of both my arm and so I remain you etc. Commiss Nottingham Suffolk Worcester Devonshire Northampton Salisbury marr Dunbar popham ed. coke w. Waad the name of those that be first in the treason and labour in the mine esquire robert catesby Robert winter gentleman Thomas Percy Thomas winter John Wright Christopher Wright Guido fawkes and Bates catesbyes man those that be make acquaint with it though not personal labour in the mine nor in the cellar Euerard Digby knight esquire Ambrose Rookewood Francis tresham John Grant gent. Robert keyes but here let we leave fawkes in a lodging fit for such a guest and take time to advise upon his conscience and turn ourselves to that part of the history which concern the fortune of the rest of his partaker in that abominable treason the news be no soon spread abroad that morning which be upon a Tuesday the 5. of November and the first day design for that session of parliament the news I say of this so strange and unlooked for accident be no soon divulge but some of those conspirator namely winter and the two brother of wright think it high time for they to hasten out of the town for Catesby be go the night before and percy at four of the clock in the morning the same day of the discovery and all of they hold their course with more haste then good speed to Warwick shire towards Coventry where the next day morning be Wednesday and about the same hour that fawk be take in Westminster one grant a gentleman have associate unto he some other of his opinion all violent papist and strong recusant come to a stable of one Benocke a rider of great horse the take of the horse out of the stable at Warwick by granut and other and have violent break up the same carry along with they all the great horse that be therein to the number of seven or eight belong to diverse nobleman and gentleman of that country who have put they into the rider hand to be make fit for their service and so both that company of they which flee out of London as also grant and his complice meet all together at dunchurch at sir Euerard Digby his lodging the Tuesday at night after the discovery of this treacherous attempt the which Digby have likewise for his part appoint a match of hunt to have be hunt the next day the hunt match appoint by sir Euerard Digby which be Wednesday though his mind be nimrod-like upon a far other manner of hunt more bend upon the blood of reasonable man than bruit beast this company and hellish society thus convene find their purpose discover their go into arm after the plot discover and their treachery prevent do resolve to run a desperate course and since they can not prevail by so private a blow to practice by a public rebellion either to attain to their intent or at least to save themselves in the throng of other and therefore gather all the company they can unto they and pretend the quarrel of religion have intercept such provision of armour horse and powder as the time can permit think by run up and down the country both to augment piece and piece their number dream to themselves that they have the virtue of a snowball which be little at the first and tumble down from a great hill grow to a great quantity by increase itself with the snow that it meet by the way and also that they begin first this brave show in one part of the country shall by their sympathy and example stir up and encourage the rest of their religion in other part of England to rise as they have do there but when they have gather their force to the great their number never above fourscore they come not to the number of fourscore and yet be they trouble all the hour of the day to keep and contain their own servant from steal from they who notwithstanding of all their care daily leave they be far inferior to gedeons host in number but far more in faith or justness of quarrel and so after that this catholic troop have wander a while through warwickshire to worcester-shire and from thence to the edge and border of stafford-shire this gallant arm band have not the honour at the last to be beat with a king lieutenant or extraordinary commissioner send down for the purpose their flight but only by the ordinary sheriff of worcester-shire be they all beat kill take and disperse wherein you have to note this follow circumstance so admirable and so lively display the greatness of God justice as it can not be conceal without betray in a manner the glory due to the almighty for the same although diverse of the king proclamation be post down after these traitor with all the speed possible declare the odiousness of that bloody attempt the necessity to have have Percy preserve alive if it have be possible and the assembly together of that rightly-damned crew now no more darned conspirator but open and avow rebel yet the far distance of the way which be above a hundred mile together with the extreme deepness thereof join also with the shortness of the day be the cause that the hearty and love affection of the king good subject in those part prevent the speed of his proclamation for upon the three day after the fly down of these rebel overtake at Holbeech in Stafford shire Stephen Littleton house which be upon the Friday next after the discovery of their plot they be most of they all surprise by the sheriff of worcester-shire at holbeach about the noon of the day and that in manner follow grant of who I have make mention before for take the great horse who have not all the prepare time stir from his own house till the next morning after the attempt shall have be put in execution he then lay his account without his host as the proverb be that their plot have without fail receive the day before their hoped-for success take or rather steal out those horse as I say before for enable he and so many of that soulless society that have still remain in the country near about he to make a sudden surprise upon the king elder daughter the lady ELIZABETH have her residence never by that place grant attempt to surprise the lady Elizabeth who they think to have use for the colour of their treacherous design his majesty her father her mother and male child be all destroy above and to this purpose also have that Nimrod Digby provide his hunt match against that same time that number of people be flock together upon the pretence thereof they may the easy have bring to pass the sudden surprise of her person now the violent take away of those horse long before day do seem to be so great a riot in the eye of the common-people that know of no great mystery and the bold attempt thereof do engender such a suspicion of some follow rebellion in the heart of the wise sort as both great and small begin to stir and arm themselves upon this unlooked-for accident among who sir fulke Grevill the elder knight as become one both so ancient in year and good reputation and by his office be deputy lieutenant of warwickshire though unable in his body yet by the zeal and true fervency of his mind do first apprehend this foresay riot to be nothing but the sparkle and sure of a follow rebellion whereupon both stout and honest he take order to get into his own hand the munition and armour of all such gentleman about he as be either absent from their own house or in doubtful guard and also send such direction to the town about he as thereupon do follow the strike of winter by a poor smith who have likewise be take by those vulgar people but that he be rescue by the rest of his company who perceive that the country before they have notice of they hasten away with loss in their own sight sixteen of their follower be take by the townsman and send present to the sheriff at Warwick and from thence to London but before twelve or sixteen hour past Catesby Percy the winter wright Rookewood and the rest bring then the assurance that their main plot be fail and bewray whereupon they have build the golden mountain of their glorious hope they than take their last desperate resolution to flock together in a troop and wander as they do for the reason aforetold but as upon the one part the zealous duty to their god and their sovereign be so deep imprint in the heart of all the mean and poor sort of the people although then know of no further mystery than such public misbehaviour as their own eye teach they as notwithstanding of their fair show and pretence of their catholic cause no creature man or woman through all the country will once so much as give they willing a cup of drink or any sort of comfort or support but with execration detest they so on the other part the sheriff of the shire wherethrough they wander convene their people with all speed possible hunt as hot after they as the evilness of the way and the unprovidedness of their people upon that sudden can permit they and so at last after sir Richard verney sheriff of warwickshire have careful and straight be in chase of they to the confine of his county part of the mean sort be also apprehend by he sir Richard walsh sheriff of worcester-shire do likewise dutiful and hot pursue they through his shire and have get sure trial of their take harbour at the house abovenamed he do send trumpeter and messenger to they command they in the king name to render unto he his majesty minister and know no more at that time of their guilt then be public visible do promise upon their dutiful and obedient render unto he to intercede at the king hand for the spare of their life who receive only from they this scornful answer they be better witness to themselves of their inward evil conscience that he have need of better assistance then of those few number that be with he before he can be able to command or control they but here fall the wondrous work of God justice the preparation to assault the house that while this message pass between the sheriff and they the shiriffes and his people zeal be just kindle and augment by their arrogant answer and so they prepare themselves to give a furious assault and the other party make themselves ready within the house to perform their promise by a defence as resolute it please God that in the mend of the fire in their chamber one small spark shall fly out and light among less than two pound weight of powder which be dry a little from the chimney which be thereby blow up so maim the face of some of the principal rebel and the hand and side of other of they blow up with it also a great bag full of powder which notwithstanding never take fire as they be not only disable and discourage hereby from any further resistance in respect Catesby himself Rookwood grant and diverse other of great account among they catesby who be the first inventor of this treason in general and of the manner of work the same by powder in special himself now first maim with the blow up of powder and next he and Percy both kill with one shot proceed from powder be thereby make unable for defence but also wonderful strike with amazement in their guilty conscience call to memory how God have just punish they with that same instrument which they shall have use for the effectuate of so great a sin according to the old latin say as they present see the wonderful power of God justice upon guilty conscience do all fall down upon their knee pray GOD to pardon they for their bloody enterprise and thereafter give over any further debate open the gate suffer the sheriff people to rush in furious among they and desperate seek their own present destruction the three special of they join back together catesby Percy and winter whereof two with one shot catesby and Percy be slay and the three winter take and save alive and thus these resolute and high aspire catholic who dream of no less than the destruction of king and kingdom and promise to themselves no low estate than the government of great and ancient monarchy be miserable defeat and quite overthrow in a instant fall in the pit which they have prepare for other and so fulfil that sentence which his majesty do in a manner prophesy of they in his oration to the parliament some present slay other deadly wound strip of their clothes leave lie miserable naked and so die rather of cold then of the danger of their wound and the rest that either be whole or but light hurt take and lead prisoner by the sheriff the ordinary minister of justice to the gaol the ordinary place even of the base malefactor where they remain till their send up to London be meet with a huge confluence of people of all sort desirous to see they as the rare sort of monster fool to laugh at they woman and child to wonder all the common people to gaze the wise sort to satisfy their curiosity in see the outward case of so unheard of a villainy and general all sort of people to satiate and fill their eye with the sight of they who in their heart they so far admire and detest serve so for a fearful and public spectacle of God fierce wrath and just indignation what hereafter will be do with they be to be leave to the justice of his majesty and the state which as no good subject needs to doubt will be perform in the own due time by a public and a exemplary punishment so have we all that be faithful and humble subject great cause to pray earnest to the almighty that it will please he who have the heart of all prince in his hand to put it in his majesty heart to make such a conclusion of this tragedy to the traitor but tragicomedy to the king and all his true subject as thereby the glory of God and his true religion may be advance the future security of the king and his estate procure and provide for all hollow and unhonest heart discover and prevent and this horrible attempt lack due epithere to be so just avenge that where they think by one catholic indeed and universal blow to accomplish the wish of that roman tyrant who wish all the body in Rome to have but one neck and so by the violent force of powder to break up as with a pettard our triple lock peaceful gate of janus which God be thank they can not compass by any other mean they may just be so recompense for their true viperous intend parricide as Aeneas Syl^^^^ do notable write concern the murder of k. james the first of Scotland and the follow punishment of the traitor whereof himself be a eye witness hist Europa cap. 46. as the shame and infamy that otherwise will light upon this whole nation for have unfortunate hatch such cockatrice egg may be repair by the execution of famous and honourable justice upon the offender and so the kingdom purge of they may hereafter perpetual flourish in peace and prosperity by the happy conjunction of the heart of all honest and true subject with their just and religious sovereign and thus whereas they think to have efface our memory the memory of they shall remain but to their perpetual infamy and we as I say in the beginning shall with all thankfulness eternal preserve the memory of so great a benefit to which let every good subject say amen or a apology for the oath of allegiance against the two of pope Paulus QVINTUS and the late letter of cardinal Bellarmine to g. blackwel the archpriest what a monstrous rare nay never heardof treacherous attempt be plot within these few year here in England for the destruction of i my bed-fellow and our posterity the whole house of parliament and a great number of good subject of all sort and degree be so famous already through the whole world by the infamy thereof as it be needless to be repeat or publish any more the horror of the sin itself do so loud proclaim it for if those * * gen. 4.10 cry sin whereof mention be make in the scripture have that epithet give they for their public infamy and for procure as it be with a loud cry from heaven a just vengeance and recompense and yet those sin be both old and too common neither the world nor any one country be ever at any time clean void of they if those sin I say be say in the scripture to cry so loud what then must this sin do plot without cause infinite in cruelty and singular from all example what proceed hereupon be likewise notorious to the whole world our justice only take hold upon the offender and that in as honourable and public a form of trial as ever be use in this kingdom 2. for although the only reason they give for plot so heinous a attempt be the zeal they carry to the romish religion yet be never any other of that profession the worse use for that cause as by our gracious proclamation immediate after the discovery of the say fact do plain appear only at the next sit down again of the parliament there be law make set down some such order as be think fit for prevent the like mischief in time to come among which a form of oath be frame to be take by my subject whereby they shall make a clear profession of their resolution faithful to persist in their obedience unto i according to their natural allegiance to the end that I may hereby make a separation not only between all my good subject in general and unfaithful traitor that intend to withdraw themselves from my obedience but special to make a separation between so many of my subject who although they be otherwise popish affect yet retain in their heart the print of their natural duty to their sovereign and those who be carry away with the like fanatical zeal that the powder-traitor be can not contain themselves within the bound of their natural allegiance but think diversity of religion a safe pretext for all kind of treason and rebellion against their sovereign which godly and wise intent God do bless with success according for very many of my subject that be popish affect aswell priest as layick do free take the same oath whereby they both give i occasion to think the better of their fidelity and likewise free themselves of that heavy slander that although they be fellow professor of one religion with the
