that he have build and set up before with a other discover withal the reluctation and privy check of his own conscience there yet remain one objection the knot whereof the l. cardinal in a manner sweat to untie his word be these page 84. the champion for the negative fly to the analogy of other proceed and practice in the church they affirm that private person master or owner of good and possession among the common people be not deprive of their good for heresy and consequent that prince much more shall not for the same crime be deprive of their estate for answer to this reason he bring in the defendant of deposition speak after this manner in the kingdom of France the strict execution of law decree in court against heretic be favourable suspend and stop for the preservation of peace and public tranquillity he say elsewhere connivance be use towards these heretic inregard of their multitude because a notable part of the french nation and state be make all of heretic I suppose that out of special charity he will have those heretic of his own make forewarn what courteous use and entreaty they be to expect when he affirm that execution of the law be but suspend for indeed suspension hold but for a time but in a cause of that nature and importance I dare promise myself that my most honour brother the king of France will make use of other counsel will rather seek the amity of his neighbour prince and the peace of his kingdom will bear in mind the great and faithful service of those who in matter of religion dissent from his majesty as of the only man that have preserve and save the crown for the king his father of most glorious memory I be persuade my brother of France will believe that his liege people pretend by the l. cardinal to be heretic be not half so bad as my roman catholic subject who by secret practice undermine my life serve a foreign sovereign be discharge by his bull of their obedience due to i their natural sovereign be bind by the maxim and rule publish and maintain in favour of the pope before this full and famous assembly of the estate at Paris if the say maxim be of any weight and authority to hold i for no lawful king be there teach and instruct that Paul commandemement concern subjection unto the high power adverse to their profess religion be only a provisional precept frame to the time and watch for the opportunity to shake off the yoke all which notwithstanding I deal with such romane-catholikes by the rule and way of princely clemency their heinous and pernicious error in effect no less than the capital crime of high treason I use to call some disease or distemper of the mind last of all I believe my say brother of France will set down in his table as in record how little he stand engage to the l. cardinal in this behalf for those of the reform religion profess and proclaim that next under God they owe their preservation and safety to the wisdom and benignity of their king but now come the cardinal and he seek to steal this persuasion out of their heart he tell they in open parliament and without any go about bush that all their welfare and security stand in their multitude and in the fear which other conceive to trouble the state by the strict execution of law against heretic he add moreover note by the way that here the church of Rome be call a sect. that in case a three sect shall peep out and grow up in France the professor thereof shall suffer confiscation of their good with loss of life itself as have be practise at Geneva against and in England against arian my answer be this that punishment for heretic due and according to law convict be set down by decree of the civil magistrate bear rule in the country where the say heretic inhabit and not by any ordinance of the pope I say withal the l. cardinal have no reason to match and parallel the reform church with and the arian for those heretic be powerful convict by God word and lawful condemn by the ancient general council where they be permit and admit to plead their own cause in person but as for the truth profess by i and those of the reform religion it be never yet hiss out of the school nor cast out of any council like some parliament bill where both side have be hear with like indifferency yea what council soever have be offer unto we in these latter time it have be propose with certain presupposition as that his holiness be a party in the cause and consequent to come under judgement as it be to the bar upon his trial shall be the judge of assize with commission of oyer and determiner it shall be celebrate in a city of no safe access without safe conduct or convoy to come or go at pleasure and without danger it shall be assemble of such person with free suffrage and voice as uphold this rule which they have already put in practice against John hus and Hierome of prague that faith give and oath take to a heretic must not be observe now then to resume our former matter if the pope hitherto have never presume for pretend heresy to confiscate by sentence either the land or the good of private person or common people of the french nation wherefore shall he dare to dispossess king of their royal throne wherefore take he more upon he over king then over private person wherefore shall the sacred head of king be more churlish uncivil and rigorous handle than the hood of the mean people here the l. cardinal in stead of a direct answer break out of the list allege clean from the purpose example of heretic punish not by the pope but by the civil magistrate of the country but Bellarmine speak to the point with a more free and open heart he be absolute and resolute in this opinion that his holiness have plenary power to dispose all temporal estate and matter in the whole world I be confident say Bellarmine and I speak it with assurance Contr. Barclaium cap. 27. that our lord jesus Christ in the day of his mortality have power to dispose of all temporal thing yea to strip sovereign king and absolute lord of their kingdom and seignory and without all doubt have grant and leave even the same power unto his vicar to make use thereof whensoever he shall think it necessary for the salvation of soul and so his lordship speak without exception of any thing at all for who do not know that jesus Christ have power to dispose no less of private man possession then of whole realm and kingdom at his pleasure if it have be his pleasure to display the ensign of his power the same fullness of power be likewise in the pope in good time belike his holiness be the sole heir of Christ in whole and in part Sess. 9 the last Lateran council fine a laic that speak blasphemy for the first offence if he be a gentleman at 25. ducat and at 50. for the second it presuppose and take it for grant that the church may rifle and ransack the purse of private man and cast lot for their good the council of Trent dig as deep for the same vein of gold and silver it ordain that emperor king duke prince Sess. 25. cap. 19 and lord of city castle and territory hold of the church in case they shall assign any place within their limit or liberty for the duel between two christian shall be deprive of the say city castle or place where such duel shall be perform they hold the say place of the church by any kind of tenure that all other estate hold in fee where the like offence shall be commit shall forthwith fall and become forfeit to their immediate and next lord that all good possession and estate as well of the combatant themselves as of their second shall be confiscate this council do necessary presuppose it lie in the hand and power of the church to dispose of all the land and estate hold in fee throughout all Christendom because the church forsooth can take from one and give unto a other all estate hold in fee whatsoever as well such as hold of the church as of secular lord and to make ordinance for the confiscation of all private person good by this canon the kingdom of Naples have need to look well unto itself for one duel it may fall into the exchequer of the roman church because that kingdom pay a relief to the church as a royalty or seignory that hold in fee of the say church and in France there be not one lordship not one manor not one farm which the pope by this mean can shift over to a new Lord his lordship therefore have carry himself and the cause much better if in stead of seek such idle shift he have by a more large assertion maintain the pope power to dispose of private man possession with no less right and authority then of kingdom for what colour of reason can be give for make the pope lord of the whole and not of the part for make he lord of the forest in gross and not of the tree in parcel for make he lord of the whole house and not of the parlour or the dine chamber his lordship allege yet a other reason but of no better weight between the power of private owner over their good and the power of king over their estate there be no little difference for the good of private person be ordain for their owner and prince for the benefit of their commonwealth hear i now answer if this cardinal-reason have any force to infer that a king may lawful be deprive of his kingdom for heresy but a private person can for the same crime be turn out of his mansion house than it shall follow by the same reason that a father for the same cause may be deprive of all power over his child but a private owner can be deprive of his good in the like case because good be ordain for the benefit and comfort of their owner but father be ordain for the good and benefit of their child but most certain it be that king represent the image of God in earth and god place have a better and close seat in their chair of estate than any private person have in the saddle of their inheritance and patrimony which be daily see for sleight cause to flit and to fall into the hand of new lord whereas a prince be the head can be loose in the proper joint nor dismount like a cannon when the carriage thereof be unlock without a sore shake and a most grievous dislocation of all the member yea without subvert the whole body of the state whereby private person without number be enwrap together in the same ruin even as the low shrub and other brush-wood be crush in piece altogether by the fall of a great oak but suppose his lordship reason be somewhat ponderous and solid withal yet a king which will not be forget be endow not only with the kingdom but also with the ancient Desmenes and crowne-land for which none can be so simple to say the king be ordain and create king which nevertheless he lose when he lose his crown admit again this reason be of some pith to make mighty king more easy deposeable then private person from their patrimony yet all this make nothing for the derive and fetch of deposition from the pope consistory what he never confer by what right or power can he claim to take away but see here no doubt a sharp and subtle difference put by the l. cardinal between a kingdom and the good of private person good as his lordship say be without life they can be constrain by no force by no example by no inducement of their owner to lose eternal life subject by their prince may now I be of the contrary belief that a heretical owner or master of a family have great power and mean withal to seduce his own servant and child than a prince have to pervert his own subject and yet for the contagion of heresy and for corrupt religion child be not remove from their parent nor servant be take away from their master history abound with example of most flourish church under a prince of contrary religion and if thing without life or soul be with less danger leave in a heretic hand why then shall not a heretical king with more facility and less danger keep his crown his royal charge his land his custom his impost and c for will any man except he be out of his wit affirm these thing to have any life or soul or why shall it be count folly to leave a sword in the hand of a mad bedlam be not a sword also without life and soul for my part I shall rather be of this mind that possession of thing without reason be more dangerous and pernicious in the hand of a evil master than the possession of thing endue with life and reason for thing without life lack both reason and judgement how to exempt and free themselves from be instrument in evil and wicked action from be employ to ungodly and abominable use I will not deny that a heretical prince be a plague a pernicious and mortal sickness to the soul of his subject but a breach make by one mischief must not be fill up with a great inconvenience a error must not be shock and shoulder with disloyalty nor heresy with perjury nor impiety with sedition and arm rebellion against GOD and the King GOD who use to try and to school his church will never forsake his church nor have need to protect his church by any proditorious and prodigious practice of perfidious christian for he make his church to be like the burn bush in the midst of the fire and flame of persecution he will provide that she shall not be consume because he stand in the midst of his church and suppose there may be some just cause for the french to play the rebel against their king yet will it not follow that such rebellious motion be to be raise by the bellows of the roman bishop to who pastoral charge and office it be nothing proper to intermeddle in the civil affair of foreign kingdom here be the sum and substance of the l. cardinal whole discourse touch his pretence of the second inconvenience which discourse he have close with a impossible for he to warrant his own life who be not able to warrant his own kingdom let a cat be throw from a high roof to the bottom of a cellour or vault she light on her foot and run away without take any harm a king be not like a cat howsoever a cat may look upon a king he can fall from the lofty pinnacle of royalty to light on his foot upon the hard pavement of a private state without crush in foreign country or else have be condemn like captive to perpetual imprisonment at home a thousand-fold worse and more lamentable than death itself Dionysius the tyrant of Syracuse from a great king their grave and tomb the cardinal himself seem to take some notice hereof the church as he speak abhor sudden and unprepensed murder above there pag. 95. do not his lordship in this phrase of speech acknowledge that murder commit by open force be not so much disavow or disclaim by the church a little after he speak not in the tooth as before but with full and open mouth that he do not dislike a king once depose by the pope shall be pursue with open war whereupon it follow that in war the king may be lawful slay no doubt a remarkable degree of his lordship clemency a king shall be better entreat and more mild deal withal if he be slay by the shot of a harquebuse or caleever in the field then if he be stab by the stroke or thrust of a knife in his chamber or if at a siege of some city he be blow up with a i then by a my make and a train of gunpowder lay under his palace or parliament house in time of peace his reason forsooth because in sudden murder oftentimes the soul and the body perish both together o singular bounty and rare clemency provoker instigator strong puffer and blower of parricide in merciful compassion of the soul become unmerciful and shameful murderer of the body this device may well claim and challenge kindred of Mariana the jesuite invention for he like not at any hand the poison of a tyrant by his meat or drink for fear lest he take the poison with flight out of the body may be innocent o hellhound o diabolical wretch o infernal monster do they only suspect and imagine that either in king there be any remainder of kingly courage or in their who the lord have call his anoint king the breathe image of God upon earth king that with a wry or frown look be able to crush these earthworm in piece how long shall they suffer this viperous brood we with a like manifest jest and notable trifle first distinguish between tyrant by administration and tyrant by usurpation then show that he by no mean do approve those profane and heathenish law whereby secret practice and conspiracy against a tyrant by administration be permit his reason because after deposition there be a certain habitude to royal dignity and as it be a kind of politic be at length reinvest and endow again with lawful use of royal dignity and with lawful administration of the kingdom be it possible that his lordship can speak and utter these word according to the himself out of deadly danger so far he be from hold or retain any remainder of dignity or politic impression that on the contrary he fall into great contempt and misery then if he have be a very peasant by birth and have never hold or govern the stern of royal estate what fowl be more beautiful than the peacock let she be plume and bereave of her feather what owl what jackdaw more ridiculous more without all pleasant fashion the homely souter the infamous catchpol the base tinker the rude artificer the pack-horse-porter then live in Rome with liberty when Valentinian be detain captive by Saporas the persian king be more happy than that roman emperor and in case the l. cardinal himself shall be so happy I shall say so unfortunate to be strip of all his dignity and ecclesiastical promotion will it not redound to his lordship wonderful consolation that in his great extremity in the low of his bareness and nakedness he still retain a certain habitual right and character of a cardinal whereby to recover the loss of his former dignity and honour when he behold these print and impression of his foresay honour will it not make he the more willing and glad to forsake the back of his venerable mule to use his cardinal foot-cloth no long but ever after like a cardinal in print and character to walk on foot but let we examine his lordship consolation of king thrust out of their kingdom by the pope for heresy the obstacle as the l. cardinal speak be take away that be to say when the king shall be reform th^^ habitual right and character yet inherent in the person of a king restore he to the lawful administration of his kingdom I take this to be but a cold comfort for here his lordship do only presuppose and not prove that after a king be thrust out of his throne when he shall repent and turn true roman catholic the other by who he have be cast out and by force disseise will recall he to the royal seat and faithful settle he again in his ancient right as one that rejoice for the recovery of such a lose sheep but I shall rather fear the new king will press and stand upon other term as a term of year for a trial whether the repentance of the king displace be true and sound to the core or counterfeit dissemble and paint holiness for the word the sorrowful and heavy look the sad and formal gesture of man pretend repentance be not always to be take to be politic character of regal majesty alas good king in how hard in how miserable a state do they stand once depose and ever bar of repentance as if the escape and error of king be all sin against the stand pure catholic the regal seat be empty who shall succeed in the depose king place shall a stranger be prefer by the pope that be to do the innocent son egregious and notorious wrong shall the son himself that be a more injurious part in the son against his father for if the son be touch with any fear of God or move with any reverence towards his father he will diligent and serious take heed by this doctrine shall imbrue his hand in his own father blood so soon as he shall be depose by the pope a matter so much the near and more deep to be apprehend because the say most outrageous book fly like a furious mastiff direct at my throat and withal instil such precept into the tender disposition of my son as if hereafter he shall become a roman catholic so soon as the pope shall give i the lift out of my throne shall bind he forthwith to make effusion of his own father blood such be the religion of these reverend father the pillar of the pontificial monarchy in comparison of who religion and holiness all the impiety that ever be among the infidel and all the barbarous cruelty that ever be among the cannibal may pass henceforth in the christian world for pure clemency and pass their understanding all these thing be nothing in a manner if we compare they with the last clause which be the close and as it be the upshot of his lordship discourse for therein he labour to persuade concern this article frame to bridle the pope tyrannical power over king if it shall receive gracious entertainment and general approbation that it will breed great danger and work effect of pernicious consequence unto king the reason because it will prove a introduction to schism and schism will stir up civil war contempt of king distemper inclination and motion to entrap their life and which be worst of all the fierce wrath of God inflict all sort of calamity a admirable paradox and able to strike man stone-blind that his holiness must have power to depose king for the better security and safeguard of their life that when their crown be make subject unto another will and pleasure than they be come to the high altitude and elevation of honour that for the only warrant of their life their supreme and absolute greatness must be depress that for the long keep of their crown another must pluck the crown from their head as if it shall be say will they not be strip naked by another the best way be for themselves to untruss for themselves to put off all and to go naked of their own accord will they keep their sovereignty in safety for ever the best way be to let another have their sovereign authority and supreme estate in his power but I have be ever of this mind that when my good be at no man command or dispose but my own than they be true and certain i own it may be this error be grow upon i and other prince for lack of brain whereupon it may be fear or at least conjecture the pope mean to shave our crown and thrust we into some cloister there to hold rank in the brotherhood of good king childerie forasmuch then as my dull capacity do not serve i to reach or comprehend the pith of this admirable reason I have think good to seek and to use the instruction of old and learned experience which teach no such matter by name that civil war and fearful perturbation of state in any nation of the world have at any time grow from this faithful credulity of subject that pope in right have no power to wrest and lift king out of their dignity and possession on the other side by establish the contrary maxim to yoke and hamper the people with pontificial tyranny what rebellious trouble and stir what extreme desolation have England be force to fear and feel in the reign of my predecessor Henry 2 John and Henry 3 these be the maxim and principle which under the emperor Henry 4 and frederic the i. make all Europe flow with channel and stream of blood like a river with water while the saracen by their incursion and victory overflow and in a manner drown the honour of the christian name in the east these be the maxim and principle which make way for the war of the last league into France by which the very bowel of that most famous and flourish kingdom be set on such a combustion that France herself be bring within two finger breadth of bondage to another nation and the death of her two last king most villenous and traitorous accomplish the l. cardinal then give these diabolical maxim for mean to secure the life and estate of king speak as if he will give man counsel to dry themselves in the river when they come as wet as a water spaniel the strength of his arm and the deep skill of his head in fortification at last he leave his lofty scaffold and fall to work near the ground with more easy tool of humble prayer and gentle exhortation the sum of the whole be this he adiure his auditor never to forge remedy never so to provide for the temporal safety of king as thereby to work their final fall from eternal salvation never to make any rent or rapture in the unity of the church in this corrupt age infect with pestilent heresy which already have make so great a breach in the wall of France will no doubt double their strength by the dissension division and schism of catholic if this infectious plague shall still increase and grow to a carbuncle it can by no mean poison religion without bring king to their wind sheet and woeful hearse the first ruler of that stone of offence aim at no other mark then to make a ignominious and lamentable rent in the church he think the deputy of the three estate have neither head nor over and over again with reiterate extraction and calcination it may be conjecture that all his lordship labour and cost be long since evaporate and vanish in the air howbeit as well the friendly conference of a king for I will not call it a contention as also the dignity excellency and importance of the matter long since deserve and as long since require the publish of some or other answer life and crown that stand centinel and walk the round for the preservation of his princely diadem when all other have no more touch no more feel thereof then so many stone and what need the deputy for of court all tend to insinuate and suggest the introduction of the same remedy suggestion be needless from abroad when the mischief be feel at home it seem to i that his lordship in smooth and tickle towards their king for the preservation of his life and honour if the remedy be not beat into their head by those of the religion repute heretic touch myself range by his lordship in the same rank with sower of dissension I take my god to witness and my own conscience that I never dream of any such unchristian project it have be hitherto my ordinary course to follow honest counsel and to walk in open way I never wont myself to hole and corner to crafty shift but evermore to plain and open design I need not hide my intention for fear of any mortal man that puff breath of life out of his nostril nor in any sort do I purpose to set julian the before my eye as a pattern for i to follow julian of a christian become a pagan I profess the same faith of Christ still which I have ever profess julian go about his design with crafty conveyance I never with any of his captious and cunning sleight julian force his subject to infidelity against I Christ I labour to induce my subject unto such term of loyalty towards myself as jesus Christ have prescribe and teach in his word but how far I differ from julian it be to be see more at large in my answer to Bellarmine epistle write to blackwell from whence the lord cardinal borrow this example it may well have beseem his lordship to borrow likewise my answer from the same place now as it move i nothing at all to be draw by his lordship into suspicion of this nature and quality so by the praise that he rock i withal I will never be lull asleep to commend a man for his knowledge and withal to take from he the fear of God be lady wisdom call to be of her train in the 9 proverb be moral virtue and human science which then become pernicious when they run away from their sovereign lady-mistris and put over themselves in service to the devil what difference be between two man both alike want the knowledge of God the one furnish with art and civil virtue the other brutish barbarous and of a deform life or of profane manner what be the difference between these two I make this the only difference the first go to hell with a better grace and fall into perdition with more facility than the second but he become exceed wicked even threefold and fourfold abominable if he be his treasure and stock of civil virtue in persecute the church of CHRIST and if that may be lay in his dish which be cast in Caesar tooth that in contest against all praise which grace i with petty accessory but rob i of the principal that one thing necessary namely the fear and knowledge of my GOD unto who majesty alone I have devote my sceptre my sword my pen my whole industry my whole self with all that be i in whole and in part I do it I do it in all humble acknowledgement of his unspeakable mercy and favour who have vouchsafe to that without shameful calumniate of my person it shall not rest in the tip of any tongue to touch my life with just reprehension or blame nor be I so privy to i own guiltiness as to think my state so the lord Paramount do fall from the property of his fee this reproach of the l. cardinal be second with a other of Bellarmine his brother cardinal that Ireland be give to the king of England by the have pass sundry bickerment about collate of ecclesiastical dignity John the son after his father death renew undertake and pursue the same quarrel drive certain english bishop out of the kingdom for defend the pope insolent usurpation upon his royal prerogative and regal right show such princely courage and resolution in those time when all that stand and suffer for the pope temporal pretension against king be enroll martyr or confessor the pope take the matter in foul scorn and great indignation shut the king by his excommunicatory bull out of the church stir up his baron for other cause the king heavy friend to rise in arm give the kingdom of England like a masterless man turn over to a new master to Philippus Augustus king of France bind Philip to make a conquest of England by the sword or else no bargain or else no gift promise Philip in recompense of his travel and royal expense in that conquest full absolution and a general pardon at large for all his sin to be short cuts king John out so much work and make he keep so many iron in the fire for his work that he have none other way none other mean to pacify the pope high displeasure to correct or qualify the malignity of the pope choleric humour by who he be then so entangle in the pope toil but by yield himself to become the pope vassal and his kingdom feudatary or to hold by fealty of the papal See by this mean his crown be make tributary all his people liable to payment of tax by the poll for a certain yearly tribute and he be bless with a pardon for all his sin whether king John be move to do this dishonourable act upon any devotion or inflame with any zeal of religion or enforce by the unresistable weapon of necessity who can be so blind that he do not well see and clear perceive for to purchase his own freedom from this bondage to the pope what can he be unwilling to do that be willing to bring his kingdom under the yoke of Amirales Murmelinus a mahometan prince than king of Granado and Barbaria the pope after that send a legate into England the king now the pope vassal and hold his crown of the pope like a man that hold his land of another by knight service or by homage and fealty do fair homage for his crown to the pope legate and lay down at his foot a great mass of the pure gold in coin the reverend legate in token of his master sovereignty with more than usual pride fall to kick and spurn the treasure no doubt with a pair of most holy foot not only so but likewise at solemn feast be easy entreat to take the king chair of estate here I will fain know the lord cardinal opinion whether these action of the pope be just or unjust lawful or unlawful according to right or against all right and reason if he will say against right it be then clear that against right his lordship have make way to this example if according to right let he then make it know from whence or from who this power be derive and convey to the pope whereby he make himself sovereign lord of temporalty in that kingdom where neither he nor any of his predecessor ever pretend any right or lay any claim to temporal matter before be such prank to be play by the pontificial bishop be this a act of holiness to set a kingdom on fire by the flame brand of sedition to dismember and quarter a kingdom with intestine war only to this end that a king once reduce to the low degree of misery may be lift by his holiness out of his royal prerogative the very soul and life of his royal estate when begin this papal power in what age begin the pope to practice this power what! have the ancient canon for the scripture in this question bear no pawme have the canon of the ancient church impose any such satisfaction upon a sinner that of a sovereign and free king he shall become vassal to his ghostly father that he shall make himself together with all his people and subject tributary to a bishop that shall rifle a whole nation of their coin that shall receive homage of a king and make a king his vassal what! shall not a sinner be quit of his fault except his pastor turn robber and one that go about to get a booty except he make his pastor a feoffee in his whole estate and suffer himself under a shadow of penance to freeze naked to be turn out of all his good and possession of inheritance but be it grant admit his holiness rob one prince of his right and revenue to confer the same upon another be it not a high degree of tyranny to finger another man estate and to give that away to a three which the second have no right no lawful authority to give well if the pope then shall become his own carver in the right of another if he shall make his own coffer to swell with another revenue if he shall deck and array his own back in the spoil of a sinner with who in absolution he make peace and take truce what can this be else but run into further degree of wickedness and mischief what can this be else but heap of robbery upon fraud and impiety upon robbery for by such deceitful crafty and cunning practice the nature of the pontificial see mere spiritual be change into the kings-bench-court mere temporal the bishop chair be change into a monarch throne and not only so but beside the sinner repentance be change into a snare or pitfall of cozen deceit and s. Peter net be change into a casting-net or a fly to fish for all the wealth of most flourish kingdom moreover the king a hard case be drive by such wile and subtlety to work impossibility to act more than be lawful or within the compass of his power to practice for the king neither may in right nor can by power trans-nature his crown impair the majesty of his kingdom or leave his royal dignity less free to his heir apparent or next successor than he receive the same of his predecessor much less by any dishonourable capitulation by any unworthy contract degrade his posterity bring his people under the grievous burden of tribute and tax to a foreign prince lest of all make they tributary to a priest unto who it no way appertain to have any hand in the civil affair of king or to distain and unhallow their crown and therefore when the pope dispatch his nuntio to Philippus Augustus request the king to avert Lewis his son from lay any claim to the kingdom of England Philip answer the legate as we have it in matth. Paris no king no prince can alienate or give away his kingdom but by consent of his baron bind by knight service to defend the say kingdom and in case the pope shall stand for the contrary error his holiness shall give to kingdom a most pernicious example by the same author it be testify that king John become odious to his subject for such dishonourable and unworthy enthrall of his crown and kingdom therefore the pope right pretend to the crown of England which be nothing else but a ridiculous usurpation have long ago vanish into smoke and require not so much as the draw of one sword to snatch and pull it by violence out of his hand for the pope power lie altogether in a certain wild and wander conceit or opinion of man and be only a imaginary castle in the air build by pride and underprop by superstition be very speedy disperse upon the first rise and appear of the truth in her glorious brightness there be none so very a dolt or blockhead to deny that in case this right of the pope over England be ground upon God word than his holiness may challenge the like right over all other kingdom because all other kingdom crown and sceptre be subject alike to God word for what privilege what charter what evidence can France fetch out of the roll or any other treasury of her monument or record to show that she owe less subjection to God then England or be this yoke of bondage then bring upon the english nation be it a prerogative whereby they may more easy come to the liberty of the son of God or be the people of England persuade that for all their substance wealth and life bestow on the pope his holiness by way of exchange return they better weight and measure of spiritual grace it be ridiculous only to conceive these toy in thought and yet with such ridiculous with such toy in conceit his lordship feed and entertain his auditor from this point he fall to another bout and fling at his heretic with who he play no fair play before pag. 105. there be not one synod of minister as he say which will willing subscribe to this article whereunto we shall be bind to swear but herein his lordship shoot far from the mark this article be approve and preach by the minister of my kingdom it be likewise preach by those of France and if need be I asssure myself will be sign by all the minister of the french church the l. cardinal proceed for he mean not so soon to give over these heretic all their consistorte believe it as their creed that if catholic prince at any time shall offer force unto their conscience than they be dispense withal for their oath of allegiance hence be these modification and restricition toss so much in their mouth provide the king force we not in our conscience hence be these exception in the profession of their faith provide the sovereign power and authority of God be not in any sort violate or infringe I be not able to conceive what engine can be frame of these material for the bear of king out of their eminent seat by any lawful authority or power in the pope for say those of the religion shall be taint with some like error how can that be any shelter of excuse for those of the romish church to undermine or to dig up the throne of their king but in this allegation of the l. cardinal there be nothing at all which do not jump just and accord to a hair with the article of the three estate and with obedience due to the king for they do not profess that in case the king shall command they to do any act contrary to their conscience they will fly at his throat will make any attempt against his life will refuse to pay their taxation or to defend he in the war they make no profession of depose the king or discharge the people from the oath of allegiance tender to the king which be the very point or issue of the matter in controversy and the main mischief against which the three estate have be most worthy careful to provide a wholesome remedy by this article there be a world of difference between the term of disobedience and of deposition it be one thing to disobey the king command in matter prohibit by divine law and yet in all other matter to perform full subjection unto the King it be another thing of a far high degree or strain of disloyalty to bare the king of his royal robe throne and sceptre and when he be thus far disgrace to degrade he and to put he from his degree and place of a King if the holy father shall charge the l. cardinal to do some act repugnant in his own knowledge to the law of God I will religious and according to the rule of charity presume that his lordship in this case will stand out against his holiness and notwithstanding will still acknowledge he to be pope his lordship yet prosecute and follow his former purpose hence be those arm which they have oftentimes bear against king when king practise to take away the liberty of their conscience and religion hence be those turbulent commotion and sedition by they raise as well in the low-countryes against the king of Spain as in Swethland against the catholic king of Polonia beside he cast junius Brutus Buchananus Barclaius and Gerson in our tooth to what end all this I see not how it can be available to authorize the depose of king especial the pope power to depose and yet his lordship here do outface by his leave and bear down the truth for I can never yet learn by any good and true intelligence that in France those of the religion take arm at any time against their king in the first civil war they stand only upon their guard they stand only to their lawful ward and lock of defence they arm not nor take the field before they be pursue with fire and sword burn up and slaughter beside religion be neither the root nor the rind of those intestine trouble the true ground of the quarrel be this during decline from good to evil at last his lordship come to the close of his oration and bind up his whole harangue with a feat wreath of praise proper to his King he style the king the elder son of the church a young shoot of the lily which king Solomon in all his royalty be not able to match he lead we by the hand into the pleasant meadow of history there to learn upon the very first sight and view that so long so oft as the king of France embrace union and keep good term of concord with pope and the apostolic see so long as the spouse of the church be pasture and feed among the lily all sort of spiritual and temporal grace abundant shower upon their crown and upon their people on the contrary when they make any rent or separation from the most holy see then the lily be prick and almost choke with sharp thorn they begin to droop to stoop and to bear their beautiful head down to the very ground under the strong flaw and gust of boisterous wind and tempest my answer to this flourish close and upshot shall be no less apert than apt it savour not of good and faithful service to smooth and stroke the king head with a soft hand of oil speech and in the mean time to take away the crown from his head and to defile it with dirt but let we try the cause by evidence of history yea by the voice and verdict of experience to see whether the glorious beauty of the french lily have be at any time blast and thereupon have fade by start aside and make separation from the holy See under the reign of king Philip the fair France be bless with peace and prosperity notwithstanding some outrageous act of Holland Zealand and Friesland what need I speak yet a word and no more be they not a kind of naked and bare people of small value before God light the torch of the gospel and advance it in those nation be they not a ill feed and scrag people in comparison of the inestimable wealth and prosperity both in all military action and mechanical trade in traffic as merchant in mart as man of war in long navigation for discovery to which they be now raise and mount by the merciful blessing of God since the darkness of popery have be scatter and the bright sun of the gospel have shine in those Lewis the courteous against their own father who life be a pattern and example of innocence have they not by their infinite exaction rob and scour the kingdom of all their treasure be not the king of France drive to stop their violent course by the pragmatical sanction do they not sundry time interdict the kingdom degrade the king solicit the neighbour-prince to invade and lay hold on the kingdom and stir up the people against the king whereby a gate be open to a world of trouble and parricide do not Ravaillac render this reason for his monstrous and horrible attempt that king Henry have a design to war with God because he have a design to take arm against his holiness who be God this make i to wonder what move the l. cardinal to marshal the last civil war and motion in France in the rank of example of unhappy separation from the pope when the pope himself be the trumpeter of the same troublesome motion if the pope have be wrong and offend by the french king or his people and the kingdom of France have be scourge with pestilence or famine or some other calamity by foreign enemy it may have be take in probability as a vengeance of God for some injury do unto his vicar but his holiness be the root the ground the master-workeman and artificer of all these mischief how can it be say that God punish any injury do to the pope but rather that his holiness do revenge his own quarrel and which be worst of all when his holiness have no just cause of quarrel or offence now then to exhort a nation as the l. cardinal have do by the remembrance of former calamity to curry favour with the pope and to hold a strict union with his holiness be no exhortation to bear the pope any respect of love or of reverence but rather a rub of memory and a call to mind of those grievous calamity whereof the pope have be the only occasion it be also a threaten and obtrude of the pope terrible thunderbolt which never scorch nor parch any skin except craven and meticulous body and have bring many great shower of blessing upon my kingdom as for France if she have enjoy prosperity in the time of her good agreement with pope it be because the pope seek the amity of prince that be in prosperity have the mean to curb his pretension and to put he to some plunge king be not in prosperity because the pope hold amity with king but his holiness use all device and seek all mean to have amity with king because he see they flourish and sail with prosperous wind the swallow be no cause but a companion of the spring the pope be no worker of a kingdom felicity but a wooer of king when they sit in felicity lap he be no founder but a follower of their good fortune on the other side let a kingdom fall into some grievous disaster or calamity let civil war boil in the bowel of the kingdom civil war no less dangerous to the state then fearful and grievous to the people who rise soon than the pope who rush soon into the trouble stream then the pope who thrust himself soon into the heat of the quarrel then the pope who run soon to raise his gain by the public wrack then the pope and all under colour of a heart wound and bleed for the salvation of soul if the lawful king happen to be foil to be oppress and thereupon the state by his fall to get a new master by the pope practice then the say new master must hold the kingdom as of the pope free gift and rule or guide the stern of the state at his beck and by his instruction if the first and right lord in despite of all the pope fulmination and firework shall get the honourable day and upper hand of his enemy then the holy father with a cheerful and pleasant grace yea with fatherly gratulation open the rich cabinet of his jewel I mean the treasury of his indulgence and fall now to dandle and cocker the king in his fatherly lap who throat if he can he will have cut not long before this pestilent mischief have now a long time take root and be grow to a great head in the christian world through the secret but just judgement of God by who Christian king have beenesmitten with a spirit of dizziness christian king who for many aage past have live in ignorance without any sound instruction without any true sense and right feel of their own right and power while under a shadow of religion and false cloak of piety their kingdom have be over-burdened yea over-borne with tribute and their crown make to stoop even to miserable bondage that God in who hand the heart of king be poise and at his pleasure turn as the watercourse that mighty God alone in his good time be able to rouse they out of so deep a slumber and to take order their drowsy fit once over and shake off with heroical spirit that pope hereafter shall play no more upon their patience nor presume to put bit and snaffle in their noble mouth to the bind up of their power with weak scruple like mighty bull lead about by little child with a small twist thread to that God that king of king I devote my sceptre at his foot in all humbleness I lay down my crown to his holy decree and command I will ever be a faithful servant and in his battle a faithful champion to conclude in this just cause and quarrel I dare send the challenge and will require no second to maintain as a defendant of honour that my brother-prince and myself who God have advance upon the throne of sovereign majesty and supreme dignity do hold the royal dignity of his majesty alone to who service as a most humble homager and vassal I consecrate all the glory honour splendour and lustre of my earthly kingdom a speech as it be deliver in the upper house of the parliament to the lord SPIRITVALL and temporal and to the knight citizen and burgess there assemble on Monday the 19 Day of march 1603. be the first day of the first parliament it do no soon please God to lighten his hand and relent the violence of his devour angel against the poor people of this city but as soon do I resolve to call this parliament and that for three chief and principal reason the first whereof be and which of itself although there be no more be not only a sufficient but a most full and necessary ground and reason for convene of this assembly this first reason I say be that you who be here present assemble to represent the body of this whole kingdom and of all sort of people within the same may with your own ear hear and that I out of my own mouth may deliver unto you the assurance of my due thankfulness for your so joyful and general applause to the declare and receive of i in this seat which GOD by my birthright and lineal descent have in the fullness of time provide for i and that immediate after it please God to call your late sovereign of famous memory full of day but full of immortal trophy of honour out of this transitory life not that I be able to express by word or utter by eloquence the vive image of my inward thankfulness but only that out of my own mouth you may rest assure to expect that measure of thankfulness at my hand which be according to the infiniteness of your desert and to my inclination and ability for requital of the same shall I ever nay can I ever be able or rather so unable in memory as to forget your unexpected readiness and alacrity your ever memorable resolution and your most wonderful conjunction and harmony of your heart in declare and embrace i as your undoubted and lawful king and governor or shall it ever be blot out of my mind how at my first entry into this kingdom the people of all sort rid and run nay rather fly to meet i their eye flame nothing but sparkle of affection their mouth and tongue utter nothing but sound of joy their hand foot and all the rest of their member in their gesture discover a passionate long and earnestness to meet and embrace their new sovereign shall I allow in myself that which I can never bear with in another no I must plain and free confess here in all your audience that I do ever natural so far mislike a tongue to smoothe and diligent in pay their creditor with lip payment and verbal thanks as I ever suspect that sort of people mean not to pay their debtor in more substantial sort of coin and therefore for express of my thankfulness I must resort unto the other two reason of my convene of this parliament by they in action to utter my thankfulness both the say reason have but one ground which be the deed whereby all the day of my life I be by God grace to express my say thankfulness towards you but divide in this that in the first of these two my action of thanks be so inseparable conjoin with my person as they be in a manner become individual annex to the same in the other reason my action be such as I may either do they or leave they undo although by God grace I hope never to be weary of the do of they as to the first it be the blessing which God have in my person bestow upon you all wherein I protest I do more glory at the same for your weal then for any particular respect of my own reputation or advantage therein the first then of these blessing which God have joint with my person send unto you be outward peace that be peace abroad with all foreign neighbour for I thank God I may just say that never since I be a king I either receive wrong of any other christian prince or state or do wrong to any I have ever I praise God yet keep peace and amity with all which have be so far tie to my person as at my come here you be witness I find the state embark in a great and tedious war and only by my arrival here and by the peace in my person be now amity keep where war be before which be no small blessing to a christian commonwealth for by peace abroad with their neighbour the town flourish the merchant become rich the trade do increase and the people of all sort of the land enjoy free liberty to exercise themselves in their several vocation without peril or disturbance not that I think this outward peace so unseparable tie to my person as I dare assure promise to myself and to you the certain continuance thereof but thus far I can very well assure you and in the word of a king promise unto you that I shall never give the first occasion of the breach thereof neither shall I ever be move for any particular or private passion of mind to interrupt your public peace except I be force thereunto either for reparation of the honour of the kingdom or else by necessity for the weal and preservation of the same in which case a secure and honourable war must be prefer to a unsecure and dishonourable peace yet do I hope by my experience of the bypass blessing of peace which God have so long ever since my birth bestow upon i that he will not be weary to continue the same nor repent he of his grace towards i transfer that sentence of king David upon his bypass victory of war to i of peace that that God who preserve i from the devour jaw of the bear and of the lion and deliver they into my hand shall also now grant i victory over that uncircumcised Philistine but although outward peace be a great blessing yet be it as far inferior to peace within as civil war be more cruel and unnatural than war abroad and therefore the second great blessing that GOD have with my person send unto you be peace within and that in a double form first by my descent lineal out of the loin of Henry the seven be reunite and confirm in i the union of the two princely rose of the two house of lancaster and York whereof that king of happy memory be the first as he be also the first ground-lay of the other peace the lamentable and miserable event by the civil and bloody dissension betwixt these two house be so great and so late as it need not be renew unto your memory which as it be first settle and unite in he so be it now reunite and confirm in i be just and lineal descend not only of that happy conjunction but of both the branch thereof many time before but the union of these two princely house be nothing comparable to the union of two ancient and famous kingdom which be the other inward peace annex to my person and here I must crave your patience for a little space to give i leave to discourse more particular of the benefit that do arise of that union which be make in my blood be a matter that most proper belong to i to speak of as the head wherein that great body be unite and first if we be to look no high then to natural and physical reason we may easy be persuade of the great benefit that by that union do redound to the whole island for if twenty thousand man be a strong army be not the double thereof forty thousand a double the strong army if a baron enrich himself with double as many land as he have before be he not double the great nature teach we that mountain be make of mote and that at the first kingdom be divide and every particular town or little county as tyrant or usurper can obtain the possession a signory apart many of these little kingdom be now in process of time by the ordinance of God join into great monarchy whereby they be become powerful within themselves to defend themselves from all outward invasion and their head and governor thereby enable to redeem they from foreign assault and punish private transgression within do we not yet remember that this kingdom be divide into seven little kingdom beside Wales and be it not now the strong by their union and have not the union of Wales to England add a great strength thereto which though it be a great principality be nothing comparable in greatness and power to the ancient and famous kingdom of Scotland but what shall we stick upon any natural appearance when it be manifest that God by his almighty providence have preordain it so to be have not God first unite these two kingdom both in language religion and similitude of manner yea have he not make we all in one island compass with one sea and of itself by nature so indivisible as almost those that be borderer themselves on the late border can distinguish nor know or discern their own limit these two country be separate neither by sea nor great river mountain nor other strength of nature but only by little small brook or demolish little wall so as rather they be divide in apprehension then in effect and now in the end and fullness of time unite the right and title of both in my person alike lineal descend of both the crown whereby it be now become like a little world within itself be entrench and fortify round about with a natural and yet admirable strong pond or ditch whereby all the former fear of this nation be now quite cut off the other part of the island be ever before now not only the place of land to all stranger that be to make invasion here but likewise move by the enemy of this state by untimely incursion to make enforce diversion from their conquest for defend themselves at home and keep sure their backdoor as than it be call which be the great hindrance and let that ever my predecessor of this nation get in disturb they from their many famous and glorious conquest abroad what God have conjoin then let no man separate I be the husband and all the whole isle be my lawful wife I be the head and it be my body I be the shepherd and it be my flock I hope therefore no man will be so unreasonable as to think that I that be a christian king under the gospel shall be a polygamist and husband to two wife that I be the head shall have a divide and monstrous body or that be the shepherd to so fair a flock who fold have no wall to hedge it but the four sea shall have my flock part in two but as I be assure that no honest subject of whatsoever degree within my whole dominion be less glad of this joyful union than I be so may the frivolous objection of any that will be hinderer of this work which God have in my person already establish be easy answer which can be none except such as be either blind with ignorance or else transport with malice be unable to live in a well govern commonwealth and only delight to fish in trouble water for if they will stand upon their reputation and privilege of any of the kingdom I pray you be not both the kingdom monarchy from the beginning and consequent can ever the body be count without the head which be ever unseparable join thereunto so that as honour and privilege of any of the kingdom can not be divide from their sovereign so be they now confound and join in my person who be equal and alike kindly head to you both when this kingdom of England be divide into so many little kingdom as I tell you before one of they behoove to eat up another till they be all unite in one and yet can Wiltshire or Devonshire which be of the west saxon although their kingdom be of long durance and do by conquest overcome diverse of the rest of the little kingdom make claim to priority of place or honour before Sussex Essex or other shire which be conquer by they and have we not the like experience in the kingdom of France be compose of diverse dutchy and one after another conquer by the sword for even as little brook lose their name by their run and fall into great river and the very name and memory of the great river swallow up in the ocean so by the conjunction of diverse little kingdom in one be all these private difference and question swallow up and since the success be happy of the saxon kingdom be conquer by the spear of Bellona Mars how much great reason have we to expect a happy issue of this great union which be only fasten and bind up by the wedding ring of Astrea love and peace and as God have make Scotland the one half of this isle to enjoy my birth and the first and most unperfect half of my life and you here to enjoy the perfect and the last half thereof so can I not think that any will be so injurious to i no not in their thought and wish as to cut asunder the one half of i from the other but in this matter I have far enough insist rest assure that in your heart and mind you all applaud this my discourse now although these blessing before rehearse of inward and outward peace be great yet see that in all good thing a great part of their goodness and estimation be lose if they have not appearance of perpetuity or long continuance so have it please almighty God to accompany my person also with that favour have healthful and hopeful issue of my body whereof some be here present for continuance and propagation of that undoubted right which be in my person under who I doubt not but it will please God to prosper and continue for many year this union and all other blessing of inward and outward peace which I have bring with i but neither peace outward nor peace inward nor any other blessing that can follow thereupon nor appearance of the perpetuity thereof by propagation in the posterity be but a weak pillar and a rot reed to lean unto if God do not strengthen and by the staff of his blessing make they durable for in vain do the watchman watch the city if the lord be not the principal defence thereof in vain do the builder build the house if God give not the success and in vain as Paul say do Paul plant and Apollo water if God give not the increase for all worldly blessing be but like swift pass shadow fade flower or chaff blow before the wind if by the profession of true religion and work according thereunto God be not move to maintain and settle the throne of prince and although that since my entry into this kingdom I have both by meeting with diverse of the ecclesiastical estate and likewise by diverse proclamation clear declare my mind in point of religion yet do I not think it amiss in this so solemn a audience I shall now take occasion to discover somewhat of the secret of my heart in that matter for I shall never with God grace be ashamed to make public profession thereof at all occasion lest God shall be ashamed to profess and allow i before man and angel especial lest that at this time man may presume further upon the misknowledge of my meaning to trouble this parliament of we than be convenient at my first come although I find but one religion and that which by myself be profess public allow and by the law maintain yet find I another sort of religion beside a private sect lurk within the bowel of this nation the first be the true religion which by i be profess and by the law be establish the second be the false call catholic but true papist the three which I call a sect rather than religion be the puritan and novelist who do not so far differ from we in point of religion as in their confuse form of policy and parity be ever discontent with the present government and impatient to suffer any superiority which make their sect unable to be suffer in any well govern common wealth but as for my course towards they I remit it to my proclamation make upon that subject and now for the papist I must put a difference betwixt my own private profession of my own salvation and my politic government of the realm for the weal and quietness thereof as for my own profession you have i your head now among you of the same religion that the body be of as I be no stranger to you in blood no more be I a stranger to you in faith or in the matter concern the house of God and although this my profession be according to my education wherein I thank God I suck the milk of God truth with the milk of my nurse yet do I here protest unto you that I will never for such a conceit of constancy or other prejudicate opinion have so firm keep my first profession if I have not find it agreeable to all reason and to the rule of my conscience but I be never violent nor unreasonable in my profession I acknowledge the roman church to be our mother church although defile with some infirmity and corruption as the jew be when they crucify Christ and as I be none enemy to the life of a sick man because I will have his body purge of ill humour no more be I enemy to their church because I will have they reform their error not wish the down throw of the temple but that it may be purge and cleanse from corruption otherwise how can they wish we to enter if their house be not first make clean but as I will be loath to dispense in the least point of my own conscience for any worldly respect than the foolish precisian of they all so will I be as sorry to straight the politic government of the body and mind of all my subject to my private opinion nay my mind be ever so free from persecution or thral of my subject in matter of conscience as I hope that those of that profession within this kingdom have a proof since my come that I be so far from increase their burden with rehoboam as I have so much as either time occasion or law can permit lighten they and even now at this time have I be careful to revise and consider deep upon the law make against they that some overture may be propon to the present parliament for clear these law by reason which be the soul of the law in case they have be in time past further or more rigorous extend by judge than the meaning of the law be or may tend to the hurt aswell of the innocent as of guilty person and as to the person of my subject which be of that profession I must divide they into two rank cleric and layicke for the part of the layick certain I ever think they far more excusable than the other sort because that sort of religion contain such a ignorant doubtful and implicit kind of faith in the layicke ground upon their church as except they do general believe whatsoever their teacher please to affirm they can be think guilty of these particular point of heresy and corruption which their teacher do so wilful profess and again I must subdivide the same layicke into two rank that be either quiet and well mind man peaceable subject who either be old have retain their first drunken in liquor upon a certain shamefastness to be think curious or changeable or be young man through evil education have never be nurse or bring up but upon such venom in place of wholesome nutriment and that sort of people I will be sorry to punish their body for the error of their mind the reformation whereof must only come of God and the true spirit but the other rank of layick who either through curiosity affectation of novelty or discontentment in their private humour have change their coat only to be factious stirrer of sedition and perturber of the common wealth their backwardness in their religion give a ground to i the magistrate to take the better heed to their proceed and to correct their obstinacy but for the part of the cleric I must direct say and affirm that as long as they maintain one special point of their doctrine and another point of their practice they be no way sufferable to remain in this kingdom their point of doctrine be that arrogant and ambitious supremacy of their head the pope whereby he not only claim to be spiritual head of all christian but also to have a imperial civil power over all king and emperor dethrone and decrow prince with his foot as please he and dispense and dispose of all kingdom and empire at his appetite the other point which they observe in continual practice be the assassinate and murder of king think it no sin but rather a matter of salvation to do all action of rebellion and hostility against their natural sovereign lord if he be once curse his subject discharge of their fidelity and his kingdom give a prey by that three crown monarch or rather monster their Head and in this point I have no occasion to speak further here save that I can wish from my heart that it will please God to make i one of the member of such a general christian union in religion as lay wilfulness aside on both hand we may meet in the midst which be the centre and perfection of all thing for if they will leave and be ashamed of such new and gross corruption of they as themselves can maintain nor deny to be worthy of reformation I will for my own part be content to meet they in the midway so that all novelty may be renounce on either side for as my faith be the true ancient catholic and apostolic faith ground upon the scripture and express word of God so will I ever yield all reverence to antiquity in the point of ecclesiastical policy and by that mean shall I ever with God grace keep myself from either be a heretic in faith or schismatic in matter of policy but of one thing will I have the papist of this land to be admonish that they presume not so much upon my lenity because I will be loath to be think a persecuter as thereupon to think it lawful for they daily to increase their number and strength in this kingdom whereby if not in my time at least in the time of my posterity they may be in hope to erect their religion again no let they assure themselves that as I be a friend to their person if they be good subject so be I a vow enemy and do denounce mortal war to their error and that as I will be sorry to be drive by their ill behaviour from the protection and conservation of their body and life so will I never cease as far as I can to tread down their error and wrong opinion for I can not permit the increase and grow of their religion without first betray of myself and my own conscience second this whole isle aswell the part I be come from as the part I remain in in betray their liberty and reduce they to the former slavish yoke which both have cast off before I come among they and three the liberty of the crown in my posterity which I shall leave again under a new slavery have find i^ leave free to i by my predecessor and therefore will I wish all good subject that be deceive with that corruption first if they find any begin of instinction in themselves of knowledge and love to the truth to foster the same by all lawful mean and to beware of quench the spirit that work within they and if they can find as yet no motion tend that way to be studious to read and confer with learned man and to use all such mean as may further their resolution assure themselves that as long as they be disconformable in religion from we they can be but half my subject be able to do but half service and I to want the best half of they which be their soul and here have I occasion to speak to you my lord the bishop for as you my lord of Durham say very learned to day in your sermon correction without instruction be but a tyranny so ought you and all the clergy under you to be more careful vigilant and diligent than you have be to win soul to God aswell by your exemplary life as doctrine and since you see how careful they be spare neither labour pain nor extreme peril of their person to divert the devil be so busy a bishop you shall be the more careful and wakeful in your charge follow the rule prescribe you by s. Paul be careful to exhort and to instruct in season and out of season and where you have be any way sluggish before now waken yourselves up again with a new diligence in this point remit the success to God who call they either at the second three ten or twelve hour as they be alike welcome to he so shall they be to i his lieutenant here the three reason of my convene of you at this time the three reason of assemble the parliament which contain such action of my thankfulness towards you as I may either do or leave undo yet shall with God grace ever press to perform all the day of my life it consist in these two point in make of law at certain time which be only at such time as this in parliament or in the careful execution thereof at all other time as for the make of they I will thus far faithful promise unto you that I will ever prefer the weal of the body and of the whole commonwealth in make of good law and constitution to any particular or private end of i think ever the wealth and weal of the commonwealth to be my great weal and worldly felicity a point wherein a lawful king do direct differ from a tyrant but at this time I be only thus far to forewarn you in that point that you beware to seek the make of too many law for two especial reason first because and the execution of good law be far more profitable in a commonwealth then to burden man memory with the make of too many of they and next because the make of too many law in one parliament will bring in confusion for lack of leisure wise to deliberate before you conclude for the bishop say well to day that to deliberation will a large time be give but to execution a great promptness be require as for the execution of good law it have be very wise and honourable foresee and order by my predecessor in this kingdom in plant such a number of judge and all sort of magistrate in convenient place for the execution of the same and therefore must I now turn i to you that be judge and magistrate under i as my eye and ear in this case I can say none otherwise to you then as Ezekias the good king of juda say to their judge remember that the throne that you fit on be god and neither you nor i and that as you must be answerable to i so must both you and I be answerable to GOD for the due execution of our office that place be no place for you to utter your affection in you must not there hate your foe nor love your friend fear the offence of the great party or pity the misery of the mean you must be blind and not see distinction of person handless not to receive bribe but keep that just temper and mid-course in all your proceed that like a just balance you may neither sway to the right nor left hand three principal quality be require in you knowledge courage and sincerity that you may discern with knowledge execute with courage and do both in upright sincerity and as for my part I do vow and protest here in the presence of God and of this honourable audience I never shall be weary nor omit no occasion wherein I may show my carefulness of the execution of good law and as I wish you that be judge not to be weary in your office in do of it so shall I never be weary with God grace to take account of you which be proper my call and thus have tell you the three cause of my convene of this parliament all three tend only to utter my thankfulness but in diverse form the first by word the other two by action I do confess that when I have do and perform all that in this speech I have promise because the meaning of the word in that place of scripture be understand that in do all that service which we can to God it be but our due and we do nothing to God but that which we be bind to do and in like manner when I have do all that I can for you I do nothing but that which I be bind to do and be accountable to God upon the contrary for I do acknowledge that the special and great point of difference that be betwixt a rightful king and a usurp tyrant be in this that whereas the proud and ambitious tyrant do think his kingdom and people be only ordain for satisfaction of his desire and unreasonable appetite the righteous and just king do by the contrary acknowledge himself to be ordain for the procure of the wealth and prosperity of his people and that his great and principal worldly felicity must consift in their prosperity if you be rich I can be poor if you be happy I can but be fortunate and I protest that your welfare shall ever be my great care and contentment and that I be a servant it be most true that as I be head and governor of all the people in my dominion who be my natural vassal and subject consider they in number and distinct rank so if we will take the whole people as one body and mass then as the head be ordain for the body and not the body for the head so must a righteous king know himself to be ordain for his people and not his people for he for although a king and people be yet can he be no king if he want people and subject but there be many people in the world that lack a head wherefore I will never be ashamed to confess it my principal honour to be the great servant of the commonwealth and ever think the prosperity thereof to be my great felicity as I have already say but as it be the whole body of this kingdom with a uniform assent and harmony as I tell you in the begin of my speech which do so far oblige i in good will and thankfulness of requital by their alacrity and readiness in declare and receive i to that place which God have provide for i and not any particular person for then it have not be the body so be my thankfulness due to the whole State for even as in matter of fault even so in the matter of virtuous and good deed what be do by the willing consent and harmony of the whole body no particular person can just claim thanks as proper to he for the same and therefore I must here make a little apology for myself in that I can not satisfy the particular humour of every person that look for some advancement or reward at my hand since my entry into this kingdom three kind of thing be crave of i advancement to honour preferment to place of credit about my person and reward in matter of land or profit if I have bestow honour upon all no man can have be advance to honour for the degree of honour do consist in prefer some above their fellow if every man have the like access to my privy or bedchamber than no man can have it because it can contain all and if I have bestow land and reward upon every man the fountain of my liberality will be so exhaust and dry as I will lack mean to be liberal to any man and yet be I not so spare but I may without vaunt affirm that I have enlarge my favour in all the three degree towards as many and more than ever king of England do in so short a space no I rather crave your pardon that I have be so bountiful for if the mean of the crown be waste I behove then to have recourse to you my subject and be burdensome to you which I will be loath to be of any king alive for as it be true that as I have already say it be a whole body which do deserve so well at my hand and not every particular person of the people yet be there some who by reason of their office credit with the people or otherwise take occasion both before and at the time of my come among you to give proof of their love and affection towards i not that I be any way in doubt that if other of my subject have be in their place and have have the like occasion but they will have utter the like good effect so general and so great be the love and affection of you all towards i but yet this have be perform by some special person I can not without unthankfulness but requite they according and therefore have I just occasion to advance some in honour some to place of service about i and by reward to enable some who have deserve well of i and be not otherwise able to maintain the rank I think they capable of and other who although they have not particular deserve before yet I find they capable and worthy of place of preferment and credit and not able to sustain those place for which I think they fit without my help two especial cause move i to be so open hand whereof the one be reasonable and honourable but the other I will not be ashamed to confess unto you proceed of my own infirmity that which be just and honourable be that be so far behold to the body of the whole state I think I can not refuse to let run some small brook out of the fountain of my thankfulness to the whole for refresh of particular person that be member of that multitude the other which
