proceed out of my own infirmity be the multitude and importunity of suitor but although reason come by infusion in a manner yet experience grow with time and labour and therefore do I not doubt but experience in time come will both teach the particular subject of this kingdom not to be so importune and undiscreet in crave and i not to be so easy and light move in grant that which may be harmful to my estate and consequent to the whole kingdom and thus have at length declare unto you my mind in all the point for the which I call this parliament my conclusion shall only now be to excuse myself in case you have not find such eloquence in my speech as peradventure you may have look for at my hand I may if I listen allege the great weight of my affair and my continual business and distraction that I can never have leisure to think upon what I be to speak before I come to the place where I be to speak and I may also allege that my first sight of this so famous and honourable a assembly may likewise breed some impediment but leave these excuse I will plain and free in my manner tell you the true cause of it which be that it become a king in my opinion to use no other eloquence than plainness and sincerity by plainness I mean that his speech shall be so clear and void of all ambiguity that they may not be throw nor rend asunder in contrary sense like the old oracle of the pagan god and by sincerity I understand that uprightness and honesty which ought to be in a king whole speech and action that as far as a king be in honour erect above any of his subject so far shall he strive in sincerity to be above they all and that his tongue shall be ever the true messenger of his heart and this sort of eloquence may you ever assure look for at my hand a speech in the parliament house as near the very word as can be gather at the instant my lord spiritual and temporal and you the knight and burgess of this parliament it be far from my thought till very late before my come to this place that this subject shall have be minister unto i whereupon I be now to speak but now it so fall out that whereas in the prepare session of this parliament the principal occasion of my speech be to thank and congratulate all you of this house and in you all the whole commonwealth as be the representative body of the state for your so willing and love receive and embrace of i in that place which GOD and nature by descent of blood have in his own time provide for i so now my subject be to speak of a far great thanksgiving then before I give to you be to a far great person which be to GOD for the great and miraculous delivery he have at this time grant to i and to you all and consequent to the whole body of this estate I must therefore begin with this old and most approve sentence of divinity for almighty God do not furnish so great matter to his glory by the creation of this great world as he do by the redemption of the same neither do his generation of the little world in our old and first Adam so much set forth the praise of God in his justice and mercy as do our regeneration in the last and second Adam and now I must crave a little pardon of you that since king be in the word of GOD itself call god as be his lieutenant and vicegerent on earth and so adorn and furnish with some sparkle of the divinity to compare some of the work of GOD the great king towards the whole and general world to some of his work towards i and this little world of my dominion compass and sever by the sea from the rest of the earth GOD for the just punishment of the first great sin in the original world when the son of GOD go in to the daughter of man and the cup of their iniquity of all sort be fill and heap up to the full do by a general deluge and overflow of water baptize the world to a general destruction and not to a general purgation only except Noah and his family who do repent and believe the threaten of God judgement so now when the world shall wax old as a garment and that all the impiety and sin that can be devise against both the first and second table have and shall be commit to the full measure GOD be to punish the world the second time by fire to the general destruction and not purgation thereof although as be do in the former to Noah and his family by the water so shall all we that believe be likewise purge and not destroy by the fire in the like sort I say I may just compare these two great and fearful domes-daye wherewith GOD threaten to destroy i and all you of this little world that have interest in i for although I confess as all mankind so chief king as be in the high place like the high tree or stay mountain and steep rock be most subject to the daily tempest of innumerable danger and I among all other king have ever be subject unto they not only ever since my birth but even as I may just say before my birth and while I be yet in my mother belly yet have I be expose to two more special and great danger than all the rest the first of they in the kingdom where I be bear and pass the first part of my life and the last of they here which be the great in the former I shall have be baptize in blood and in my destruction not only the kingdom wherein I then be but you also by your future interest shall have taste of my ruin yet it please GOD to deliver i as it be from the very brink of death from the point of the dagger and so to purge i by my thankful acknowledgement of so great a benefit but in this which do so late fall out and which be a destruction prepare not for i alone but for you all that be here present and wherein no rank age nor sex shall have be spare this be not a cry sin of blood as the former but it may well be call a roar nay a thunder sin of fire and brimstone from the which GOD have so miraculous deliver we all what I can speak of this I know not nay rather what can I not speak of it and therefore I must for horror say with the poet in this great and horrible attempt three miraculous event be to be observe in the attempt whereof the like be never either hear or read I observe three wonderful or rather miraculous event first in the cruelty of the plot itself 1 1 the cruelty of the plot. wherein can be enough admire the horrible and fearful cruelty of their device which be not only for the destruction of my person nor of my wife and posterity only but of the whole body of the state in general wherein shall neither have be spare or distinction make of young nor of old of great nor of small of man nor of woman the whole nobility the whole reverend clergy bishop and most part of the good preacher the most part of the knight and gentry yea and if that any in this society be favourer of their profession they shall all have go one way the whole judge of the land with the most of the lawyer and the whole clerk and as the wretch himself which be in the tower do confess it be purposely devise by they and conclude to be do in this house that where the cruel law as they say be make against their religion both place and person shall all be destroy and blow up at once three way how mankind may come to death and then consider therewithal the cruel form of that practice for by three different sort in general may mankind be put to death the first by other man and reasonable creature which be least cruel 1 1 by man. for then both defence of man against man may be expect and likewise who know what pity God may stir up in the heart of the actor at the very instant beside the many way and mean whereby man may escape in such a present fury and the second way more cruel than that 2 2 by unreasonable creature be by animal and unreasonable creature for as they have less pity than man so be it a great horror and more unnatural for man to deal with they but yet with they both resistance may avail and also some pity may be have as be in the lion in who den Daniel be throw or that thankful lion that have the roman in his mercy but the three which be most cruel and unmerciful of all 3 3 by insensible thing be the destruction by insensible and inanimate thing and among they all the most cruel be the two element of water and fire and of those two the fire mostrage and merciless second 2 2 the small ground the conspirator have to move they how wonderful it be when you shall think upon the small or rather no ground whereupon the practiser be entice to invent this tragedy for if these conspirator have only be bankrupt person or discontent upon occasion of any disgrace do unto they this may have seem to have be but a work of revenge but for my own part as iscarce ever know any of they so can they allege so much as a pretend cause of grief and the wretch himself in hand do confess that there be no cause move he or they but mere and only religion and special that Christian man at least so call Englishmen bear within the country and one of the special of they my swear servant in a honourable place shall practice the destruction of their king his posterity their country and all wherein their follow obstinacy be so join to their former malice as the fellow himself that be in hand can be move to discover any sign or note of repentance except only that he do not yet stand to avow that he repent for not be able to perform his intent three 3 3 miraculous event the discovery the discovery hereof be not a little wonderful which will be think the more miraculous by you all if you be aswell acquaint with my natural disposition as those be who be near about i for as I ever do hold suspicion to be the sickness of a tyrant so be I so far upon the other extremity as I rather contemn all advertisement or apprehension of practice and yet now at this time be I so far contrary to myself as when the letter be show to i by my secretary wherein a general obscure advertisement be give of some dangerous blow at this time I do upon the instant interpret and apprehend some dark phrase therein contrary to the ordinary grammar construction of they and in a other sort than I be sure any divine or lawyer in any university will have take they to be mean by this horrible form of blow we up all by powder and thereupon order that search to be make whereby the matter be discover and the man apprehend whereas if I have apprehend or interpret it to any other sort of danger no worldly provision or prevention can have make we escape our utter destruction and in that also be there a wonderful providence of God that when the party himself be take he be but new come out of his house from work have his fireworke for kindle ready in his pocket wherewith as he confess if he have be take but immediate before when he be in the house he be resolve to have blow up himself with his taker one thing for my own part have I cause to thank GOD in that if GOD for our sin have suffer their wicked intent to have prevail it shall never have be speak nor write in aage succeed that I have die inglorious in a alehouse a stew or such vile place but my end shall have be with the most honourable and best company and in that most honourable and fit place for a king to be in for do the turn most proper to his Office and the more have we all cause to thank and magnify GOD for this his merciful delivery and special I for my part that he have give i yet once leave whatsoever shall come of i hereafter to assemble you in this honourable place and here in this place where our general destruction shall have be to magnify and praise he for our general delivery that I may just now say of my enemy and you as David do often say in the psalm and since Scipio a ethnic lead only by the light of nature that day when he be accuse by the tribune of the people of Rome for mispend and waste in his punic war the city treasure even upon the sudden brake out with that diversion of they from that matter call they to remembrance how that day be the day of the year wherein God have give they so great a victory against Hannibal and therefore it be fit for they all leave other matter to run to the temple to praise God for that so great delivery which the people do all follow with one applause how much more cause have we that be christian to bestow this time in this place for thanksgiving to God for his great mercy though we have have no other errant of assemble here at this time wherein if I have speak more like a divine then will seem to belong to this place the matter itself must plead for my excuse for be here come to thank God for a divine work of his mercy how can I speak of this deliverance of we from so hellish a practice so well as in language of divinity which be the direct opposite to so damnable a intention and therefore may I just end this purpose as I do begin it with this sentence the mercy of God be above all his work it rest now that I shall short inform you what be to be do hereafter upon the occasion of this horrible and strange accident as for your part that be my faithful and love subject of all degree I know that your heart be so burn up with zeal in this errant and your tongue so ready to utter your dutiful affection and your hand and foot so bend to concur in the execution thereof for which as I need not to spur you so can I not but praise you for the same as it may very well be possible that the zeal of your heart shall make some of you in your speech rash to blame such as may be innocent of this attempt but upon the other part I wish you to consider that I will be sorry that any be innocent of this practice either domestical or foreign shall receive blame or harm for the same for although it can be deny that it be the only blind superstition of their error in religion that lead they to this desperate device yet do it not follow that all profess that romish religion be guilty of the same for as it be true that no other sect of heretic not except Turk jew nor pagan no not even those of Calicute who adore the devil do ever maintain by the ground of their religion that it be lawful or rather meritorious as the romish catholic call it to murder prince or people for quarrel of religion and although particular man of all profession of religion have be some thief some murderer some traitor yet ever when they come to their end and just punishment they confess their fault to be in their nature and not in their profession these romish catholic only except yet it be true on the other side that many honest man blind peradventure with some opinion of popery as if they be not sound in the question of the real presence or in the number of the sacrament or some such schoole-question yet do they either not know or at least not believe all the true ground of popery which be in deed the mystery of iniquity and therefore do we just confess that many papist especial our forefather lay their only trust upon CHRIST and his merit at their last breath may be and often time be save detest in that point and think the cruelty of puritan worthy of fire that will admit no salvation to any Papist I therefore thus do conclude this point that as upon the one part many honest man seduce with some error of popery may yet remain good and faithful subject so upon the other part none of those that true know and believe the whole ground and school conclusion of their doctrine can ever prove either good christian or faithful subject and for the part of foreign prince and state I may so much the more acquit they and their minister of their knowledge and consent to any such villainy as I may just say that in that point I better know all christian king by myself that no king nor prince of honour will ever abase himself so much as to think a good thought of so base and dishonourable a treachery wish you therefore that as God have give i a happy peace and amity with all other christian prince my neighbour as be even now very grave tell you by my l. chancellor that so you will reverent judge and speak of they in this case and for my part I will wish with those ancient philosopher that there be a crystal window in my breast wherein all my people may see the secret thought of my heart for than may you all see no alteration in my mind for this accident further than in these two point the first caution and wariness in government to discover and search out the mystery of this wickedness as far as may be the other after due trial severity of punishment upon those that shall be find guilty of so detestable and unheard of villainy and now in this matter if I have trouble your ear with a abrupt speech undigested in any good method or order you have to consider that a abrupt and unadvised speech do best become in the relation of so abrupt and unorderly a accident and although I have ordain the prorogue of this parliament until after Christmas upon two necessary respect whereof the first be that neither I nor my council can have leisure at this time both to take order for the apprehension and trial of these conspirator and also to wait upon the daily affair of the parliament as the council must do and the other reason be the necessity at this time of diverse of your presence in your shire that have charge and commandment there for as these wretch think to have blow up in a manner the whole world of this island every man be now come up here either for public cause of parliament or else for their own private cause in law or otherwise so these rebel that now wander through the country can never have get so fit a time of safety in their passage or whatsoever unlawful action as now when the country by the foresay occasion be in a manner leave desolate and waste unto they beside that it may be that I shall desire you at your next session to take upon you the judgement of this crime for as so extraordinary a fact deserve extraordinary judgement so can there not I think follow even their own rule be a fit judgement for they then that they shall be measure with the same measure wherewith they think to measure we and that the same place and person who they think to destroy shall be the just avengers of their so unnatural a parricide yet not know that I will have occasion to meet with you myself in this place at the begin of the next session of this parliament because if it have not be for deliver of the article agree upon by the commissioner of the union which be think most convenient to be do in my presence where both head and member of the parliament be meet together my presence have not otherwise be requisite here at this time I have therefore think good for conclusion of this meeting to discourse to you somewhat anent the true nature and definition of a parliament which I will remit to your memory till your next sit down that you may then make use of it as occasion shall be minister for albeit it be true that at the first session of my first parliament which be not long after my entry into this kingdom it can not become i to inform you of any thing belong to law or state here for all knowledge must either be infuse or acquire and see the former sort thereof be now with prophecy cease in the world it can not be possible for i at my first entry here before experience have teach it i to be able to understand the particular mystery of this state yet now that I have reign almost three year among you and have be careful to observe those thing that belong to the office of a king albeit that time be but a short time for experience in other yet in a king may it be think a reasonable long time especial in i who although I be but in a manner a new king here yet have be long acquaint with the office of a king in such another kingdom as do near of all other agree with the law and custom of this State remit to your consideration to judge of that which have be conclude by the commissioner of the union wherein I be at this time to signify unto you that as I can bear witness to the foresay commissioner that they have not agree nor conclude therein any thing wherein they have not foresee as well the weal and commodity of the one country as of the other so can they all bear i record that I be so far from press they to agree to any thing which may bring with it any prejudice to this people as by the contrary I do ever admonish they never to conclude upon any such union as may carry hurt or grudge with it to either of the say nation for the leave of any such thing can not but be the great hindrance that may be to such a action which God by the law of nature have provide to be in his own time and have now in effect perfect in my person to which purpose my lord chancellor have better speak than I be able to relate and as to the nature of this high court of parliament it be nothing else but the king great council which the king do assemble either upon occasion of interpret or abrogate old law or make of new according as ill manner shall deserve or for the public punishment of notorious evil doer or the praise and reward of the virtuous and well deserver wherein these four thing be to be consider first whereof this court be compose second what matter be proper for it three to what end it be ordain and four what be the mean and way whereby this end shall be bring to pass as for the thing itself it be compose of a head and a body the head be the king the body be the member of the parliament this body again be subdivide into two part the upper and low house the upper compound partly of nobility temporal man who be heritable councillor to the high court of parliament by the honour of their creation and land and partly of bishop spiritual man who be likewise by the virtue of their place and dignity counsellor life renter or of this court. the other house be compose of knight for the shire and gentry and burgess for the town but because the number will be infinite for all the gentleman and burgess to be present at every parliament therefore a certain number be select and choose out of that great body serve only for that parliament where their person be the representation of that body now the matter whereof they be to treat aught therefore to be general and rather of such matter as can well be perform without the assemble of that general body and no more of these general neither than necessity shall require for as so do the life and strength of the law consist not in heap up infinite and confuse number of law but in the right interpretation and good execution of good and wholesome law if this be so then neither be this a place on the one side for every rash and harebrained fellow to propone new law of his own invention nay rather I can wish these busy head to remember that law of the lacedaemonian that whosoever come to propone a new law to the people behoove public to present himself with a rope about his neck that in case the law be not allow he shall be hang therewith so wary shall man be of propon novelty but most of all not to propone any bitter or seditious law which can produce nothing but grudge and discontentment between the prince and his people nor yet be it on the other side a convenient place for private man under the colour of general law to propone nothing but their own particular gain either to the hurt of their private neighbour or to the hurt of the whole state in general which many time under fair and please title be smooth pass over and so by stealth procure without consideration that the private meaning of they tend to nothing but either to the wreck of a particular party or else under colour of public benefit to pill the poor people and serve as it be for a general impost upon they for fill the purse of some private person and as to the end for which the parliament be ordain be only for the advancement of God glory and the establishment and wealth of the king and his people it be no place then for particular man to utter there their private conceit nor for satisfaction of their curiosity and least of all to make show of their eloquence by tyne the time with long study and eloquent oration no the reverence of God their king and their country be well settle in their heart will make they ashamed of such toy and remember that they be there as swear counsellor to their king to give their best advice for the furtherance of his service and the flourish weal of his estate and last if you will right consider the mean and way how to bring all your labour to a good end you must remember that you be here assemble by your lawful king to give he your best advice in the matter propose by he unto you be of that nature which I have already tell wherein you be grave to deliberate and upon your conscience plain to determine how far those thing propound do agree with the weal both of your king and of your country who weal can be separate and as for myself the world shall ever bear i witness that I never shall propone any thing unto you which shall not as well tend to the weal public as to any benefit for i so shall I never oppone myself to that which may tend to the good of the commonwealth for the which I be ordain as I have often say and as you be to give your advice in such thing as shall by your king be propose so be it on your part your duty to propone any thing that you can after mature deliberation judge to be needful either for these end already speak of or otherwise for the discovery of any evil in the kingdom which peradventure may not have come to the king ear if this than ought to be your grave manner of proceed in this place man shall be ashamed to make show of the quickness of their wit here either in taunt scoff or detract the prince or state in any point or yet in break jest upon their fellow for which the ordinary or alehouse be fit place than this honourable and high court of parliament in conclusion then since you be to break up for the reason I have already tell you I wish such of you as have any charge in your country to hasten you home for the repress of the insolence of these rebel and apprehension of their person wherein as I hearty pray to the almighty for your prosperous success so do I not doubt but we shall short hear the good news of the same and that you shall have a happy return and meet here to all our comfort here the lord chancellor speak touch the prorogue of the parliament and have do his majesty rise again and say since it please God to grant i two such notable delivery upon one day of the week which be Tuesday and likewise one day of the month which be the five thereby to teach i that as it be the same devil that still persecute i so it be one and the same GOD that still mighty deliver i I think it therefore not amiss that the one and twenty day of january which fall to be upon Tuesday shall be the day of meeting of this next session of parliament hope and assure myself that the same GOD who have now grant i and you all so notable and gracious a delivery shall prosper all our affair at that next session and bring they to a happy conclusion and now I consider GOD have well provide it that the end of this parliament have be so long continue for as for my own part I never have any other intention but only to seek so far my weal and prosperity as may coniunct stand with the flourish state of the whole commonwealth as I have often tell you so on the other part I confess if I have be in your place at the begin of this parliament which be so soon after my entry into this kingdom wherein you can not possible have so perfect a knowledge of my inclination as experience since have teach you I can not but have suspect and misinterpret diverse thing in the try whereof now I hope by your experience of my behaviour and form of government you be well enough clear and resolve a speech to both the house of parliament deliver in the great chamber at Whitehall the last day of march 1607. my lord of the high house and you knight and burgess of the low house all man at the begin of a feast bring forth good wine first and after worse this be the say of the governor of the feast at Cana in Galilee where CHRIST wrought his first miracle by change water into wine but in this case now whereof I be to speak unto you I must follow that governors rule and not Christ example in give you the worst and sour wine last for all the time of this long session of the parliament you have be so feed and cloy special you of the low house with such banquet and choice of delicate speech and your ear so season with the sweetness of long precogitate oration as this my speech now in the break up of this assembly can but appear unto your taste as the worst wine propose in the end of the banquet since I be only to deliver now unto you matter without curious form substance without ceremony truth in all sincerity yet consider the person that speak the party to who I speak the matter whereof I mean to speak it fit better to utter matter rather than word in regard of the greatness of my place who be to speak to you the gravity of you the auditory which be the high court of parliament the weight of the matter which concern the security and establishment of this whole empire and little world study oration and much eloquence upon little matter be fit for the university where not the subject which be speak of but the trial of his wit that speak be most commendable but on the contrary in all great counsel or parliament few word with most matter do become best where the dispatch of the great errand in hand and not the praise of the person be most to be look unto like the garment of a chaste woman who be only set forth by her natural beauty which be proper her own other deck be but ensign of a harlot that fly with borrow feather and beside the conveniency I be force hereunto by necessity my place call i to action and not leave i to the liberty of contemplation have always my thought busy with the public care of you all where every one of you have but himself and his own private to think of be at more leisure to make study speech and therefore the matter which I deliver you confuse as in a sack I leave it to you when you be in your chamber and have better leisure than I can have to rank they in order every one in their own place thus much by way of preface but I proceed to the matter whereof I may say with s. Paul I can speak in as many tongue as you all but I have rather speak three word to edification then talk all day without understanding in vain say the psalmist do the builder build the house or the watchman watch the city unless the lord give his blessing thereunto and in the new testament s Paul say that he may plant Apollo may water but it be GOD only that must give the increase this I speak because of the long time which have benespent about the treaty of the union for myself I protest unto you all when I first propound the union I then think there can have be no more question of it then of your declaration and acknowledgement of my right unto this crown and that as two twin they will have grow up together the error be my mistake I know i own end but not other fear but now find many cross long disputation strange question and nothing do I must needs think it proceed either of mistake of the errand or else from some jealousy of i the propounder that you so add delay unto delay search out as it be the very bowel of curiosity and conclude nothing neither can I condemn you for be yet in some jealousy of my intention in this matter have not yet have so great experience of my behaviour and inclination in these few year past as you may peradventure have in a long time hereafter and not have occasion to consult daily with myself and hear my own opinion in all those particular which be debate among you but here I pray you now mistake i not at the first when as I seem to find fault with your delay and curiosity as if I will have you to resolve in a hour time that which will take a month advisement for you all know that and you have oft hear i say that the king will and intention be the speak law aught to be Luce and I hope you of the low house have the proof of this my clearness by a bill send you down from the upper house within these few day or rather few hour wherein may very well appear unto you the care I have to put my subject in good security of their possession for all posterity to come and therefore that you may clear understand my meaning in that point I do free confess you have reason to advise at leisure upon so great a cause for great matter do ever require great deliberation before they be well conclude consultation must proceed but the execution of a sentence upon the resolution will be speedy if you will go on it matter not though you go with leaden foot so you make still some progress and that there be no let or needless delay and do not I be ever for the in every thing between foolish rashness and extreme length there be a middle way search all that be reasonable but omit that which be idle curious and unnecessary otherwise there can never be a resolution or end in any good work and now from the general I will descend to particular and will only for the ease of your memory divide the matter that I be to speak of into four head by open unto you first what I crave second in what manner I desire it three what commodity will ensue to both the kingdom by it four what the suppose inconveniency may be that give impediment thereunto for the first what I crave I protest before GOD who know my heart and to you my people before who it be a shame to lie that I claim nothing but with acknowledgement of my bond to you that as you owe to i subjection and obedience so my sovereignty oblige i to yield to you love government and protection neither do I ever wish any happiness to myself which be not conjoin with the happiness of my people I desire a perfect union of law and person and such a naturalize as may make one body of both kingdom under i your king that I and my posterity if it so please God may rule over you to the world end such a union as be of the scot and picte in Scotland and of the heptarchy here in England and for Scotland I avow such a union as if you have get it by conquest but such a conquest as may be cement by love the only sure bond of subjection or friendship that as there be over both but so there may be in both but for no more possible be it for one king to govern two country contiguous the one a great the other a less a rich and a poor the great draw like a adamant the lesser to the commodity thereof then for one head to govern two body or one man to be husband of two wife whereof Christ himself say but in the general union you must observe two thing for I will discover my thought plain unto you I study clearness not eloquence and therefore with the old philosopher I will hearty wish my breast be a transparent glass for you all to see through that you may look into my heart and then will you be satisfy of my meaning for when I speak of a perfect union I mean not confusion of all thing you must not take from Scotland those particular privilege that may stand as well with this union as in England many particular custom in particular shire as the custom of Kent and the royalty of the county Palatine of Chester do with the common law of the kingdom for every particular shire almost and much more every county have some particular custom that be as it be natural most fit for that people but I mean of such a general union of law as may reduce the whole island that as they live already under one monarch so they may all be govern by one law for I must needs confess by that little experience I have have since my come hither and I think I be able to prove it that the ground of the common law of England be the best of any law in the world either civil or municipal and the fit for this people but as every law will be clear and full so the obscurity in some point of this our write law and want of fullness in other the variation of case and man curiosity breed every day new question have enforce the judge to judge in many case here by case and precedent wherein I hope lawyer themselves will not deny but that there must be a great uncertainty and I be sure all the rest of you that be gentleman of other profession be long ago weary of it if you can have have it amend for where there be variety and uncertainty although a just judge may do right yet a ill judge may take advantage to do wrong and then be all honest man that succeed he tie in a manner to his unjust and partial conclusion wherefore leave not the law to the pleasure of the judge but let your law be look into for I desire not the abolish of the law but only the clear and the sweep off the rust of they and that by parliament our law may be clear and make know to all the subject yea rather it be less hurt that all the approve case be set down and allow by parliament for stand law in all time to come for although some of they peradventure may be unjust as set down by corrupt judge yet better it be to have a certain law with some spot in it nor live under such a uncertain and arbitrary law since as the proverb be it be less harm to suffer a inconvenience than a mischief and now may you have fair occasion of amend and polish your law when Scotland be to be unite with you under they for who can blame Scotland to say if you will take away our own law I pray you give we a better and clear in place thereof but this be not possible to be do without a fit preparation he that build a ship must first provide the timber and as Christ himself say no man will build a house but he will first provide the material nor a wise king will not make war against another without he first makeprovision of money and all great work must have their preparation and that be my end in cause the instrument of the union to be make union be a marriage will he not be think absurd that for further of a marriage between two friend of he will make his first motion to have the two party be lay in bed together and perform the other turn of marriage must there not precede the mutual sight and acquaintance of the party one with another the condition of the contract and jointure to be talk of and agree upon by their friend and such other thing as in order ought to go before the end of such a work the union be a eternal agreement and reconciliation of many long bloody war that have be between these two ancient kingdom be it the ready way to agree a private quarrel between two to bring they at the first to shake hand and as it be kiss other and lie under one roof or rather in one bed together before that first the ground of their quarrel be commune upon their mind mitigate their affection prepare and all other circumstance first use that aught to be use to proceed to such a final agreement every honest man desire a perfect union but they that say so and admit no preparation thereto have in over fall in cord if after your so long talk of union in all this long session of parliament you rise without agree upon any particular what will the neighbour prince judge who eye be all fix upon the conclusion of this action but that the king be refuse in his desire whereby the nation shall be tax and the king disgrace and what a ill preparation be it for the mind of Scotland towards the union when they shall hear that ill be speak of their whole nation but nothing be do nor advance in the matter of the union itself but this I be glad be but the fault of one and one be no number yet have your neighbour of Scotland this advantage of you that none of they have speak ill of you nor shall as long as I be king in parliament or any such public place of juditature consider therefore well if the mind of Scotland have not need to be well prepare to persuade their mutual consent see you here have all the great advantage by the union be not here the personal residence of the king his whole court and family be not here the seat of justice and the fountain of government must they not be subject to the law of England and so with time become but as Cumberland and Northumberland and those other remote and northern shire you be to be the husband they the wife you conqueror they as conquer though not by the sword but by the sweet and sure bond of love beside that they as other northern country will beseldome see and salute by their king and that as it be but in a post or hunt journey how little cause then they may have of such a change of so ancient a monarchy into the case of private shire judge right herein and that you may be the more upright judge suppose yourselves the patient of who such sentence shall be give but what preparation be it which I crave only such as by the entrance may show something be do yet more be intend there be a conceit entertain and a double jealousy possess many wherein I be misjudge first that this union will be the crisis to the overthrow of England and set up of Scotland England will then be overwhelm by the swarm of the scot who if the union be effect will reign and rule all the second be my profuse liberality to the scottish man more than the english and that with this union all thing shall be give to they and you turn out of all to you shall be leave the sweat and labour to they shall be give the fruit and sweet and that my forbearance be but till this union may be gain how agreeable this be to the truth judge you and that not by my word but by my action do I crave the union without exception do I not offer to bind myself and to reserve to you as in the instrument all place of judicature do I intend any thing which stand not with the equal good of both nation I can then have do it and not speak of it for all man of understanding must agree that I may dispose without assent of parliament office of judicature and other both ecclesiastical and temporal but herein I do voluntary offer by my letter from Royston to the commissioner to bind my prerogative some think that I will draw the scottish nation hither talk idle of transport of tree out of a barren ground into a better and of lean cattle out of bad pasture into a more fertile soil can any man displant you unless you will or can any man think that Scotland be so strong to pull you out of your house or do you not think I know England have more people Scotland more waste ground so that there be roomth in Scotland rather to plant your idle people that swarm in London street and other town and disburden you of they then to bring more unto you and in case of justice if I be partial to either side let my own mouth condemn i as unworthy to be your King I appeal to yourselves if in favour or justice I have be partial nay my intention be ever you shall then have most cause to praise my discretion when you see I have most power if hitherto I have do nothing to your prejudice much less mean I hereafter if when I may have do it without any breach of promise think so of i that much less I will do it when a law be to restrain i I owe no more to the scottish man then to the english I be bear there and swear here and now reign over both such particular person of the scottish nation as may claim any extraordinary merit at my hand I have already reasonable reward and I can assure you that there be none leave who for I mean extraordinary to strain myself further then in such ordinary benefit as I may equal bestow without my own great hurt upon any subject of either nation in which case no king hand can ever beful close to both I owe justice and protection which with God grace I shall ever equal balance for my liberality I have tell you of it heretofore my three first year be to i as a Christmas I can not then be miserable shall I have be oversparing to they they may have think joseph have forget his brother or that the king have be drink with his new kingdom but suit go not now so cheap as they be wont neither be there so many fee take in the hamper and pettibagge for the great seal as have be and if I do respect the english when I come first of who I be receive with joy and come as in a hunt journey what may the scottish have just say if I have not in some measure deal bountiful with they that so long have serve i so far adventure themselves with i and be so faithful to i I have give you now four year proof since my come and what I may have do more to have raise the scottish nation you all know and the long I live the less cause have I to be acquaint with they and so the less hope of extraordinary favour towards they for since my come from they I do not already know the one half of they by face most of the youth be now rise up to be man who be but child when I be there and more be bear since my come thence now for my land and revenue of my crown which you may think I have diminish they be not yet so far diminish but that I think no prince of Christendom have fair possession to his crown then yet I have and in token of my care to preserve the same to my posterity for ever the entail of my land to the crown have be long ago offer unto you and that it be not yet do be not my fault as you know my treasurer here know my care and have already in part declare it and if I do not hope to treble my revenue more than I have impair it I shall never rest quiet in my bed but notwithstanding my come to the crown with that extraordinary applause which you all know and that I have two nation to be the object of my liberality which never any prince have here before will you compare my gift out of my inheritance with some prince here that have only this nation to respect and who whole time of reign be little long than i have be already it will be find that their gift have far surpass i albeit as I have already say they have nothing so great cause of use their liberality for the manner of the union present desire it stand in 3. part second the first take away of hostile law for since there can be now no war betwixt you be it not reason hostile law shall cease for the king of England now can have war with the king of Scotland therefore this fail of itself the second be community of commerce I be no stranger unto you for you all know I come from the loin of your ancient king they of Scotland be my subject as you be but how can I be natural liege lord to you both and you stranger one to the other shall they which be of one allegiance with you be no better respect of you nor free among you than Frenchmen and spaniard since I be sovereign over both you as subject to one king it must needs follow that you converse and have commerce together there be a rumour of some ill deal that shall be use by the commissioner merchant of Scotland they be here in England and shall remain till your next meeting and abide trial to prove themselves either honest man or knave for the three point of naturalisation all you agree that they be no alien and yet will not allow they to be natural what kind of prerogative will you make but for the postnati your own lawyer and judge at my first come to this crown inform i there be a difference between the and the post of each kingdom which cause i to publish a proclamation that the post be naturalize by my accession to this crown I do not deny but judge may err as man and therefore I do not press you here to swear to all their reason I only urge at this time the conveniency for both kingdom neither press you to judge nor to be judge but remember also it be as possible and likely your own lawyer may err as the judge therefore as I wish you to proceed herein so far as may tend to the weal of both nation so will I have you on the other part to beware to disgrace either my proclamation or the judge who when the parliament be do have power to try your land and life for so you may disgrace both your king and your law for the do of any act that may procure less reverence to the judge can but breed a looseness in the government and a disgrace to the whole nation the reason that most move i for aught I have yet hear that there can but be a difference between the and the post and that in the favour of the last be that they must be near unto you be bear under the present government and common allegiance but in point of conveniency there be no question but the post be more to be respect for if you will have a perfect and perpetual union that can be in the who be but few in comparison of those that shall be in all aage succeed and can live long but in the shall the union be continue and live ever age after age which want a difference can but leave a perpetual mark of separation in the work of the union as also that argument of jealousy will be so far remove in the case of the post which be to reap the benefit in all succeed aage as by the contrary there will then rise pharaos which never know joseph the king my successor who be bear and breed here can never have more occasion of acquaintance with the scottish nation in general than any other english king that be before my time be not therefore abuse with the flatter speech of such as will have the prefer allege their merit in my service and such other reason which indeed be but sophism for my reward out of my liberality of any particular man have nothing ado with the general act of the union which must not regard the desert of private person but the general weal and conjoin of the nation beside that the actual naturalize which be the only point that be in your hand be already grant to by yourselves to the most part of such particular person as can have any use of it here and if any other well deserve man be to sue for it hereafter I doubt not but there will never be question move among you for the grant of it and therefore it be most evident that such discourser have in over fall in cord as I say before carry a outward appearance of love to the union but indeed a contrary resolution in their heart and as for limitation and restriction such as shall by i be agree upon to be reasonable and necessary after you have full debate upon they you may assure yourselves I will with indifferency grant what be requisite without partial respect of Scotland I be as I have often say bear and swear king over both kingdom only this far let i entreat you in debate the point at your next meeting that you be as ready to resolve doubt as to move they and to be satisfy when doubt be clear and as for commodity that come by the union of these kingdom they be great and evident peace plenty love free intercourse and common society of two great nation all foreign king that have send their ambassador to congratulate with i since my come have salute i as monarch of the whole isle and with much more respect of my greatness then if I be king alone of one of these realm and with what comfort do yourselves behold irish scottish welsh and english diverse in nation yet all walk as subject and servant within my court and all live under the allegiance of your king beside the honour and lustre that the increase of gallant man in the court of diverse nation carry in the eye of all stranger that repair hither those confine place which be the border of the two kingdom where heretofore much blood be shed and many of your ancestor lose their life yea that lie waste and desolate and be habitation but for runagate be now become the navel or Vmbilick of both kingdom plant and people with civility and riches their church begin to be plant their door stand now open they fear neither rob nor spoil and where there be nothing before hear nor see in those part but bloodshed oppression complaint and outcry they now live every man peaceable under his own figgetree and all their former cry and complaint turn only into prayer to God for their king under who they enjoy such ease and happy quietness the march beyond and on this side Twede be as fruitful and as peaceable as most part of England if after all this there shall be a scissure what inconvenience will follow judge you and as for the inconvenience that be fear on England part it be allege that the scot be a populous nation they shall be harbour in our nest they shall be plant and flourish in our good soil they shall eat our commons bare and make we lean these be foolish and idle surmise that which you possess they be not to enjoy by law they can nor by my partiality they shall not for set apart conscience and honour which if I shall set apart indeed I have rather wish myself to be set apart and out of all be can any man conclude either out of common reason or good policy that I will prefer those which perhaps I shall never see or but by post for a month before those with who I must always dwell can they conquer or overcome you with swarm of people as the goth and the vandal do Italy sure the world know they be nothing so populous as you be and although they have have the honour and good fortune never to be conquer yet be they ever but upon the defensive part and may in a part thank their hill and inaccessible passage that preserve they from a utter overthrow at the hand of all that pretend to conquer they or be they so very poor and miserable in their own habitation that necessity shall force they all to make incursion among you and for my part when I have two nation under my government can you imagine I will respect the lesser and neglect the great will I not think it a less evil and hazard to i that the plague be at Northampton or Berwick then at London so near Westminster the seat of my habitation and of my wife and child will not a man be more careful to quench the fire take in his near neighbour house then if a whole town be a fire far from he you know that I be careful to preserve the wood and game through all England nay through all the isle yet none of you doubt but that I will be more offend with any disorder in the forest of Waltham for steal of a stag there which lie as it be under my nose and in a manerjoin with my garden then with cut of timber or steal of a dear in any forest of the north part of Yorkshire or the bishopric think you that I will prefer they that be absent less powerful and far off to do i good or hurt before you with who my security and live must be and where I desire to plant my posterity if I may by any such favour raise myself to a greatness it may be probable all I can draw and to lose a whole state here to please a few there be madness I need speak no more of this with protestation speak but of wit it be not likely and to doubt of my intention in this be more than devilish for i own part I offer more than I receive and conveniency I prefer before law in this point for three part wherein I may hurt this nation by partiality to the scot you know do absolute lie in my hand and power for either in disposition of rent or whatsoever benefit or in the prefer of they to any dignity or office civil or ecclesiastical or in call they to the parliament it do all full and only lie within the compass of my prerogative which be the part wherein the scottish man can receive either benefit or preferment by the union and wherein for the care I have of this people I be content to bind myself with some reasonable restriction as for the four part the naturalize which only lie in your hand it be the point wherein they receive least benefit of any for in that they can obtain nothing but what they buy by their purse or acquire by the self same mean that you do and as for the point of naturalize which be the point think so fit and so precise belong to parliament not to speak of the common law wherein as yet I can profess no great knowledge but in the civil law wherein I be a little better verse and which in the point of conjunction of nation shall bear a great sway it be the law of nation I will maintain two principle in it which no learned and grave civilian will deny as be clear to be prove both out of the text itself in many place and also out of the best approve doctor and interpreter of that law the one that it be a special point of the king own prerogative to make alien citizen and the other that in any case wherein the law be think not to be clear as some of yourselves do doubt that in this case of the postnati the law of England do not clear determine then in such a question wherein no positive law be resolute rexe for he be and be to supply the law where the law want and if many famous history be to be believe they give the example for maintain of this law in the person of the king of England and France especial who special prerogative they allege it to be but this I speak only as know what belong to a king although in this case I press no further then that which may agree with your love and stand with the weal and conveniency of both nation and whereas some may think this union will bring prejudice to some town and corporation within England it may be a merchant or two of Bristol or Yarmouth may have a hundred pound less in his pack but if the empire gain and become the great it be no matter you see one corporation be ever against another and no private company can be set up but with some loss to another for the suppose inconvenience rise from Scotland they be three four first that there be a evil affection in the scottish nation to the union next the union be incompatible between two such nation three that the gain be small or none if this be so to what end do we talk of a union for proof of the first point there be allege a averseness in the scottish nation express in the instrument both in the preface and body of their act in the preface where they declare that they will remain a absolute and free monarchy and in the body of the act where they make a exception of the ancient fundamental law of that kingdom and first for the general of their averseness all the main current in your lower-house run this whole session of parliament with that opinion that Scotland be so greedy of this union and apprehend that they shall receive so much benefit by it as they care not for the strictness of any condition so they may attain to the substance and yet you now say they be backward and averse from the union this be a direct contradiction in for how can they both be beggar and backward in one and the self same thing at the same time but for answer to the particular it be a old school point you can interpret their law nor they you I that make they with their assent can best expound they and first I confess that the english parliament be so long and the scottish so short that a mean between they will do well for the shortness of their continue together be the cause of their hasty mistake by set these word of exception of fundamental law in the body of the act which they only do in press to imitate word by word the english instrument wherein the same word be contain in your preface and as to their meaning and interpretation of that word I will not only deliver it unto you out of my own conceit but as it be deliver unto i by the best lawyer of Scotland both counselor and other lawyer who be at the make thereof in Scotland and be commissioner here for performance of the same their meaning in the word of fundamental law you shall perceive more full hereafter when I handle the objection of the difference of law for they intend thereby only those law whereby confusion be avoid and their king descent maintain and the heritage of the succession and monarchy which have be a kingdom to which I be in descent three hundred year before CHRIST not mean it as you do of their common law for they have none but that which be call and their desire of continue a free monarchy be only mean that all such particular privilege whereof I speak before shall not be so confound as for want either of magistrate law or order they may fall in such a confusion as to become like a naked province without law or liberty under this kingdom I hope you mean not I shall set garrison over they as the spaniard do over Sicily and Naples or govern they by commissioner which be seldom find succeed all wise and honest man this I must say for Scotland and I may true vaunt it here I sit and govern it with my pen I write and it be do and by a clerk of the council I govern Scotland now which other can not do by the sword and for their aversensse in their heart against the union it be true indeed I protest they do never crave this union of i nor seek it either in private or the state by letter nor ever once do any of that nation press i forward or wish i to accelerate that business but on the other part they offer always to obey i when it shall come to they and all honest man that desire my greatness have be thus mind for the personal reverence and regard they bear unto my Perion and any of my reasonable and just desire I know there be many piggot among they I mean a number of seditious and discontent particular person as must be in all commonwealth that where they dare may peradventure talk lewd enough but no scottish man ever speak dishonourable of England in parliament for here must I note unto you the difference of the two parliament in these two kingdom for there they must not speak without the chauncellors leave and if any man do propound or utter any seditious or uncomely speech he be straight interrupt and silence by the chauncellors authority where as here the liberty for any man to speak what he listen and as long as he listen be the only cause he be not interrupt it have be object that there be a great antipathy of the law and custom of these two nation it be much mistake for Scotland have no common law as here but the law they have be of three sort all the law of Scotland for tenor ward and livery signory and land be draw out of the chancery of England and for matter of equity and in many thing else differ from you but in certain term james the first breed here in England bring the law thither in a write hand the second be statute law which be their act of parliament wherein they have power as you to make and altar law and those may be look into by you for I hope you shall be no more stranger to that nation and the principal work of this union will be to reconcile the statute law of both kingdom the three be the civil law james the five bring it out of France by establish the session there according to the form of the court of parliament of France which he have see in the time of his be there who occupy there the place of civil udge in all matter of plea or controversy yet not to govern absolute by the civil law as in France for if a man plead that the law of the nation be otherwise it be a bar to the civil and a good chancellor or precedent will oftentimes repel and put to silence a argument that the lawyer bring out of the civil law where they have a clear solution in their own Law so as the civil law in Scotland be admit in no other case but to supply such case wherein the municipal law be defective then may you see it be not so hard a matter as be think to reduce that country to be unite with you under this law which neither be subject to the civil law nor yet have any old common law of their own but such as in effect be borrow from you and for their statute law in parliament you may alter and change they as oft as occasion shall require as you do here it have likewise be object as a other impediment that in the parliament of Scotland the king have not anegative voice but must pass all the law agree on by the lord and commons of this I can best resolve you for I be the elder parliament man in Scotland and have sit in more parliament than any of my predecessor I can assure you that the form of parliament there be nothing incline to popularity about a twenty day or such a time before the parliament proclamation be make throughout the kingdom to deliver in to the king clerk of register who you here call the master of the roll all bill to be exhibit that session before a certain day then be they bring unto the king and peruse and consider by he and only such as I allow of be put into the chancellor hand to be propound to the parliament and none other and if any man in parliament speak of any other matter than be in this form first allow by i the chancellor tell he there be no such bill allow by the King beside when they have pass they for law they be present unto i and I with my sceptre put into my hand by the chancellor must say I ratify and approve all thing do in this present parliament and if there be any thing that I dislike they raze it out before if this may be call a negative voice than I have one I be sure in that parliament the last impediment be the french liberty which be think so great as except the scot farsake France England can be unite to they if the scottish nation will be so unwilling to leave they as be say it will not lie in their hand for the league be never make between the people as be mistake but betwixt the prince only and their crown the beginning be by a message from a king of France Charlemagne I take it but I can certain remember unto a king of Scotland for a league defensive and offensive between we and they against England France be at that time in war with England the like at that time be then desire by England against France who also send their ambassador to Scotland at the first the disputation be long maintain in favour of England that they be our near neighbour join in one continent and a strong and powerful nation it be more fit for the weal and security of the state of Scotland to be in league and amity with they then with a country though never so strong yet divide by sea from we especial englandly betwixt we and they where we may be sure of a sudden mischief but behoove to abide the hazard of wind and weather and other accident that may hinder our relief but after when the contrary part of the argument be maintain wherein allegation be make that England ever seek to conquer Scotland and therefore in regard of their pretend interest in the kingdoome will never keep any sound amity with they long than they see their advantage whereas France lie more remote and claim no interest in the kingdom will therefore be find a more constant and faithful friend it be unhappy conclude in favour of the last party through which occasion Scotland gate many mischief after and it be by the very tenor thereof order to be renew and confirm from king to king successive which according be ever perform by the mediation of their ambassador and therefore mere personal and so be it renew in the queen my mother time only between the two king and not by assent of parliament or convention of the three estate which it can never have want if it have be a league between the people and in my time when it come to be ratify because it appear to be in it be by i leave unrenew or confirm as a thing incompatible to my person in consideration of my title to this crown some privilege indeed in the merchant favour for point of commerce be renew and confirm in my time wherein for my part of it there be scarce three counselor more than my secretary to who place it belong that meddle in that matter it be true that it behoove to be entertain as they call it in the court of parliament of Paris but that only serve for publication and not to give it authority that parliament as you know be but a judicial seat of judge and lawyer and nothing agree with the definition or office of our parliament in this isle and therefore that any fruit or privilege possess by the league with France be able now to remain in Scotland be impossible for you may be sure that the french king stay only upon the sight of the end of this union to cut it off himself otherwise when this great work be at a end I will be force for the general care I owe to all my subject to crave of France like privilege to they all as Scotland already enjoy see the personal friendship remain as great between we as between our progenitor and all my subject must be alike dear unto i which either he will never grant and so all will fall to the ground or else it will turn to the benefit of the whole island and so the scottish privilege can hold long than my league with France last and for another argument to prove that this league be only between the king and not between the people they which have pension or be privy intelligence giver in France without my leave be in no better case by the law of Scotland then if they be pensioner to Spain as for the scottish guard in France the beginning thereof be when a earl of Boghan be send in aid of the french with ten thousand man and there be make constable and have obtain a victory be murder with the most of the scottish army in recompense whereof and for a future security to the scottish nation the scottish guard be ordain to have the privilege and prerogative before all other guard in guard the king person and as for the last point of this subdivision concern the gain that England may make by this union I think no wise nor honest man will ask any such question for who be so ignorant that do not know the gain will be great do you not gain by the union of Wales and be not Scotland great than Wales shall not your dominion be increase of land sea and person add to your greatness and be not your land and sea adjoin for who can set down the limit of the border but as a mathematical line or idea then will that back door be shut and those port of janus be for ever close you shall have they that be your enemy to molest you a sure back to defend you their body shall be your aid and they must be partner in all your quarrel two snowball put together make one the great two house join make one the large two castle wall make in one make one as thick and strong as both and do you not see in the low country how available the english and the scottish be be join together this be a point so plain as no man that have wit or honesty but must acknowledge it feel and where it be object that the scottishman be not tie to the service of the king in the war above forty day it be a ignorant mistake for the truth be that in respect the king of Scotland do not so abound in treasure and money to take up a army under pay as the king of England do therefore be the scottish army wont to be raise only by proclamation upon the penalty of their breach of allegiance so as they be all force to come to the war like snail who carry their house about with they every nobleman and gentleman bring with he their tent money provision for their house victual of all sort and all other necessary the king supply they of nothing necessity thereupon enforce a warning to be give by the proclamation of the space of their attendance without which they can not make their provision according especial as long as they be within the bound of Scotland where it be not lawful for they to help themselves by the spoil or waste of the country but neither be there any law prescribe precise such a certain number of day nor yet be it without the limit of the king power to keep they together as many more day as he listen to renew his proclamation from time to time some reasonable number of day before the expire of the former they be ever bind to serve and wait upon he though it be a hundred year if need be now to conclude I be glad of this occasion that I may you be now to recede when you meet again remember I pray you the truth and sincerity of my meaning which in seek union be only to advance the greatness of your empire seat here in England and yet with such caution I wish it as may stand with the weal of both state what be now desire have oft before be seek when it can not be obtain to refuse it now then be double iniquity strengthen your own felicity London must be the seat of your king and Scotland join to this kingdom by a golden conquest but cyment with love as I say before which within will make you strong against all civil and intestine rebellion as without we will be compass and guard with our wall of brass judge i charitable since in this I seek your equal good that so both of you may be make fearful to your enemy powerful in yourselves and available to your friend study therefore hereafter to make a good conclusion avoid all delay cut off all vain question that your king may have his lawful desire and be not disgrace in his just end and for your security in such reasonable point of restriction whereunto I be to agree you need never doubt of my inclination for I will not say any thing which I will not promise nor promise any thing which I will notsweare what I swear I will sign and what I sign I shall with God grace ever perform a speech to the lord and Commons of the parliament at Whitehall on Wednesday the 21 of march 1609. we be now in the midst of this season appoint for penitence and prayer it have so fall out that these two last day have be spend in a far other sort of exercise I mean in eucharisticke sacrifice and gratulation of thanks present unto i by both the part of this body of parliament and therefore to make up the number of three which be the number of Trinity and perfection I have think good to make this the three day to be spend in this exercise as you make i a fair present indeed in present your thanks and love duty unto i so have I now call you here to recompense you again with a great and a rare present which be a fair and a crystal mirror not such a mirror wherein you may see your own face or shadow but such a mirror or crystal as through the transparantnesse thereof you may see the heart of your King the philosopher wish that every man breast be a crystal wherethrough his heart may be see be vulgar know and I touch it in one of my former speech unto you but though that be impossible in the general yet will I now perform this for my part that as it be a true axiom in divinity that be so will I now set I know that I can say nothing at this time whereof some of you that be here have not at one time or other hear i say the like already yet as corporal food nourish and maintain the body so do nourish and maintain memory I will reduce to three general and main ground the principal thing that have be agitate in this parliament and whereof I will now speak first the errand for which you be call by i and that be for support of my state and necessity the second be that which the people be to move unto the king to represent unto he such thing whereby the subject be vex or wherein the state of the common wealth be to be redress and that be the thing which you call grievance the three ground that have be handle among you and not only in talk among you in the parliament but even in many other people mouth aswell within as without the parliament be of a high nature than any of the former though it be but a incident and the reason be because it concern a high point and this be a doubt which have be in the head of some of my intention in two thing first whether I be resolve in the general to continue still my government according to the ancient form of this state and the law of this kingdom or if I have a intention not to limit myself within those bound but to alter the same when I think convenient by the absolute power of a King the other branch be anent the common law which some have a conceit I dislike and in respect that I be bear where another form of law be establish that I will have wish the civil law to have be put in place of the common law for government of this people and the complaint make among you of a book write by doctor cowel be a part of the occasion of this incident but as touch my censure of that book I make it already to be deliver unto you by the treasurer here sit which he do out of my own direction and note and what he say in my name that have he direct from i but what he speak of himself therein without my direction I shall always make good for you may be sure I will be loath to make so honest a man a liar or deceive your expectation always within very few day my edict shall come forth anent that matter which shall full discover my meaning there be never any reason to move man to think that I can like of such ground for there be two quality principal or rather privation that make king subject to flattery credulity and ignorance and I hope none of they can be just object to i for if Alexander the great for all his learning have be wise in that point to have consider the state of his own natural body and disposition he will never have think himself a god and now to the matter as it be a Christian duty in every man and not to be ashamed to give a account of his profession before man and angel as oft as occasion shall require so do I ever hold it a necessity of honour in a just and wise king though not to give a account to his people of his action yet clear to deliver his heart and intention unto they upon every occasion but I must invert my order and begin first with that incident which be last in my division though high of nature and so go backward the state of monarchy be the supreme thing upon earth for king be not only God lieutenant upon earth and sit upon God throne but even by GOD himself they be call god there be three principal similitude that illustrate the state of monarchy one take out of the word of GOD and the two other out of the ground of policy and philosophy in the scripture king be call god and so their power after a certain relation compare to the divine power king be also compare to father of family for a king be true the politic father of his people and last king be compare to the head of this microcosm of the body of man king be just call god for that they exercise a manner or resemblance of divine power upon earth for if you will consider the attribute to God you shall see how they agree in the person of a King God have power to create or destroy make or unmake at his pleasure to give life or send death to judge all and to be judge nor accountable to none to raise low thing and to make high thing low at his pleasure and to God be both soul and body due and the like power have king they make and unmake their subject they have power of raise and cast down of life and of death judge over all their subject and in all cause and yet accountable to none but God only they have power to exalt low thing and abase high thing and make of their subject like man at the chess a pawn to take a bishop or a knight and to cry up or down any of their subject as they do their money and to the king be due both the affection of the soul and the service of the body of his subject and therefore that reverend bishop here among you though I hear that by diverse he be mistake or not well understand yet do he preach both learned and true this point concern the power of a king for what he speak of a king power in be most true in divinity for to emperor or king that be monarch their subject body and good be due for their defence and maintenance but if I have be in his place I will only have add two word which will have clear all for after I have tell as a divine what be due by the subject to their king in general I will then have conclude as a Englishman show this people that as in general all subject be bind to relieve their king so to exhort they that as we live in a settle state of a kingdom which be govern by his own fundamental law and order that according thereunto they be now be assemble for this purpose in parliament to consider how to help such a king as now they have and that according to the ancient form and order establish in this kingdom put so a difference between the general power of a king in divinity and the settle and establish state of this crown and kingdom and I be sure that the bishop mean to have do the same if he have not be strait by time which in respect of the greatness of the presence preach before i and such a auditory he dare not presume upon as for the father of a family they have of old under the law of nature which be over their child or family I mean such father of family as be the lineal heir of those family whereof king do original come for king have their first original from they who plant and spread themselves in colony through the world now a father may dispose of his inheritance to his child at his pleasure yea even disinherit the elder upon just occasion and prefer the young according to his like make they beggar or rich at his pleasure restrain or banish out of his presence as he find they give cause of offence or restore
