they in favour again with the penitent sinner so may the king deal with his subject and last as for the head of the natural body the head have the power of direct all the member of the body to that use which the judgement in the head think most convenient it may apply sharp cure or cut off corrupt member let blood in what proportion it think fit and as the body may spare but yet be all this power ordain by God for although God have power aswell of destruction as of creation or maintenance yet will it not agree with the wisdom of God to exercise his power in the destruction of nature and overturn the whole frame of thing since his creature be make that his glory may thereby be the better express so be he a foolish father that will disinherit or destroy his child without a cause or leave off the careful education of they and it be a idle head that will in place of physic so poison or phlebotomize the body as may breed a dangerous distemper or destruction thereof but now in these our time we be to distinguish between the state of king in their first original and between the state of settle king and monarch that do at this time govern in civil kingdom for even as God during the time of the old testament speak by oracle and wrought by miracle yet how soon it please he to settle a church which be buy and redeem by the blood of his only son Christ then be there a cessation of both he ever after govern his people and church within the limit of his reveiledwill so in the first original of king whereof some have their beginning by conquest and some by election of the people their will at that time serve for law yet how soon kingdom begin to be settle in civility and policy than do king set down their mind by law which be proper make by the king only but at the rogation of the people the king grant be obtain thereunto and so the king become to be after a sort bind himself by a double oath to the observation of the fundamental law of his kingdom tacit as by be a king and so bind to protect aswell the people as the law of his kingdom and express by his oath at his coronation so as every just king in a settle kingdom be bind to observe that paction make to his people by his law in frame his government agreeable thereunto according to that paction which God make with Noah after the deluge hereafter seedtime and harvest cold and heat summer and winter and day and night shall not cease so long as the earth remain and therefore a king govern in a settle kingdom leave to be a king and degenerate into a tyrant assoon as he leave off to rule according to his law in which case the king conscience may speak unto he as the poor widow say to Philip of Macedon either govern according to your law and though no christian man ought to allow any rebellion of people against their prince yet do God never leave king unpunished when they transgress these limit for in that same psalm where God say to king he immediate thereafter conclude but you shall die like man the high we be place the great shall our fall be the tall the tree be the more in danger of the wind and the tempest beat sore upon the high mountain therefore all king that be not tyrant or perjure will be glad to bound themselves within the limit of their law and they that persuade they the contrary be viper and pest both against they and the commonwealth for it be a great difference between a king government in a settle state and what king in their original power may do in as for my part I thank God I have ever give good proof that I never have intention to the contrary and I be sure to go to my grave with that reputation and comfort that never king be in all his time more careful to have his law due observe and himself to govern thereafter then i. I conclude then this point touch the power of king with this axiom of divinity that as to dispute what God may do be blasphemy but that divine may lawful and do ordinary dispute and discuss for to dispute be both against logic and divinity so be it sedition in subject to dispute what a king may do in the height of his power but just king will ever be willing to declare what they will do if they will not incur the curse of God I will not be content that my power be dispute upon but I shall ever be willing to make the reason appear of all my do and rule my action according to my law the other branch of this incident be concern the common law be conceive by some that I contemn it and prefer the civil law thereunto as I have already say king action even in the secret place be as the action of those that be set upon the stage or on the top of house and I hope never to speak that in private which I shall not avow in public and print it if need be as I say in my DORON for it be true that within these few day I speak free my mind touch the common law in my privy chamber at the time of my dinner which be come to all your ear and the same be likewise relate unto you by my treasurer and now I will again repeat and confirm the same myself unto you first as a king I have least cause of any man to dislike the common law for no law can be more favourable and advantageous for a king and extend further his prerogative than it do and for a king of England to despile the common law it be to neglect his own crown it be true that I do great esteem the civil law the profession thereof serve more for general learning and be most necessary for matter of treaty with all foreign nation and I think that if it shall be take away it will make a entry to barbarism in this kingdom and will blemish the honour of England for it be in a manner and maintain intercourse with all forreme nation but I only allow it to have course here according to those limit of jurisdiction which the common law itself do allow it and therefore though it be not fit for the general government of the people here it do not follow it shall be extinct no more then because the latin tongue be not the mother or radical language of any nation in the world at this time that therefore the english tongue shall only now be learn in this kingdom which be to bring in barbarism my meaning therefore be not to prefer the civil law before the common law but only that it shall not be extinguish and yet so bound I mean to such court and cause as have be in ancient use as the ecclesiastical court court of admiralty court of request and such like reserve ever to the common law to meddle with the fundamental law of this kingdom either concern the king prerogative or the possession of subject in any question either between the king and any of they or among themselves in the point of for it be true that there be no kingdom in the world not only Scotland but not France nor Spain nor any other kingdom govern mere by the civil law but every one of they have their own municipal law agreeable to their custom as this kingdom have the common law nay I be so far from disallow the common law as I protest that if it be in my hand to choose a new law for this kingdom I will not only prefer it before any other Nationall law but even before the very judicial law of Moses and yet I speak no blasphemy in prefer it for conveniency to this kingdom and at this time to the very law of God for God govern his select people by these three law ceremonial moral and judicial the judicial be only fit for a certain people and a certain time which can not serve for the general of all other people and time as for example if the law of hang for theft be turn here to restitution of treble or quadruple as it be in the law of Moses what will become of all the middle shire and all the irishrie and highlander but the main point be that if the fundamental law of any kingdom shall be alter who shall discern what be or how shall a king govern it will be like the gregorian calendar which destroy the old and yet do this new trouble all the debt and account of traffic and merchandise nay by that account I can never tell my own age for now be my birthday remove by the space often day near i than it be before the change but upon the other part though I have in one point prefer our common law concern our use to the very law of GOD yet in another respect I must say both our law and all law else be far inferior to that judicial law of GOD for no book nor law be perfect nor free from corruption except only the book and law of god. and therefore I can wish some three thing special to be purge and clear in the common law but always by the advice of parliament for the king with his parliament here be absolute as I understand in make or form of any sort of law first I can wish that it be write in our vulgar language for now it be in a old mix and corrupt language only understand by lawyer whereas every subject aught to understand the law under which he live for since it be our plea against the papist that the language in God service ought not to be in a unknown tongue according to the rule in the law of Moses that the law shall be write in the fringe of the priest garment and shall be public read in the ear of all the people so i think aught our law to be make as plain as can be to the people that the excuse of ignorance may be take from they for conform themselves thereunto next our common law have not a settle text in all case be chief ground either upon old custom or else upon the report and case of judge which you call the like whereof be in all other law for they be much rule by precedent save only in Denmark and Norway where the letter of the law resolve all doubt without any trouble to the judge but though it be true that no text of law can be so certain wherein the circumstance will not make a variation in the case for in this age man wit increase so much by civility that the circumstance of every particular case vary so much from the general text of law as in the civil law itself there be therefore so many doctor that comment upon the text and never a one almost agree with another otherwise there need no judge but the bare letter of the Law yet can I wish that some more certainty be set down in this case by parliament for since the very report themselves be not always so bind but that diverse time judge do disclaim they and recede from the judgement of their predecessor it be good that upon a mature deliberation the exposition of the law be set down by act of parliament and such report therein confirm as be think fit to serve for law in all time hereafter and so the people shall not depend upon the bare opinion of judge and uncertain report and last there be in the common law diverse contrary report and precedent and this corruption do likewise concern the statute and act of parliament in respect there be diverse cross and cuff statute and some so pen as they may be take in diverse yea contrary sense and therefore will I wish both those statute and report aswell in the parliament as common law to be once mature review and reconcile and that not only all contrariety shall be scrape out of our book but even that such penal statute as be make but for the use of the time from breach whereof no man can be free which do not now agree with the condition of this our time may likewise beleft out of our book which under a tyrannous or avaritious king can not be endure and this reformation may i think be make a worthy work and well deserve a parliament to be set of purpose for it I know now that be upon this point of the common law you look to hear my opinion concern prohibition and I be not ignorant that I have be think to be a enemy to all prohibition and a utter stayer of they but I will short now inform you what have be my course in proceed therein it be true that in respect of diverse honourable court and jurisdiction plant in this kingdom I have often wish that every court have his own true limit and jurisdiction clear set down and certain know which if it be exceed by any of they or that any of they encroach one upon another than I grant that a prohibition in that case be to go out of the king bench but chieflie out of the chancery for other bench I be not yet so well resolve of their jurisdiction in that point and for my part I be never against prohibition of this nature nor the true use of they which be indeed to keep every river within his own bank and channel but when I see the swell and overflow of prohibition in a far great abundance than ever before every court strive to bring in most moulture to their own mill by multitude of cause which be a disease very natural to all court and jurisdiction in the world then deal I with this cause and that at two several time once in the midst of winter and again in the midst of the next follow summer at every of which time I spend three whole day in that labour and then after a large hear I tell they as Christ say concern marriage for as God contain the sea within his own bound and march as it be in the psalm so be it my office to make every court contain himself within his own limit and therefore I give admonition to both side to the other court that they shall be careful hereafter every of they to contain themselves within the bound of their own jurisdiction and to the court of common law that they shall not be so forward and prodigal in multiply their prohibition two caution I will they to observe in grant their prohibition first that they shall be grant in a right and lawful form and next that they shall not grant they but upon a just and reasonable cause as to the form it be that none shall be grant by any one particular judge or in time of vacation or in any other place but open in Court and to this the judge themselves give their willing assent and as to the cause that they shall not be grant upon every sleight furmise or information of the party but always that a due and grave examination shall first precede otherwise if prohibition shall rash and heady be grant than no man be the more secure of his own though he have get a sentence with he for as good have no law or sentence as to have no execution thereof a poor minister with much labour and expense have exhaust his poor mean and be force to forbear his study and to become resident from his flock obtain a sentence and then when he look to enjoy the fruit thereof he be defraud of all by a prohibition according to the parable of Christ that night when he think himself most happy shall his soul be take from he and so be he torture like Tantalus who when he have the apple at his mouth and that he be gape and open his mouth to receive it then must it be pull from he by a prohibition and he not suffer to taste thereof so as to conclude this point I put a difference between the true use of prohibition and the superintend abuse thereof for as a thing which be good ought not therefore be abuse so ought not the lawful use of a good thing be forbear because of the abuse thereof now the second general ground whereof I be to speak concern the matter of grievance there be two special cause of the people present grievance to their king in time of parliament first for that the king can at other time be so well inform of all the grievance of his people as in time of parliament which be the representative body of the whole realm second the parliament be the high court of justice and therefore the fit place where diverse nature of grievance may have their proper remedy by the establishment of good and wholesome law but though my speech be before direct to the whole body of parliament yet in this case I must address my speech in special to you of the low house I be now then to recommend unto your consideration the matter and manner of your handle and present of grievance as for the manner though I will not deny but that you represent the body of the people may as it be both and I mean either in parliament as a body or out of parliament as private man present your grievance unto i yet will I have you to use this caution in your behaviour in this point which be that your grievance be not as it be greedy seek out by you or take up in the street as one say thereby to show a willingness that you will have a show make that there be many abuse in the government and many cause of complaint but that according to your first institution you shall only meddle with such grievance as yourselves do know have need of reformation or have information thereof in your country for which you serve and not so to multiply they as may make it noise among the people that all thing in the government be amiss and out of frame for even at the begin of this very session of parliament the general name of grievance be mention among you such a conceit come in the head of many that you have a desire to multiply and make a great muster of they as every one exhibit what his particular spleen stir he unto indeed there fall out a accident upon this occasion for which I have reason to thank you of the low house I mean for your fire work wherein I confess you do honour to i and right to yourselves for have one afternoon find many grievance close present in paper and so all thrust up in a sack together rather like Pasquil than any lawful complaint far against your own order and diverse of they proceed from grudge and murmur spirit you upon the hear read two or three of the first line of diverse of they be not content with a public consent to condemn they and to discharge any further read of they but you also make a public bonfire of they in this I say you show your care and jealousy of my honour and I send you thanks for it by the chancellor of the exchequer a member of your own house who by your appointment that same night acquaint i with your proceed and by he also I promise at that time that you shall hear more of my thanks for the same at the first occasion and now I tell you it myself that you may know how kind I take your dutiful behaviour in this case but since this be a good effect of a evil cause I must not omit also to admonish you upon the other part to take a course among yourselves to prevent the like accident in all time hereafter otherwise the low house may become a place for Pasquil and at another time such grievance may be cast in among you as may contain treason or scandal against i or my posterity therefore in this case look over your ancient order and follow they and suffer not hereafter any petition or grievance to be deliver obscure or in the dark but open and avowed in your public house and there to be present to the speaker and as to the matter of your grievance I wish you here now to understand i right and because I see many writing and note I will crave your pardon to hold you a little long by speak the more distinct for fear of mistake first then I be not to find fault that you inform yourselves of the particular just grievance of the people nay I must tell you you can neither be just nor faithful to i or to your country that trust and employ you if you do it not for true plaint proceed not from the person employ but from the body represent which be the people and it may very well be that many direction and commission just give forth by i may be abuse in the execution thereof upon the people and yet I never to receive information except it come by your mean at such a time as this be as in the case of Stephen procter but I will wish you to be careful to avoid three thing in the matter of grievance first that you do not meddle with the main point of government that be my craft to meddle with that be to lesson i I be now a old king for six and thirty year have I govern in Scotland personal and now have I accomplish my apprenticeship of seven year here and seven year be a great time for a king experience in government therefore there will not be too many phormio to teach Hannibal I must not be teach my Office second I will not have you meddle with such ancient right of i as I have receive from my predecessor possess they more such thing I will be sorry shall be account for grievance all novelty be dangerous as well in a politic as in a natural body and therefore I will be loath to be quarrel in my ancient right and possession for that be to judge i unworthy of that which my predecessor have and leave i and last I pray you beware to exhibit for grievance any thing that be establish by a settle law and whereunto as you have already have a proof you know I will never give a plausible answer for it be a undutiful part in subject to press their king wherein they know beforehand he will refuse they now if any law or statute be not convenient let it be amend by parliament but in the mean time term it not a grievance for to be grieve with the law be to be grieve with the king who be swear to be the patron and maintainer thereof but as all man be flesh and may err in the execution of law so may you just make a grievance of any abuse of the law distinguish wise between the fault of the person and the thing itself as for example complaint may be make unto you of the high commissioner if so be try the abuse and spare not to complain upon it but say not there shall be no commission for that be to abridge the power that be in i and I will plain tell you that something I have with myself resolve that point which I mean ever to keep except I see other great cause which be that in regard the high commission be o^ so high a nature from which there be no appellation to any other court I have think good to restrain it only to the two archbishop where before it be common among a great part of the bishop in England this law I have set to myself and therefore you may be assure that I will never find fault with any man nor think he the more Puritan that will complain to i out of parliament aswell as in parliament of any error in execution thereof so that he prove it otherwise it be but a calumny only I will be loath that any man shall grieve at the commission itself as I have already say you have hear I be sure of the pain I take both in the cause of the admiralty and of the prohibition if any man therefore will bring i any just complaint upon any matter of so high a nature as this be you may assure yourselves that I will not spare my labour in hear it in faith you never have a more painful king or that will be ready in his person to determine cause that be fit for his hear and when ever any of you shall make experience of i in this point you may be sure never to want access nor you shall never come wrong to i in or out of parliament and now to conclude this purpose of grievance I have one general grievance to commend unto you and that in the behalf of the country from whence you come and this be to pray you to beware that your grievance savour not of particular man thought but of the general grief rise out of the mind of the people and not out of the humour of the propounder and therefore I will wish you to take heed careful and consider of the party that propound the grievance for you may if you listen easy discern whether it be his own passion or the people grief that make he to speak for many a man will in your house propound a grievance out of his own humour because peradventure he account high of that matter and yet the country that employ he may perhaps either be of a contrary mind or at least little care for it as for example I assure you I can very well smell between a petition that move from a general grievance or such a one as come from the spleen of some particular person either against ecclesiastical government in general or the person of any one noble man or commissioner in particular and now the three point remain to be speak of which be the cause of my call of this parliament and in this I have do but as I use to do in all my life which be to leave my own errand hindmost it may be you do wonder that I do not speak unto you public at the begin of this session of parliament to tell you the cause of your call as I do if I be right remember in every session before but the truth be that because I call you at this time for my particular errand I think it fit to be open unto you by my treasurer who be my public and most principal officer in matter of that nature then that I shall do it myself for I confess I be less natural eloquent and have great cause to distrust my elocution in matter of this nature then in any other thing I have make my treasurer already to give you a very clear and true account both of my have and expense a favour I confess that king do seldom bestow upon their subject in make they so particular acquaint with their state if I have not more than cause you may be sure I will be loath to trouble you but what he have affirm in this upon the honour of a gentleman who you never have cause to distrust for his honesty that do I now confirm and avow to be true in the word and honour of a king and therein you be bind to believe i duty I may just claim of you as my subject and one of the branch of duty which subject owe to their sovereign be supply but in what quantity and at what time that must come of your love I be not now therefore to dispute of a king power but to tell you what I may just crave and expect with your good will I be ever against all extreme and in this case I will likewise wish you to avoid they on both side for if you fail in the one I may have great cause to blame you as parliament man be call by i for my errand and if you fall into the other extreme by supply of my necessity without respective care to avoid oppression or partiality in the levy both I and the country will have cause to blame you when I think upon the composition of this body of parliament I do well consider that the upper house be compose of the secular nobility who be hereditary lord of parliament and of bishop that be live Renter baron of the same and therefore what be give by the upper house be give only from the true body of that house and out of their own purpose that do give it whereas the low house be but the representative body of the commons and so what you give you give it aswell for other as for yourselves and therefore you have the more reason to eschew both the extreme on the one part you may the more easy be liberal since it come not all from yourselves and yet upon the other part if you give more than be fit for good and love subject to yield upon such necessary occasion you abuse the king and hurt the people and such a gift I will never accept for in such a case you may deceive a king in give your flatter consent to that which you know may move the people general to grudge and murmur at it and so shall the king find himself deceive in his caloule and the people likewise grieve in their heart the love and possession of which I protest I do and ever will account the great earthly security next the favour of GOD to any wise or just King for though it be vain say by one of your house that you have need to beware that by give i too much your throat be not in danger of cut at your come home yet may you assure yourselves that I will ever be loath to press you to do that which may wrong the people and make you just to bear the blame thereof but that you may the better be acquaint with my inclination I will appeal to a number of my privy council here present if that before the call of this parliament and when I find that the necessity of my estate require so great a supply they find i more desirous to obtain that which I be force to seek then careful that the people may yield i a supply in so great a measure as my necessity require without their too great loss and you all that be parliament man and here present of both house can bear i witness if ever I burden or employ any of you for any particular subsidy or sum by name further than my lay open the particular necessity of my state or yet if ever I speak to any privy counsellor or any of my learned council to labour voice for i to this end I ever detest the hunt for a king that will rule and govern just must have regard to conscience honour and judgement in all his great action as yourself m. speaker remember the other day and therefore you may assure yourselves that I ever limitall my great action within that compass but as upon the one side I do not desire you shall yield to that extreme in give i more than as I say former upon such necessary occasion be fit for good and love subject to yield for that be to give i a purse with a knife so on the other side I hope you will not make vain pretence of want out of causeless apprehension or idle excuse neither cloak your own humour when yourselves be unwilling by allege the poverty of the people for although I will be no less just as a king to such person than any other for my justice with God grace shall be alike open to all yet you must think I have no reason to thank they or gratify they with any suit or matter of grace when their errand shall come in my way and yet no man can say that ever I quarrel any man for refuse i a subsidy if he do it in a moderate fashion and with good reason for he that deny a good law I will not spare to quarrel but for grant or deny money it be but a effect of love and therefore for the point of my necessity I only desire that I be not refuse in that which of duty I ought to have for I know if it be propound in the general among you whether the king want ought to be relieve or not there be not one of you that will make question of it and though in a sort this may seem to be my particular yet it can not be divide from the general good of the common wealth for the king that be tell you of his want nay by he speak unto you for if the king want the state want and therefore the strengthen of the king be the preservation and the stand of the state and woe be to he that divide the weal of the king from the weal of the kingdom and as that king be miserable how rich soever he be that rain over a poor people for the heart and riches of the people be the king great treasure so be that kingdom not able to subsist how rich and potent soever the people be if their king want mean to mainaine his state for the mean of your king be the sinew of the kingdom both in war and peace for in peace I must minister justice unto you and in war I must defend you by arm but neither of these can I do without sufficient mean which must come from your aid and supply I confess it be far against my nature to be burdensome to my people for it can but grieve i to crave of other that be bear to be beg of it be true I crave more than ever king of England do but I have far great and just cause and reason to crave than ever king of England have and though my treasurer have at length declare the reason unto you of my necessity and of a large supply that he crave for the same wherein he omit no argument that can be use for that purpose yet will I myself now short remember you some of the weighty reason that come in my head to prove the equity of my demand first you all know that by the accession of more crown which in my person I have bring unto you my charge must be the great in all reason for the great your king be both in his dominion and number of subject he can but be force thereby to be at the more charge and it be the more your honour so to have it next that posterity and issue which it have please God to send i for your use can but bring necessary with it a great proportion of charge you all know that the late queen of famous memory notwithstanding her orbity have much give unto she and more than ever any of her predecessor have before she three the time of creation of my son do now draw near which I choose for the great honour to be do in this time of parliament as for he I say no more the sight of himself here speak for he four it be true I have spend much but yet if I have spare any of those thing which cause a great part of my expense I shall have dishonour the kingdom myself and the late queen shall I have spare the funeral of the late queen or the solemnity of i and my wife entry into this kingdom in some honourable sort or shall I have spare our entry into London or our coronation and when most of the monarch and great prince in Christendom send their ambassador to congratulate my come hither and some of they come in person be I not bind both for my own honour and the honour of the kingdom to give they good entertainment but in case it may be object by some that it be only upon occasion of war that king obtain great supply from their subject notwithstanding my intern peace I be yet in a kind of war which if it be without the more be your safety for as the treasurer tell you at large I be now force both in respect of state and my promise and for the general cause of religion to send a supply of force to cleves and how long that occasion may last or what great supply the necessity of that errand may draw i unto no man can yet tell beside that although I have put down that form of warlike keep of Berwick yet be all those commander my pensioner that be the late queen soldier and I hope I sustain a pretty seminary of soldier in my fort within this kingdom beside the two cautionary town in the low-countries Flushing and Brill and as for Ireland you all know how uncertain my charge be ever there that people be so easy stir partly through their barbarity and want of civility and partly through their corruption in religion to break forth in rebellion you know how unlooked for a rebellion break forth there the last year which can not but put i to extraordinary charge beside I do maintain there continual a army which be a goodly seminary of expert and old soldier and I dare never suffer the same to be diminish till this plantation take effect which no doubt be the great moat that ever come in the rebel eye and it be to be look for that if ever they will be able to make any stir they will press at it by all mean for the prevent and discourage this plantation now it be true that beside all these honourable and necessary occasion of my charge I have spend much in liberality but yet I hope you will consider that what I have give have be give among you and so what come in from you go out again among you but it may be think that I have give much among scottishman indeed if I have not be liberal in reward some of my old servant of that nation you can never have have reason to expect my thankfulness towards any of you that be more late become my subject if I have be ingrate to the old and yet you will find that I have deal twice as much among english man as I have do to scottishman and therefore he that in your house be not ashamed to affirm that the silver and gold do so abound in Edinburgh be very far mistake but I wish he no worse punishment then that he shall only live upon such profit of the money there but I hope you will never mislike i for my liberality since I can look very few of you this day in the face that have not make suit to i at least for some thing either of honour or profit it be true a king liberality must never be dry up altogether for than he can never maintain nor oblige his servant and well deserve subject but that vastness of my expense be past which I use the first two or three year after my come hither and as I oft use to say that Christmas and open tide be end for at my first come here partly ignorance of this state which no man can acquire but by time and experience and partly the form of my come be so honourable and miraculous enforce i to extend my liberality so much the more at the beginning you see I make knight then by hundreth and baron in great number but I hope you find I do not so now nor mind not to do so hereafter for to conclude this point anent expense I hold that a king expense must always be honourable though not wasteful and the charge of your king in maintain those ancient honourable form of live that the former king of England my predecessor have do and his live to be rule according to the proportion of his greatness be aswell for the honour of your kingdom as of your King now this can be supply out of the air or liquid element but must come from the people and for remove of that diffidence which man may have that I mind not to live in any wasteful sort hereafter will you but look upon myself and my posterity and if there be no more but that it will teach you that if I be but a natural man I must needs be careful of my expense for as for my own person I hope none that know i well can think i but as little incline to any prodigal humour of unnecessary thing as any other reasonable man of a far mean estate therefore since as I have say I can be help but from the people I assure myself that you will well allow i such measure of supply as the people may bear and support he with more honourable mean than other have have that as I may say without vaunt have bring you more honour than ever you have for I hope there be no good subject either within or out of the parliament house that will not be content for set straight once and settle the honourable state of their king to spare so much every one of they out of their purse which peradventure they will in one night throw away at die or card or bestow upon a horse for their fancy that may break his neck or his leg the next morning nay I be sure every good subject will rather choose to live more spare upon his own then that his king state shall be in want for conclusion then of this purpose I wish you now to put a speedy end to your business freeness in give grace the gift bis the long I want help the great will my debt still rise and so must I look for the great help and now I will pray you to turn your eye with i from home and look upon foreign state consider that the eye of all foreign state be upon this affair and in expectation what the success thereof will be and what can they think if you depart without relieve i in that proportion that may make i able to maintain my state but that either you be unwilling to help i think i unworthy thereof or at least that my state be so desperate as it can be repair and so that the parliament part in disgrace with the king and the king in distaste with the parliament which can but weaken my reputation both at home and abroad for of this you may be assure that foreign prince care the more one for a other if they may have reason to expect that they may be able to do they good or harm in retribution and you know that if a king fall to be contemn with his neighbour that can but bring a oppression and war by they upon he and then will it be too late to support the king when the cure be almost desperate thing foresee and prevent be ever easlie remedy and therefore I will advise you now so to settle your business as you may not take in hand so many thing at once as may both cross my errand and every one of they across another you remember the french proverb embrace estreint we be not in this parliament to make our testament as if we shall never meet again and that all thing that be to be do in any parliament be to be do at this time and yet for fill up of your vacant hour I will recommend to your consideration such nature of thing as be to be special think upon in these time first I will begin at GOD for the begin with he make all other action to be bless and this I mean by the cause of religion next I will speak of some thing that concern the commonwealth and three matter of pleasure and ornament to the kingdom as for religion we have all great cause to take heed unto it papist be wax as proud at this time as ever they be which make many to think they have some new plotin hand and although the poor sort of they be God be thank much decrease yet do the great sort of they daily increase especial among the feminine sex nay they be wax so proud that some say no man dare present they nor judge meddle with they they be so back and uphold by diverse great courtier it be a sure and better way to remove the material of fire before they be kindle then to quench the fire when once it be kindle I do not mean by this to move you to make strong law than be already make but see those law may be well execute that be in force otherwise they can but fall into contempt and become rusty I never find that blood and too much severity do good in matter of religion for beside that it be a sure rule in divinity that God never love to plant his church by violence and bloodshed natural reason may even persuade we and daily experience prove it true that when man be severe persecute for religion the gallantness of many man spirit and the wilfulness of their humour rather than the justness of the cause make they to take a pride bold to endure any torment or death itself to gain thereby the reputatiom of martyrdom though but in a false shadow some doubt have be conceive anent the use of the oath of allegiance and that part of the act which ordain the take thereof be think so obscure that no man can tell who ought to be press therewith for I myself when upon a time I call the judge before i at their go to their circuit I move this question unto they wherein as I think they can not resolute answer i and therefore if there be any scruple touch the minister of it I will wish it now to be clear and since I have with my own pen bring the pope quarrel upon i and proclaim public defiance to Babylon in maintain it shall it now sleep and shall I seem as it be to steal from it again as for recusant let they be all due present without exception for in time pass there have be too great a connivance and forbear of they especial of great man wife and their kin and follower none ought to be spare from be bring under the danger of law and then it be my part to use mercy as I think convenient to wink at fault and not to suffer they to be discover be no honour nor mercy in a king neither be he ever thank for it it only argue his dulness but to forgive fault after they be confess or try be mercy and now I must turn i in this case to you my lord the bishop and even exhort you earnest to be more careful than you have be that your officer may more due present recusant than heretofore they have do without exception of person that although it must be the work of GOD that must make their mind to be alter yet at least by this course they may be stay from increase or insult upon we and that you all may know the truth of my heart in this case I divide all my subject that be papist into two rank either old papist that be so bring up in time of popery like old queen Mary priest and those that though they be young in year yet have never drink in other milk but be still nuzzle in that blindness or else such as do become apostate have once be of our profession and have forsake the truth either upon discontent or practice or else upon a light vain humour of novelty make no more scruple to seek out new form of religion then if it be but a new form of garment or a new cut or curtsy after the french fashion for the former sort I pity they but if they be good and quiet subject I hate not their person and if I be a private man I can well keep a civil friendship and conversation with some of they but as for those apostate who I know must be great hater of their own sect I confess I can never show any favourable countenance towards they and they may all of they be sure without exception that they shall never find any more favour of i further than I must needs in justice afford they and these will I have the law to strike severely upon and you careful to discover you know there have be great stir keep for beg concealment these year past and I pray you let i beg this concealment both of the bishop and judge that papist be no long conceal next as concern the common wealth I do special recommend unto you the frame of some new statute for preservation of wood in the end of the last session of parliament you have a bill among you of that subject but because you find some fault therein you cast out the whole bill but I can have rather wish that you have either mend it or make a new one for to cast out the whole bill because of some fault be even as if a man that have a new garment bring he will choose rather to go naked then have his garment make fit for he but on my conscience I can imagine why you shall so light have esteem a thing so necessary for the common wealth if it be not out of a little frowardness among you at that time that what I then recommend earnest unto you it be the worse like of the maintenance of wood be a thing so necessary for this kingdom as it can stand nor be a kingdom without it for it concern you both in your and in pleasure your for without it you want the use of one of the most necessary element which be fire and fuel to dress your meat with for neither can the people live in these cold country if they want fire altogether nor yet can you dress your meat without it and I think you will ill live like the cannibal upon raw flesh for the education of this people be far from that as to your the decay of wood will necessary bring the decay of ship which both be the security of this kingdom since God have by nature make the sea to be the wall of this island and the rather now since God have unite it all in my person and crown as also by the decay of ship will you loose both all your foreign commodity that be fit for this country and the vent of our own which be the loss of trade that be a main pillar of this kingdom and as for pleasure you know my delight in hunt and hawk and many of yourselves be of the same mind and all this must needs decay by the decay of wood you have reason therefore to provide a good law upon this subject now as to the last point concern matter of pleasure it consist in the preserve of game which be now almost utter destroy through all the kingdom and if you offer not now a better law for this than be make in the last session of parliament I will never thank you for it for as for your law anent partridge and pheasant you have give leave to every man how poor a farmer that ever he be to take and destroy they in his own ground how he listen but I pray you how can the game be maintain if gentleman that have great lordship shall breed and preserve they there and so soon as ever they shall but fly over the hedge and light in a poor fellow close they shall all be destroy sure I know no remedy for preserve the game that breed in my ground except I cast a roof over all the ground or else put veruel to the partridge foot with my arm upon they as my hawk have otherwise I know not how they shall be know to be the king partridge when they light in a farmer close and by your law against steal of deer or coney after a long discourse and prohibition of steal they you conclude in the end with a restriction that all this punishment shall be understand to be use against they that steal the game in the night which have much encourage all the loose sort of people that it be no fault to steal dear so they do it not like thief in the night as be that law of the lacedaemonian against theft that do not forbid theft but only teach they to do it cunning and without discovery whereupon a foolish boy suffer a fox to gnaw his heart through his breast and this doctrine be like that lesson of the cannon law I know you think that I speak partial in this case like a hunter but there be never a one of you that hear i that care the least for the sport for preservation of the game but he will be as glad to have a pastry of venison if you may get it as the best hunter will and if the game be not preserve you can eat no venison as for partridge and pheasant I do not deny that gentleman shall have their sport and special upon their own ground but first I do not think such game and pleasure shall be free to base people and next I will even wish that gentleman shall use it in a gentlemanlike fashion and not with net or gun or such other ungentlemanlike fashion that serve but for utter destruction of all game nor yet to kill they at unseasonable time as to kill the pheasant and partridge when they be no big than mouse when as for every one their hawk kill ten will be destroy with their dog and horse foot beside the great and intolerable harm they do to corn in that season and now in the end of all this faschious speech I must conclude like a grey friar in speak for myself at last at the beginning of this session of parliament when the treasurer open my necessity unto you than my purse only labour but now that word be spread both at home and abroad of the demand I have make unto you my reputation labour aswella my purse for if you part without the repair of my state in some reasonable sort what can the world think but that the evil will my subject bear unto i have breed a refuse and you can never part so without apprehend that I be distaste with your behaviour and yet to be in fear of my displeasure but I assure and promise myself far otherwise thus have I now perform my promise in present unto you the crystal of your king heart you know that principal by three way you may wrong a mirror Frst I pray you look not upon my mirror with a false light which you do if you mistake or mis-understand my speech and so alter the sense thereof but second I pray you beware to soil it with a foul breath and unclean hand I mean that you pervert not my word by any corrupt affection turn they to a ill meaning like one who when he hear the toll of a bell fancy to himself that it speak those word which be most in his mind and last which be worst of all beware to let it fall or break for glass be brittle which you do if you light esteem it and by contemn it conform not yourselves to my persuasion to conclude then as all these three day of jubilee have fall in the midst of this season of penitence wherein you have present your thanks to i and I the like again to you so do I wish and hope that the end of this parliament will be such as we may all have cause both I your head and you the body to join in eucharisticke thanks and praise unto God for our so good and happy a end a speech in the star-chamber the 20 of june 1616. give thy judgement to the king o GOD and thy righteousness to the king son these be the first word of one of the psalm of the kingly prophet David whereof the literal sense run upon he and his son Solomon and the mystical sense upon GOD and CHRIST his eternal son but they be both so weave together as some part be and can only be proper apply unto GOD and CHRIST and other part unto David and Solomon as this verse give thy judgement to the king o God and thy righteousness to the king son can be proper speak of any but of David and his son because it be say give thy judgement etc. now God can give to himself in another part of the same psalm where it be say that righteousness shall flourish and abundance of peace as long as the moon endure it signify eternity and can be proper apply but to GOD and CHRIST but both sense aswell literal as mystical serve to king for imitation and especial to christian king for king sit in the throne of GOD and they themselves be call god and therefore all good king in their government must imitate GOD and his Christ in be just and righteous David and Solomon in be godly and wise to be wise be understand able to discern able to judge other to be godly be that the fountain be pure whence the stream proceed for what avail it though all his work be godly if they proceed not from godliness to be righteous be to a man self to be just be towards other but justice in a king avail not unless it be with a clean heart for except he be righteous aswell as just he be no good king and whatsoever justice he do except he do it for justice sake and out of the pureness of his own heart neither from private end vainglory or any other by-respect of his own all such justice be unrighteousness and no true justice from this imitation of GOD and CHRIST in who throne we sit the government of all commonwealth and especial monarchy have be from the begin settle and establish king be proper judge and judgement proper belong to they from GOD for king sit in the throne of GOD and thence all judgement be derive in all well settle monarchy where law be establish former and orderly there judgement be defer from the king to his subordinate magistrate not that the king take it from himself but give it unto they so it come not to they but as the shooleman speak the ground be ancient ever sithence that counsel which jethro give to Moses for after that Moses have govern a long time in his own person the burden grow so great have none to help he as his father in law come to visit he find he so cumber with minister of justice that neither the people be satisfy nor he well able to perform it therefore by his advice judge be depute for easy question and the great and more profound be leave to Moses and according to this establishment all king that have have a formal government especial christian king in all aage have govern their people though after a diverse manner this deputation be after one manner in France after another here and even my own kingdom differ in this point of government for Scotland differ both from France and England herein but all agree in this I speak of such kingdom or state where the formality of law have place that the king that sit in God throne only depute subaltern judge and he depute not one but a number for no one subaltern judge mouth make law and their office be to interpret law and administer justice but as to the number of they the form of government the manner of interpretation the distinction of bench the diversity of court these vary according to the variety of government and institution of diverse king so this ground I lie that the seat of judgement be proper god and king be god vicegerent and by king judge be depute under they to bear the burden of government according to the first example of Moses by the advice of jethro and sithence practise by David and Solomon the wise king that ever be which be in this psalm so interlace that as the first verse can be apply proper but to David and Solomon in the word give thy judgement to the king etc. so the other place in the same psalm righteousness shall flourish and abundance of peace shall remain as long as the moon endure proper signify the eternity of CHRIST this I speak to show what a near conjunction there be between God and the king upward and the king and his judge downward for the same conjunction that be between God and the king upward the same conjunction be between the king and his judge downward as king borrow their power from God so judge from king and as king be to account to God so judge unto God and king and both king and judge by imitation have two quality from God and his Christ and two quality from David and his Solomon judgement and righteousness from God and Christ godliness and wisdom from David and Solomon and as no king can discharge his account to God unless he make conscience not to alter but to declare and establish the will of God so judge can discharge their account to king unless they take the like care not to take upon they to make law but join together after a deliberate consultation to declare what the law be for as king be subject unto God law so they to man Law it be the king office to protect and settle the true interpretation of the law of God within his dominion and it be the judge office to interpret the law of the king whereto themselves be also subject have now perform this ancient proverb a jove which though it be speak by a pagan yet it be good and holy I be now to come to my particular errand for which I be here this day wherein I must handle two part first the reason why I have not these fourteen year sithence my coronation until now satisfy a great many of my love subject who I know have have a great expectation and as it be a long like they that be with child to hear i speak in this place where my predecessor have often sit and especial king Henry the seven from who as diverse way before I be lineal descend and that double to this crown and as I be near descend of he so do I desire to follow he in his best action the next part be the reason why I be now come the cause that make i abstain be this when I come into England although I be a old king past middle age and practise in government ever sithence I be twelve year old yet be here a stranger in government though not in blood because my breed be in another kingdom I resolve therefore with Pythagoras to keep silence seven year and learn myself the law of this kingdom before I will take upon i to teach they unto other when this apprenticeship be end than another impediment come which be in the choice of that cause that shall first bring i hither I expect some great cause to make my first entry upon for I think that have abstain so long it shall be a worthy matter that shall bring i hither now every cause must be great or small in small cause I think it disgraceful to come have be so long absent in great cause they must be either betwixt the king and some of his subject or betwixt subject and subject in a cause where myself be concern I be loath to come because man shall not think I do come for my own private either prerogative or profit or for any other by-respect and in that case I will always abide the trial of man and angel never to have have any particular end in that which be the main of all thing justice in a great cause also between party and party great in respect either of the question or value of the thing my come may seem as it be oblique to be in favour of one party and for that cause this counsellor or that courtier may be think to move i to come hither and a mean cause be not worthy of i especial for my first entrance so lack of choice in both respect keep i off till now and now have pass a double apprenticeship of twice seven year I be come hither to speak unto you and next as to the reason of my come at this time they be these I have observe in the time of my whole reign here and my double apprenticeship diverse thing fall out in the judicature here at Westminster hall that I think require and urge a reformation at my hand whereupon I resolve with myself that I can not more fit begin a reformation than here to make a open declaration of my meaning I remember Christ say my sheep hear my voice and so I assure myself my people will most willing hear the voice of i their own shepherd and king whereupon I take this occasion in my own person here in this seat of judgement not judicial but declaratory and open to give those direction which at other time by piecemeal I have deliver to some of you in diverse less public place but now will put it up in all your audience where I hope it shall be true carry and can be mistake as it may have be when it be speak more private I will for order sake take i to the method of the number of three the number of perfection and upon that number distribute all I have to declare to you first I be to give a charge to myself for a king or judge under a king that first give not a good charge to himself will never be able to give a good charge to his inferior for as I have say good river can flow but from good spring if the fountain be impure so must the river be second to the judge and three to the auditory and the rest of the inferior minister of justice first I protest to you all in all your audience here sit in the seat of justice belong unto GOD and now by right fall unto i that I have resolve as confirmation in majority follow baptism in minority so now after many year to renew my promise and oath make at my coronation concern justice and the promise therein for maintenance of the law of the land and I protest in God presence my care have ever be to keep my conscience clear in all the point of my oath take at my coronation so far as human frailty may permit i or my knowledge inform i I speak in point of justice and law for religion I hope I be reasonable well know already I mean therefore of law and justice and for law I mean the common law of the land according to which the king govern and by which the people be govern for the common law you can all bear i witness I never press alteration of it in parliament but on the contrary when I endeavour most a union real as be already in my person my desire be to conform the law of Scotland to the law of England and not the law of England to the law of Scotland and so the prophecy to be true of my wise grandfather Henry the seven who foretell that the lesser kingdom by marriage will follow the great and not the great the lesser and therefore marry his elder daughter Margaret to james the four my great grandfather it be a foolish querke of some judge who hold that the parliament of England can not unite Scotland and England by the name of great Britain but that it will make a alteration of the law though I be since come to that knowledge that a act of parliament can do great wonder and that old wise man the treasurer burghley be wont to say he know not what a act of parliament can not do in England for my intention be always to effect union by unite Scotland to England and not England to Scotland for I ever mean be ever resolve that this law shall continue in this kingdom and two thing move i thereunto one be that in matter of policy and state you shall never see any thing ancient and mature establish but by innovation or alteration it be worse than it be I mean not by purge of it from corruption and restore it to the ancient integrity another reason be I be swear to maintain the law of the land and therefore I have be perjure if I have alter it and this I speak to root out the conceit and misapprehension if it be in any heart that I will change damnify vilify or suppress the law of this land GOD be my judge I never mean it and this confirmation I make before you all to this I join the point of justice which I call all my council and judge dead and alive can and can bear i witness how unpartial I have be in declare of Law and where it have concern i in my own inheritance I have as willing submit my interest to the law as any my subject can do and it become i so to do to give example to other much less than will I be partial to other where I be not to myself and so resolve yourselves justice with i may be moderate in point of clemency for no justice can be without mercy but in matter of justice to give every man his own to be blind without eye of partiality this be my full resolution I use to say when I be in Scotland if any man move i to delay justice that it be against the office of a king so to do but when any make suit to hasten justice I tell they I have rather grant forty of these suit than one of the other this be always my custom and shall be ever with God leave now what I have speak of law and justice I mean by the law keep in she own bound for I understand the inheritance of the king and subject in this land must be determine by the common law etc and that be by the law set down in our forefather time expound by learned man diverse time after in the declaratory comment call or else by statute law set down by act of parliament as occasion serve by this I do not seclude all other law of England but this be the law of inheritance in this kingdom there be another law of all law free and supreme which be God law and by this all common and municipal law must be govern and except they have dependence upon this law they be unjust and unlawful when I speak of that law I only give this touch that that law in this kingdom have be too much neglect and churchman too much have in contempt I must speak truth great man lord judge and people of all degree from the high to the low have too much contemn they and God will not bless we in our own law if we do not reverence and obey God law which can be except the interpreter of it be respect and reverence and it be a sign of the latter day draw on even the contempt of the church and of the governor and teacher thereof now in the church of ENGLAND which I say in my conscience of any church that ever I read or know of present or past be most pure and near the primitive and apostolical church in doctrine and discipline and be surely found upon the word of God of any church in Christendom next unto this law be the law of nation which God forbid shall be bar and that for two cause one because it be a law to satisfy stranger which will not so well hold themselves satisfy with other municipal law another to satisfy our own subject in matter of piracy marriage will and thing of like nature that law I divide into civil and canon and this law have be so much encroach upon sithence my come to the crown and so have in contempt that young man be discourage from study and the rest weary of their life that do profess it and will be glad to seek any other craft so speak of the common law I mean the common law keep within she own limit and not derogate from these other law which by long custom have be root here first the law of GOD and his church and next the law civil and canon which in many case can be want to conclude this charge which I give myself I profess to maintain all the point of my oath especial in law and of law especial the common Law and as to maintain it so to purge it for else it can be maintain and especial to purge it from two corruption incertainty and novelty incertainty be find in the law itself wherein I will be painful to clear it to the people and this be proper to be do in parliament by advice of the judge the other corruption be introduce by the judge themselves by nicety that be use where it may be say nothing in the world be more likely to be permanent to our eye then iron or steel yet the rust corrupt it if it be not keep clean which show nothing be permanent here in this world if it be not purge so I can discharge my conscience in maintain the law if I keep they not clean from corruption and now that I may be like the pastor that first take the sacrament himself and then give it to the people so I have first take my own charge upon i before I give you your charge lest it may be say now my lord the judge for your part the charge I have to give you consist likewise in three part first in general that you do justice upright as you shall answer to GOD and i for as I have only GOD to answer to and to expect punishment at his hand if I offend so you be to answer both to GOD and to i and expect punishment at God hand and i if you be find in fault second to do justice indifferent between subject and subject between king and subject without delay partiality fear or bribery with stout and upright heart with clean and uncorrupt hand when I bid you do justice bold yet I bid you do it fearful fearful in this to utter your own conceit and not the true meaning of the law and remember you be no maker of law but interpreter of law according to the true sense thereof for your office be Ius and not Ius dare and that you be so far from make law that even in the high house of parliament you have no voice in make of a law but only to give your advice when you be require and though the law be in many place obscure and not so well know to the multitude as to you and that there be many part that come not into ordinary practice which be know to you because you can find out the reason thereof by book and precedent yet know this that your interpretation must be always subject to common sense and reason for I will never trust any interpretation that agree not with my common sense and reason and true logic for in all human law without exception it must not be sophistry or strain of wit that must interpret but either clear law or solid reason but in country where the formality of law have no place as in Denmark which I may true report as have myself be a eyewitness thereof all their state be govern only by a write law there be no advocate or proctor admit to plead only the party themselves plead their own cause and then a man stand up and read the law and there be a end for the very lawbook itself be their only judge happy be all kingdom if they can be so but here curious wit various conceit different action and variety of example breed question in law and therefore when you hear the question if they be plain there be a plain way in itself if they be such as be not plain for man invention daily abound then be you to interpret according to common sense and draw a good and certain Minor of natural reason out of the mayor of direct law and thereupon to make a right and true conclusion for though the common law be a mystery and skill best know unto yourselves yet if your interpretation be such as other man which have logic and common sense understand not the reason I will never trust such a interpretation remember also you be judge and not a judge and divide into bench which show that what you do that you shall do with advice and deliberation not hasty and rash before you well study the case and confer together debate it due not give single opinion and so to give your judgement as you will answer to God and i now have speak of your office in general I be next to come to the limit wherein you be to bound yourselves which likewise be three first encroach not upon the prerogative of the crown if there fall out a question that concern my prerogative or mystery of state deal not with it till you consult with the king or his council or both for they be transcendent matter and must not be slibere carry with overrash wilfulness for so may you wound the king through the side of a private person and this I commend unto your special care as some of you of late have do very well to blunt the sharp edge and vain popular humour of some lawyer at the bar that think they be not eloquent and bold spirit enough except they meddle with the king prerogative but do not you suffer this for certain if this liberty be suffer the king prerogative the crown and I shall be as much wound by their plead as if you resolve what they dispute that which concern the mystery of the king power be not lawful to be dispute for that be to wade into the weakness of prince and to take away the mystical reverence that belong unto they that sit in the throne of God second that you keep yourselves within your own bench not to invade other jurisdiction which be unfit and a unlawful thing in this I must enlarge myself beside the court of common law there be the court of request the admiralty court the court of the precedent and council of wall the precedent and council of the north high commission court every bishop in his own Court these court ought to keep their own limit and bound of their commission and instruction according to the ancient precedent and like as I declare that my pleasure be that every of these shall keep their own limit and bound so the court of common law be not to encroach upon they no more than it be my pleasure that they shall encroach upon the common Law and this be a thing regal and proper to a king to keep every court within his own bound in Westminster hall there be four court two that handle cause civil which be the common-plea and the exchequer two that determine cause criminal which be the kings-bench and the star-chamber where now I sit the common-plea be a part and branch of the kings-bench for it be first all one court and then the common-plea be extract it be call common-plea because it meddle with the plea of private person and that which remain the kings-bench the other of the court for civil cause be the exchequer which be ordain for the king revenue that be the principal institution of that court and aught to be their chief study and as other thing come orderly thither by occasion of the former they may be handle and justice there administer keep you therefore all in your own bound and for my part I desire you to give i no more right in my private prerogative than you give to any subject and therein I will be acquiescent as for the absolute prerogative of the crown that be no subject for the tongue of a lawyer nor be lawful to be dispute it be Athiesme and blasphemy to dispute what God can do good christian content themselves with his will reveal in his word so it be presumption and high contempt in a subject to dispute what a king can do or say that a king can do this or that but rest in that which be the king reveal will in his Law the kings-bench be the principal court for criminal cause and in some respect it deal with civil cause then be there a chancery court this be a court of equity and have power to deal likewise in civil cause it be call the dispenser of the king conscience follow always the intention of law and justice not alter the law not make that black which other court make white nor but in this it exceed other court mix mercy with justice where other court proceed only according to the strict rule of law and where the rigour of the law in many case will undo a subject there the chancery temper the law with equity and so mix mercy with justice as it preserve man from destruction and thus as before I tell you be the king throne establish by mercy and justice the chancery be undependant of any other court and be only under the king there it be write from that court there be no appeal and as I be bind in my conscience to maintain every court jurisdiction so especial this and not suffer it to sustain wrong yet so to maintain it as to keep it within the own limit and free from corruption my chancellor that now be I find he keeper of the seal the same place in substance although I give he the style of chancellor and God have keep he in it till now and I pray God he may hold it long and so I hope he will he will bear i witness I never give he other warrant then to go on in his court according to precedent warrant by law in the time of the best govern king and most learned chancellor these be the limit I give unto he beyond the same limit he have promise i he will never go and as he have promise i to take no other jurisdiction to himself so be it my promise ever to maintain this jurisdiction in that court therefore I speak this to vindicate that court from misconceipt and contempt it be the duty of judge to punish those that seek to deprave the proceed of any the king court and not to encourage they any way and I must confess I think it a odious and inept speech and it grieve i very much that it shall be say in Westminster hall that a praemunire lie against the court of the chancery and officer there how can the king grant a praemunire against himself it be a foolish inept and presumptuous attempt and fit for the time of some unworthy king understand i aright I mean not the chancery shall exceed his limit but on the other part the king only be to correct it and none else and therefore I be great abuse in that attempt for if any be wrong there the complaint shall have come to i none of you but will confess you have a king of reasonable understanding and willing to reform why then shall you spare to complain to i that be the high way and not go the other way and backe-way in contempt of our authority and therefore sit here in a seat of judgement I declare and command that no man hereafter presume to sue a praemunire against the chancery which I may the more easy do because no praemunire can be sue but at my suit and I may just bar myself at my own pleasure as all inundation come with overflow the bank and never come without great inconvenience and be think prodigious by astrologer in thing to come so be this overflow the bank of your jurisdiction in itself inconvenient and may prove prodigious to the State remember therefore that hereafter you keep within your limit and jurisdiction it be a special point of my office to procure and command that among court there be a concordance and musical accord and it be your part to obey and see this keep and as you be to observe the ancient law and custom of England so be you to keep yourselves within the bind of direct law or precedent and of those not every snatch precedent carp now here now there as it be run by the way but such as have never be controvert but by the contrary approve by common usage in time of best king and by most learned judge the star-chamber court have be likewise shake of late and the last year it have receive a sore blow if it have not be assist and carry by a few voice the very name of star-chamber seem to procure a reverence to the court. I will not play the critic to descant on the name it have a name from heaven a star place in it and a star be a glorious creature and seat in a glorious place next unto the angel the star-chamber be also glorious in substance for in the composition it be of four sort of person the first two be privy counselor and judge the one by wisdom in matter of state the other by learning in matter of law to direct and order all thing both according to law and state the other two sort be peer of the realm and bishop the peer be there by reason of their greatness to give authority to that court the bishop because of their learning in divinity and the interest they have in the good government of the church and so both the learning of both divine and human law and experience and practice in government be conjoin together in the proceed of this Court there be no kingdom but have a court of equity either by itself as be here in England or else mix and incorporate in their office that be judge in the law as it be in Scotland but the order of England be much more perfect where they be divide and as in case of equity where the law determine not clear there the chancery do determine have equity belong to it which do belong to no other court so the star-chamber have that belong to it which belong to no other court for in this court attempt be punishable where other court punish only fact and also where the law punish fact easy as in case of riot or combat there the star-chamber punish in a high degree and also all combination of practice and conspiracy and if the king be dishonour or contemn in his prerogative it belong most proper to the peer and judge of this court to punish it so than this court be institute for so great cause it be great reason it shall have great honour remember now how I have teach you brotherly love one towards another for you know well that as you be judge you be all brother and your court be sister I pray you therefore labour to keep that sweet harmony which be among those sister the muse what great misery can there be to the law than contempt of the law and what ready way to contempt then when question come what shall be determine in this court and what in that whereupon two evil do arise the one that man come not now to court of justice to hear matter of right plead and decree give according but only out of a curiosity to hear question of the jurisdiction of court dispute and to see the event what court be like to prevail above the other and the other be that the plea be turn from court to court in a endless circular motion as upon Ixion wheel and this be the reason why I find just fault with that multitude of prohibition for when a poor minister have with long labour and great expense of charge and time get a sentence for his tithe then come a prohibition and turn he round from court to court and so make his cause immortal and endless for by this uncertainty of jurisdiction among court cause be scourge from court to court and this make the fruit of suit like Tantalus fruit still near the suitor lip but can never come to taste it and this in deed be a great delay of justice and make cause endless therefore the only way to avoid this be for you to keep your own bound and nourish not the people in contempt of other court but teach they reverence to court in your public speech both in your bench and in your circuit so shall you bring they to a reverence both of GOD and of the King keep therefore your own limit towards the king towards other court and towards other law bound yourselves within your own law and make not new Law remember as I say before that you be judge to declare and not to make law for when you make a decree never hear of before you be lawgivers and not lawteller I have labour to gather some
